\title{Who Pays for Green Public Investment?\\
{\large Tax Incidence, Debt Revaluation, and the Price Level
in Incomplete-Markets Economies}\thanks{%
I thank colleagues and seminar participants for helpful comments; all
remaining errors are mine. All quantitative statements are generated by the
accompanying replication package (quoted numbers are machine-written from
the saved results); the benchmark numbers come from the full-resolution
computation ($n_a=500$ asset nodes). The climate calibration is
externally disciplined where estimates exist and flagged as illustrative
where they do not. The numerical method is described in
Appendix~\ref{app:numerics}.}}
\author{Maxime Polis\\[2pt]
{\normalsize Department of Economics, Lund University}}
\date{July 2026 --- preliminary draft, comments welcome}

\begin{document}
\maketitle

\begin{abstract}
\noindent
Who bears the burden of green public investment when the price level is what
clears the market for nominal safe assets? We study a heterogeneous-agent
economy in which green public investment --- by changing future damages,
household risk, and the taxes that service it --- moves the precautionary
demand for nominal government debt, and with it the price level, the real
value of that debt, and the distribution of nominal wealth. Two results
require no computation. Nominal debt issuance is neutral, so real debt is
demand-determined: the stationary experiment is an appropriation financed by
a tax mix, never a deficit. And steady-state inflation is neutral at a fixed
real rate, so ``optimal accommodation'' is an optimal-real-rate result. What
the financing choice governs is \emph{incidence}. A proportional levy with a
rebate raises the price level (to $\RRebP$ from $\PzeroRegimes$) and
makes the program progressive; a lump-sum tax lowers it (to $\RDefP$),
handing bondholders a windfall --- the sign of the revaluation is an
instrument choice, not a property of the program. Whether the program returns
its resource cost is instead a climate-damage question: the benefit--cost
ratio of public abatement crosses one only under high-damage estimates
($\bcrLow$, $\bcrMed$, $\bcrHigh$ across damage columns) and an abatement
technology we report as a frontier, not a point estimate. Price-level
magnitudes are one-asset upper bounds, scaling with the liquid
nominal-government-debt share of household portfolios; along the announced
transition, three quarters of the long-run price response arrives in the
announcement year as a revaluation of the predetermined nominal stock.
\end{abstract}

\bigskip
\noindent\textbf{Keywords:} price-level determination, incomplete markets,
HANK, fiscal policy, climate change, green investment, self-fulfilling
equilibria.\\
\textbf{JEL:} E31, E52, E62, E63, H63, Q54.

\newpage

% ==========================================================================
\section{Introduction}\label{sec:intro}
% ==========================================================================

Financing the climate transition will require public investment flows of
several percent of GDP per year for decades, and most of it will be
appropriated the way budgets are always appropriated: in nominal terms,
against a nominal public debt stock. This paper asks who bears
the burden of that program once the price level has responded to it. The
question has an incidence half --- taxpayers, or the holders of the nominal
safe asset whose real value shifts? --- and a monetary half --- what does the
program do to the price level, and can climate damages interacting with
nominal budgets undermine determinacy itself? Both halves turn on one
economic object: \emph{green public investment changes the demand for the
nominal liabilities that finance it}, through the damages it avoids, the
idiosyncratic risk it removes, and the taxes it requires. The price level,
the real fiscal burden, and the distribution of that burden are therefore
jointly determined --- and none of them can be read off the deficit
accounting that frames the policy debate. A first result makes the point
exactly: in the stationary economy there is \emph{no deficit to account for}.
Nominal debt issuance is neutral and real debt is demand-determined, so the
permanent experiment is a nominal appropriation and the tax mix that services
it; ``deficit'' is reserved for the transitional tax-timing paths of
Section~\ref{sec:transitions}.

Our framework assembles four building blocks. From \citet{hagedorn2026dtpl}
we take price-level determination: in an incomplete-markets economy,
households demand safe nominal assets for precautionary reasons, and given
the real rate pinned jointly by monetary policy (the nominal rate $i^{ss}$)
and fiscal policy (nominal growth $\mu$, which equals steady-state
inflation), the price level clears the asset market,
$P^{*}=B/\Sfun(1+r^{ss})$. From \citet{angeletos2024deficits} we take the
self-financing question, but in a stationary form their dynamic exercise does
not cover: across steady states --- where there is no deficit --- how much of
a permanent appropriation's resource cost is returned, and to whom does the
revaluation of nominal debt shift the burden? From \citet{acharya2023optimal}
we take the optimal-policy tradeoff between aggregate objectives and the
idiosyncratic risk that policy instruments move. And from
\citet{acharyabenhabib2024} we take the possibility of belief-driven
multiplicity when idiosyncratic risk is endogenous to aggregate outcomes ---
here, to the climate itself.

The economy is a Bewley--Huggett--Aiyagari endowment economy
\citep{bewley1980,huggett1993,aiyagari1994} with a climate sector built in the
integrated-assessment tradition \citep{golosov2014,nordhaus2017}: emissions
feed a carbon stock; the carbon stock generates damages; damages scale
endowments and --- following the microeconomic evidence in
\citet{kanzig2023carbon} and \citet{fried2018} --- fall
\emph{disproportionately on the poor} through an incidence gradient, and raise
idiosyncratic income risk \citep{bilalkanzig2024}. Public abatement capital
reduces emissions and is maintained by real green investment
$g_g = G_g/P$: the \emph{real} value of a \emph{nominal} budget line. That
single link --- nominal appropriation, real abatement --- generates all of our
results.

\paragraph{Findings.} Our first result is that the theory survives the
addition of a full climate sector. The abatement capital, the carbon stock,
and the damage schedule add real equations, but they are pinned by real green
spending; the asset market retains its single job of pricing nominal debt, and
determines the price level under the same conditions as in
\citet{hagedorn2026dtpl} (Proposition~\ref{prop:determinacy}). That
determinacy is not free, and the rest of the paper is about the economics that
the climate loop injects into the asset-demand schedule --- economics that
is absent from an incomplete-markets economy without a physical state.

The centerpiece is the incidence of a permanent green appropriation, measured
by an exact steady-state decomposition
(Proposition~\ref{prop:selffinancing}): the net household burden splits into
a \emph{revaluation} component, in which the endogenous price-level response
revalues nominal debt service, and a \emph{damage dividend}, in which avoided
damages raise endowments. The two components answer different questions and
must be kept apart. The damage dividend answers the \emph{resource} question.
At a defensible fiscal scale (debt of $1.1$ times income, a program of $2\%$
of income), the benefit--cost ratio of public abatement crosses one only
under high-damage estimates ($\bcrLow$, $\bcrMed$, $\bcrHigh$ across the
DICE, empirical, and high-damage columns of
Section~\ref{subsec:calibrated}) --- and even these are conditional on an
abatement technology whose effectiveness $\theta_g$ we report as a frontier,
not a point (Section~\ref{subsec:robustness}). The revaluation component
answers the \emph{fiscal} question, and its sign is a property of the tax
instrument. A proportional levy plus rebate raises the price level (to
$\RRebP$ from a no-program $\PzeroRegimes$): the levy weakens precautionary
demand, erodes nominal debt, and falls on those who can bear it, making the
program progressive (Proposition~\ref{prop:financingdesign}). A uniform
lump-sum tax financing the same appropriation lowers the price
level (to $\RDefP$): the tax tightens constraint-adjacent households into
precautionary saving, and the ``inflation tax'' runs backwards, handing
bondholders a windfall (Result~\ref{res:disinflation}). What is robust across
instruments is not the sign of the price change but \emph{who bears the
burden} once the price level has moved.

A second, more theoretical finding concerns determinacy. Because the budget
is nominal, a higher price level lowers real abatement, raises future
damages, and --- through the incidence gradient --- concentrates those damages
on the households most prone to build precautionary buffers. When this
feedback is steep enough, the asset market can in principle admit two
stationary price levels, a green boom and a brown stagnation, each
self-fulfilling (Proposition~\ref{prop:sunspots}) --- the steady-state analog
of belief-driven fluctuations in HANK economies \citep{acharyabenhabib2024},
with the slow-moving carbon stock as the belief-relevant state. Indexing the
green budget while keeping debt nominal insulates against it
(Proposition~\ref{prop:insulation}), reversing the spending-line rule ranking
of \citet{hagedorn2026dtpl}. The result is strictly scoped: the calibrated
sunspot region is \emph{empty} (Table~\ref{tab:frontier}), the proposition
conditions on a crossing it does not derive from primitives, and the
insulation result rests on a numerically verified bound --- so anchor
insulation is design insurance, not a measured feature of the data. The
failure that \emph{does} bind quantitatively, under strong regressive
incidence, is nonexistence: a fiscal-space collapse that indexation cannot
repair and monetary accommodation can
(Section~\ref{subsec:sunspots}).

One policy margin dominates, and one dissolves. The one that dominates is
the financing instrument: because the revaluation channel is an incidence
object, \emph{how} the same real program is financed moves the price level
through asset demand. Lump-sum taxes strengthen precautionary demand
(disinflation, bondholder windfall) while a proportional carbon-style levy
weakens it (inflation, debt erosion), and at the calibrated medium column the
levy-plus-rebate design is more self-financing ($\nu$: $\RDefNu \to \RRebNu$)
and, in steady state, progressive rather than regressive (bottom-half
welfare: $\RDefBfifty\% \to \RRebBfifty\%$, top decile nearly unharmed) ---
from the same nominal appropriation
(Proposition~\ref{prop:financingdesign}, Table~\ref{tab:regimes}); along the
announced path the top decile instead bears the announcement levy, a genuine
distributional tradeoff we return to.
The margin that dissolves is trend inflation. Varying the nominal growth
rate $\mu$ at a fixed nominal rate $i^{ss}$ moves the real rate one-for-one
($1+r^{ss}=(1+i^{ss})/(1+\mu)$), and the steady state depends on $\mu$
only through $r^{ss}$ (Proposition~\ref{prop:muneutral}); the
welfare-maximizing $\mu^{*}=0.045$ is therefore the optimal \emph{real rate}
$r^{ss*}\approx-0.5\%$, the \citet{aiyagarimcgrattan1998}
optimum-quantity-of-debt force, not an inflation prescription.

These are steady-state comparisons, and the natural objection is that the
interesting economics happens on the way between them. We compute the way.
The nonlinear heterogeneous-agent transition --- the price-level \emph{path}
solved as the object that clears the asset market at every date, with no
Phillips curve anywhere (Section~\ref{subsec:tier2}) --- shows that the
mechanism operates as a capitalization jump. On a credible announcement of
the permanent program, $77\%$ of the long-run green disinflation is realized
within the announcement year; holders of the outstanding nominal stock
collect a windfall of about five percent of the program's present value
before any green capital exists; and inflation is back near trend by year two
while the capital stock accumulates for decades. The price level prices the
\emph{news}, not the spending. Household by household, the announced path is
more regressive than the steady-state comparison suggests --- the
windfall accrues at the top while front-loaded taxes bite at the constrained
bottom --- whereas the rebate design, solved along the same path, keeps the
bottom half in surplus and turns the announcement repricing into a one-time
capital levy on bondholders. The transition strengthens the financing-design
conclusion. We read the disinflationary front-loading as an internal DTPL
prediction: a Phillips-curve economy prices the same class of announcement
with the opposite sign ($+0.4\%$ impact inflation), but the two exercises
differ in rigidity, financing, persistence, scale, and the household block,
so the contrast is a model-class diagnostic locating a testable prediction
--- adjudicating it requires the matched experiment included in the
replication package (Section~\ref{subsec:tier2}) or high-frequency
identification around green-fiscal surprises.

A final extension confronts the objection that the whole analysis assumes away
aggregate uncertainty. Introducing a two-state aggregate climate process (a
recurrent calm/severe regime) makes the nominal bond nominally safe but really
risky: its real value $B/P_s$ is state-contingent, the price level is $37\%$
higher in the severe state, and the bond therefore takes a $26\%$ real loss
exactly when the climate turns bad (Section~\ref{subsec:aggrisk}). A permanent
green program compresses this systematic risk --- the state-price dispersion
falls by more than a quarter and the disaster loss shrinks --- because
abatement lowers damages most in the high-damage state. Green public investment
is thus not merely a level intervention on average damages but a reduction in
the aggregate climate risk that the safe nominal asset must bear: the paper's
``safe asset'' becomes literal, and the aggregate-uncertainty objection is met
with a computed result rather than a promise.

\paragraph{Contributions, in order of robustness.} The claims above are not
all on the same footing, and we rank them. The core contribution, robust
across the calibration, is the \textbf{financing-incidence mechanism}: under
a demand-theory price level, how a green appropriation is financed ---
lump-sum versus proportional levy --- sets the sign of the revaluation
channel, and with it the price-level response and its distribution, from an
unchanged nominal appropriation (Propositions \ref{prop:selffinancing},
\ref{prop:financingdesign}; Tables \ref{tab:calibrated}, \ref{tab:regimes}).
The mechanism is organized by one analytical object, a sufficient-statistic
formula for the price response to any productive public program
(Proposition~\ref{prop:suffstat}): the financing instrument sets the sign
through the numerator, the determinacy margin lives in the denominator, and
the formula's inputs are measured, not assumed
(Table~\ref{tab:elasticitymap}) --- an incidence theorem for nominal public
liabilities of which the climate application is the disciplined special
case.
Everything else is secondary or conditional: the self-financing
\emph{magnitudes} are frontiers conditional on climate damages and an
illustrative abatement technology (Section~\ref{subsec:robustness}); the
\emph{sunspot} and \emph{anchor-insulation} results are design insurance the
calibrated frontier never activates (Table~\ref{tab:frontier}); ``optimal
accommodation'' is an optimal-real-rate statement, not an inflation
prescription (Proposition~\ref{prop:muneutral}); and the NK contrast is a
model-class diagnostic, not a matched horse race. Each conditional result is
flagged as such where it appears.

Read against its four foundations, the paper's
contribution is to show that each of their central mechanisms changes ---
sometimes reverses --- once a nominal spending line meets an endogenous
physical state. Relative to \citet{hagedorn2026dtpl} we add a climate sector
and, with it, three findings that have no counterpart in an economy without a
physical state: a green appropriation is disinflationary under lump-sum
finance and inflationary under a proportional levy --- the price-level sign is
an incidence choice, not a property of the program; nominal-rule uniqueness can
fail and be repaired by \emph{denominating the spending line} in real terms
(anchor insulation); and the optimal nominal growth rate is a genuine
green-finance instrument. Relative to \citet{angeletos2024deficits} we evaluate a
\emph{permanent} program across stationary equilibria with an endogenous
price level, and find that the nominal (revaluation) channel they identify as
a source of self-financing flips sign for green investment. Relative to
\citet{acharya2023optimal} the policy instrument is nominal growth rather than
the interest rate and the risk externality it manages runs through the climate.
And relative to \citet{acharyabenhabib2024} the multiplicity we uncover is
across \emph{steady-state price levels} rather than in the dynamics around one,
and it is resolved by budget indexation rather than by an interest-rate rule.
The unifying object throughout is climate-state-dependent asset demand: green
public capital, damages, and damage-driven risk \emph{shift the asset-demand
schedule itself}, and every result is a consequence of where that shifted
schedule intersects the supply of nominal debt.

The rest of the paper proceeds as follows. Section~\ref{sec:literature}
positions the paper in the literature. Section~\ref{sec:model} lays out the
model and Section~\ref{sec:theory} the theoretical results.
Section~\ref{sec:quant} contains the quantitative analysis and
Section~\ref{sec:transitions} the transition dynamics;
Section~\ref{sec:conclusion} concludes. Proofs are in
Appendix~\ref{app:proofs}, the New Keynesian model used in the transition
diagnostics in Appendix~\ref{app:nkmodel}, the numerical method in
Appendix~\ref{app:numerics}, and supplementary results in
Appendix~\ref{app:supp}.

% ==========================================================================
\section{Related literature}\label{sec:literature}
% ==========================================================================

This paper sits where the theory of price-level determination meets climate
macroeconomics. We organize the discussion from the closest methodological
antecedents to the closest substantive competitors.

The starting point is how the price level is determined at all. The fiscal
theory reads $P$ off the government valuation equation under active fiscal
policy \citep{leeper1991,sims1994,woodford1995,cochrane2023}. The Demand
Theory of the Price Level \citep{hagedorn2026dtpl} takes a different route:
under \emph{passive} fiscal policy, $P$ clears the asset market, exploiting
the nondegenerate asset demand that incomplete markets create --- the
complete-markets economy being the indeterminate benchmark
\citep{sargentwallace1975}. The closest heterogeneous-agent nominal-debt
laboratory is \citet{kaplannikolakoudisviolante2023}, whose long-run anchor is
our steady-state object; what differentiates us is the climate state and the
spending-denomination margin. \citet{hoefer2025}, who proves multiplicity of
price levels in heterogeneous-agent models under constant primary deficits,
disciplines our sunspot proposition. Within this program our contribution is
twofold: we show that the DTPL's uniqueness properties interact nontrivially
with a physical state that feeds back into asset demand, and --- to our
knowledge for the first time --- that the decisive determinacy margin can be
the \emph{denomination of a spending line} (nominal versus indexed) rather
than a policy-rule coefficient.

Our multiplicity result has a close cousin in the heterogeneous-agent New
Keynesian literature. Quantitative HANK models \citep{kaplan2018,auclert2021}
established that market incompleteness reshapes the transmission of monetary and
fiscal policy, and \citet{auclertrognliestraub2025} synthesize the
fiscal-monetary toolkit we lean on for the transition exercises. Within that
program, \citet{acharya2023optimal} derive optimal monetary policy when the
instrument moves idiosyncratic risk, \citet{acharyadogra2020} show how
endogenous risk alters determinacy, \citet{ravnsterk2021} build feedback loops
through unemployment risk, \citet{acharyabenhabib2024} obtain self-fulfilling
fluctuations when risk is endogenous to beliefs, and \citet{bilbiie2024}
provides the analytical map of these mechanisms. Our indeterminacy belongs to
the same family --- beliefs move endogenous risk and precautionary demand ---
but it runs through a slow-moving physical stock, carbon, and lands on the
price level, so it is a multiplicity \emph{across steady states} that budget
indexation resolves in place of the Taylor principle.

The self-financing question we inherit from
\citet{angeletos2024deficits}, who show that deficits partly pay for
themselves through boom-induced tax revenue and the erosion of nominal debt
when households are non-Ricardian. The objects differ in a way that matters:
theirs is the dynamic self-financing of a one-off stimulus under nominal
rigidity; ours is the steady-state return on a \emph{permanent} program under
flexible prices, where the price level must clear the asset market. The
informative comparison is therefore not the level of $\nu$ but the sign and
source of its components. Their erosion channel is the analog of our
revaluation term, and our central finding is that its sign \emph{flips} for
green investment under lump-sum finance --- the program raises asset demand
and is disinflationary; their tax-base boom is the analog of our damage
dividend, a supply-side return that exists because the spending is
productive, not because it stimulates. On how much revaluation a government
can credibly deliver, \citet{hurtado2023sovereign} --- who model inflation as
a state-contingent partial default on long-term nominal debt without
commitment --- provide the template for our debt-maturity extension; because
our revaluation runs \emph{against} the fisc, commitment problems here cut
toward bondholders rather than away from them.

The climate sector rests on the integrated-assessment tradition ---
\citet{golosov2014} for the emissions--damages structure in general
equilibrium, \citet{nordhaus2017} for damage-calibration discipline, and
\citet{barrage2020} for the interaction of climate policy with distortionary
fiscal policy. On the nominal side, \citet{delnegro2023green} ask whether the
transition is inflationary in a multi-sector New Keynesian model. Their
sticky-price, transition-dynamics question complements our flexible-price,
steady-state one --- short-run reallocation inflation and long-run
asset-demand disinflation can coexist. What could separate the mechanisms
observationally is the announcement window: a Phillips-curve economy prices a
green announcement as mildly inflationary demand stimulus, the asset-demand
mechanism as an immediate disinflationary capitalization
(Section~\ref{sec:transitions}). Because our two exercises differ in
rigidity, financing, persistence, and the household block as well as in price
determination, we treat the opposite sign as a model-class diagnostic that
locates a testable prediction rather than a clean identification; the sign of
expected inflation around credible green-fiscal news remains the natural
discriminating observable. The estimated New Keynesian climate model of
\citet{sahucsmetsvermandel2024} and the climate-conscious monetary policy of
\citet{nakovthomas2026} are the points of contact for our
representative-agent transition diagnostics, while
\citet{ferrarinispilandi2023}, who study green central-bank asset purchases,
differ from us in that the active margin here is the denomination of the
fiscal green budget rather than the central bank's balance sheet. Three
sources discipline the distributional inputs: \citet{kanzig2023carbon} on
carbon-policy burdens falling disproportionately on poor households
(our incidence gradient $\psi$), \citet{fried2018} on the distributional
effects of carbon taxes, and \citet{bilalkanzig2024} on macro damages far
exceeding country-level estimates (our high-damage column).

Several applied literatures discipline individual channels. On the
revaluation side, \citet{doepkeschneider2006} map inflation episodes into
redistribution of nominal wealth, \citet{auclert2019} decomposes policy
redistribution into earnings, Fisher, and interest-rate-exposure channels,
\citet{hallsargent2011} provide the maturity-adjusted debt accounting, and
\citet{hilscher2022} show that maturity and holder composition leave little
room to inflate away U.S.\ debt --- bounds we apply in
Appendix~\ref{subsec:maturity}, with the twist that our revaluation runs
toward bondholders, so these limits \emph{help} the fisc. On the spending side, the
productive-public-capital literature --- \citet{baxterking1993} on the
consumption/investment distinction, \citet{leeperwalkeryang2010} on
implementation delays, and \citet{bomligthart2014} on the output elasticity of
public capital --- disciplines our green-capital block and its efficiency
parameter $q_g$. And on the financing side, work on green finance and sovereign
risk --- \citet{gigliokellystroebel2021} on climate-risk pricing,
\citet{bakergreenbonds} on green-bond pricing and ownership, and
\citet{bis2025sovereign} on transition risk in sovereign yields --- bounds the
greenium and sovereign-risk margins; we do not assume that green investment
improves sovereign risk.

Closest in substance are the heterogeneous-household climate-policy papers and
the climate-fiscal-institutions literature. \citet{douennehummelpedroni} study
optimal carbon and income taxation with heterogeneous households, and
\citet{ddhp} study climate policy under incomplete markets and fiscal
constraints; both are near neighbors in environment, and what sets us apart is
the nominal side, since neither models nominal public liabilities or
price-level determination, which is where every result of this paper lives. On
the policy question itself, \citet{imf2024climatedebt} argue that spending-heavy
transition strategies worsen public-debt dynamics absent carbon pricing --- the
claim this paper subjects to an endogenous price level, and one our benchmark
partly overturns (a majority of the program cost is self-financed at medium
damages) and partly confirms (low program effectiveness makes the true cost
\emph{exceed} its sticker price). \citet{caselli2024greenfiscalrules} survey
green fiscal rules, a design space in which our anchor-insulation corollary is a
concrete proposal, and \citet{ando2022sovereignclimate} document the sovereign
green-bond market, the institutional home of the denomination margin we model.

% ==========================================================================
\section{Model}\label{sec:model}
% ==========================================================================

Time is discrete and infinite. The baseline has no aggregate uncertainty
and we study stationary equilibria; Section~\ref{subsec:aggrisk} adds a
two-state aggregate climate process as an extension. The economy has a
unit continuum of households, a government (fiscal authority plus central
bank), and a climate sector. This is an \emph{endowment} economy: the price
level is determined by asset-market clearing, not by a Phillips curve. The
transition exercises of Section~\ref{sec:transitions} embed these same
household, government, and climate blocks in a New Keynesian production
economy --- adding firms, an intratemporal labor choice, sticky prices, and
an interest-rate rule --- which is stated in full in
Appendix~\ref{app:nkmodel}; we flag the connection at the relevant points
below.

\paragraph{The scenarios, up front.} To orient the reader, the paper studies
one economy under a fixed menu of \emph{financing designs} and a short list
of \emph{environments}. The financing designs are all balanced-budget
choices of the unified tax pair $(\tau_{ls},\vartheta)$ of
Section~\ref{subsec:households}: the \emph{lump-sum} benchmark ($\vartheta=0$),
a \emph{proportional levy} ($\vartheta>0$, $\tau_{ls}$ covering only service), a
\emph{levy-plus-rebate} (levy above the program, half rebated), and an even
\emph{mix} (Section~\ref{subsec:regimes}). The environments are: the
deterministic stationary economy (Sections~\ref{sec:theory}--\ref{sec:quant});
its \emph{indexed-mandate} variant, in which the green line is denominated in
real terms (Section~\ref{subsec:sunspots}); a \emph{two-state aggregate-climate}
extension (Section~\ref{subsec:aggrisk}); and the \emph{announcement transition}
of Section~\ref{sec:transitions}, computed both in the nonlinear DTPL economy
and, as diagnostics, in representative-agent and heterogeneous-agent New
Keynesian versions. Every quantitative headline is a stationary object; the
transition and NK exercises are dynamics and diagnostics, labeled as such.

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Households}\label{subsec:households}

Households value consumption streams
\begin{equation}
\E_0 \sum_{t=0}^{\infty}\beta^t u(c_t), \qquad
u(c)=\frac{c^{1-\sigma}-1}{1-\sigma},\quad \sigma>0,
\label{eq:preferences}
\end{equation}
and face idiosyncratic endowment risk: $e_t$ follows a finite-state Markov
chain with transition matrix $\Pi$ (a Rouwenhorst discretization of an AR(1)
in logs with persistence $\rho$ and innovation standard deviation
$\sigma_\epsilon$), normalized so that $\E[e]=1$. We specify the fiscal side as
a \emph{unified tax schedule} from the outset, so that the financing-incidence
comparisons of Section~\ref{subsec:regimes} are structural rather than bolted
on: taxes combine a real lump-sum component $\tau_{ls}$ (a rebate when negative)
and a proportional levy at rate $\vartheta\in[0,1)$ on effective endowments. The
budget constraint is
\begin{equation}
c_t + a_{t+1} = (1+r_t)\,a_t + (1-\vartheta_t)\,y(e_t; D_t) - \tau_{ls,t},
\qquad a_{t+1}\ge -\bar a,
\label{eq:budget}
\end{equation}
where $a$ is the real value of nominal government bonds held and $y(e;D)$ the
\emph{effective} endowment after climate damages, defined in
\eqref{eq:incidence} below. The borrowing limit is $\bar a = 0$ in the
benchmark. The instruments are linked to spending by the aggregate government
identity $\tau_{ls}+\vartheta\,(1-D)=r^{ss}b+g_g$; a financing regime is a
choice of the pair $(\tau_{ls},\vartheta)$ satisfying it (Section~\ref{subsec:regimes}).

The household problem gives a value function $V(a,e)$, a savings policy
$a'(a,e)$, and an invariant distribution $\Omega$ over $(a,e)$. Aggregate
asset demand is the single object
\begin{equation}
\Sfun\big(1+r;\ \tau_{ls},\ \vartheta,\ D\big) \;=\; \int a \,\dd\Omega .
\label{eq:Sdef}
\end{equation}
The \emph{lump-sum benchmark} sets $\vartheta=0$, in which case we abbreviate
$\tau\equiv\tau_{ls}$ and write $\Sfun(1+r;\tau,D)$ for
$\Sfun(1+r;\tau,0,D)$ throughout; the proportional-levy and rebate designs
(Section~\ref{subsec:regimes}) activate $\vartheta>0$ in the same object,
so the incidence results are read off one asset-demand schedule, not a separate
construction. A pure proportional levy scales effective endowments by
$(1-\vartheta)$ and, by the homotheticity of Lemma~\ref{lem:S}, contracts asset
demand roughly one-for-one; the lump-sum component enters through the
constraint-tightening channel of Section~\ref{subsec:safeasset}.

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{The climate sector}\label{subsec:climate}

The climate sector has four objects: abatement capital, emissions, the carbon
stock, and damages with distributional incidence.

\paragraph{Abatement capital.} Public green capital $K_g$ depreciates at rate
$\delta_g$ and is maintained by real green investment $g_g$, of which only a
fraction $q_g \in (0,1]$ becomes effective capital --- implementation
efficiency capturing planning delays, procurement frictions and project
selection \citep[in the spirit of][and the IMF's Climate-PIMA
framework]{leeperwalkeryang2010}, so $K_g = q_g\, g_g/\delta_g$ (benchmark
$q_g = 1$, swept in Section~\ref{subsec:maturity}). With $q_g$ suppressed
for notation:
\begin{equation}
K_g = \frac{g_g}{\delta_g}, \qquad g_g = \frac{G_g}{P}
\ \ \text{(nominal budget regime)} \quad\text{or}\quad g_g\ \text{fixed
(real mandate)}.
\label{eq:Kg}
\end{equation}
Abatement capital removes a share
\begin{equation}
A(K_g) = 1 - e^{-\theta_g K_g}\in[0,1)
\label{eq:abatement}
\end{equation}
of gross emissions, with effectiveness parameter $\theta_g\ge0$.

\paragraph{Emissions and the carbon stock.} Gross emissions are proportional
to aggregate activity (mean effective endowment $1-D$), reduced by abatement
with maximal abatement share $\alpha_A\in[0,1]$:
\begin{equation}
E = \varepsilon_0\,\big(1-\alpha_A A(K_g)\big)\,(1-D).
\label{eq:emissions}
\end{equation}
The carbon stock decays at rate $\delta_x$ and accumulates emissions,
$X_{t+1} = (1-\delta_x)X_t + E_t$, so in steady state
\begin{equation}
X = \frac{E}{\delta_x}.
\label{eq:carbon}
\end{equation}

\paragraph{Damages and incidence.} Aggregate damages are a bounded, increasing
function of the carbon stock,
\begin{equation}
D(X) = D_{\max}\big(1-e^{-\gamma_x X}\big)\in[0,D_{\max}),
\label{eq:damages}
\end{equation}
and are distributed \emph{regressively} across households through an incidence
gradient: household $i$'s effective endowment is
\begin{equation}
y(e;D) \;=\; \big(1 - D\,\chi(e)\big)\,e, \qquad
\chi(e) = \frac{e^{-\psi}}{\E\!\left[e^{1-\psi}\right]},\quad \psi\ge0,
\label{eq:incidence}
\end{equation}
so that $\E[\chi(e)\,e]=1$: the population-average damage share equals $D$,
while $\psi>0$ tilts the burden toward low-endowment households
\citep[the incidence evidence in][]{kanzig2023carbon,fried2018}. The
benchmark of Section~\ref{sec:quant} also allows damages to raise the
\emph{dispersion} of endowment risk directly,
$\sigma_\epsilon(D)=\sigma_{\epsilon,0}(1+\phi_D D)$, the reduced form of the
endogenous-risk channel in \citet{acharya2023optimal} and
\citet{acharyabenhabib2024}. Moreover, $\psi>0$ by itself already generates
endogenous risk: damages compress the bottom of the income distribution, so a
dirtier steady state is also a riskier one.

Given real green spending $g_g$, equations
\eqref{eq:Kg}--\eqref{eq:damages} define a steady-state damage level
$D(g_g)$ as the fixed point of
\begin{equation}
D = D_{\max}\Big(1 - \exp\Big[-\tfrac{\gamma_x \varepsilon_0}{\delta_x}
\big(1-\alpha_A A(K_g)\big)(1-D)\Big]\Big),
\label{eq:climatefixedpoint}
\end{equation}
which exists, is unique, and is decreasing in $g_g$
(Lemma~\ref{lem:climate}). The single-equation reduced form used for
exposition, $D = D_0 e^{-\theta_g K_g}$, is the version implemented as
``climate version 1'' in the package; \eqref{eq:climatefixedpoint} is
``climate version 2.''

\paragraph{Mitigation or adaptation?} The two physical readings of green
capital map onto the two climate specifications differently, and the mapping
should be exact. The carbon-stock specification (version~2,
\eqref{eq:emissions}--\eqref{eq:damages}) is \emph{mitigation}: $K_g$ lowers
gross emissions, which lowers the carbon stock $X$, which lowers damages. In
a closed economy this understates the true global externality --- one
country's abatement does not, by itself, move the global $X$ --- so read as
mitigation the carbon-stock block is literally one country's share of a
\emph{coordinated} program, or the world-planner's instrument. The
reduced-form specification (version~1, $D=D_0e^{-\theta_g K_g}$) is instead
the natural home for \emph{adaptation}: $K_g$ --- sea walls, hardened
infrastructure, resilient public health and water systems --- lowers a
country's \emph{own} damages taking the global carbon stock as exogenous
\citep{fried2022seawalls,konradtweder2023}, so it does not run through the
emissions--stock equations and the closed-economy mapping from own spending
to own damages is exact. Under that reading $\theta_g$ is a hardening
elasticity, not an abatement effectiveness. The two are structurally distinct
--- adaptation acts on $D$ at fixed $X$, mitigation acts on $X$ --- and only
the reduced form supports the closed-economy interpretation cleanly. What the
mechanism needs is common to both: public capital that lowers future damages.
Crucially, $\theta_g$ has no clean empirical counterpart under either reading
(Section~\ref{subsec:robustness} reports it as a frontier, not a point), so
the quantitative benefit--cost ratios below identify the \emph{internal
region} of $(\theta_g,\delta_g,D_0)$ space in which the program pays for
itself; they are not magnitudes trained on a calibrated mitigation or
adaptation technology.

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Government}\label{subsec:government}

The consolidated government issues nominal debt $B_t$, appropriates the
nominal green budget $G_{g,t}$ (in the nominal regime), levies real lump-sum
taxes $\tau_t$, and sets the nominal interest rate $i$. Fiscal policy is a
\emph{nominal growth rule}:
\begin{equation}
B_{t+1} = (1+\mu)B_t, \qquad G_{g,t+1} = (1+\mu)G_{g,t}.
\label{eq:nominalrule}
\end{equation}
In a stationary equilibrium real debt is constant, so inflation equals nominal
growth, $\pi^{ss}=\mu$, and the Fisher relation pins the real rate:
\begin{equation}
1+r^{ss} = \frac{1+i^{ss}}{1+\mu}.
\label{eq:fisher}
\end{equation}
The flow budget constraint in real terms requires
\begin{equation}
\tau = r^{ss}\, b + g_g, \qquad b = \frac{B}{P}.
\label{eq:gbc}
\end{equation}
Fiscal policy is passive in the sense that $\tau$ adjusts to satisfy
\eqref{eq:gbc} at all price levels: there is no FTPL-style valuation
selection \citep{reis2022,bianchimelosi2017}. The price level here is
pinned by asset demand, not by the present value of surpluses.

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Equilibrium}\label{subsec:equilibrium}

\begin{definition}[Stationary green equilibrium]\label{def:equilibrium}
Given a policy $(i^{ss},\mu,B,G_g)$ --- or $(i^{ss},\mu,B,g_g)$ under the real
mandate --- a stationary green equilibrium is a tuple
$(P^{*},D^{*},K_g^{*},X^{*},\tau^{*},\Omega^{*})$ such that:
(i) households optimize and $\Omega^{*}$ is the invariant distribution;
(ii) the climate block \eqref{eq:Kg}--\eqref{eq:climatefixedpoint} holds at
$g_g^{*}=G_g/P^{*}$ (nominal regime) or $g_g^{*}=g_g$ (mandate);
(iii) the government budget \eqref{eq:gbc} holds;
(iv) the asset market clears:
\begin{equation}
\Phi(P^{*}) \;\equiv\;
\Sfun\big(1+r^{ss};\ \tau(P^{*}),\ D(P^{*})\big) - \frac{B}{P^{*}} \;=\; 0.
\label{eq:assetmarket}
\end{equation}
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Self-financing share]\label{def:nu}
Let subscript $0$ denote the no-program steady state ($G_g=0$) and $1$ the
program steady state. The self-financing share is
\begin{equation}
\nu \;\equiv\; \frac{r^{ss}B\left(\dfrac{1}{P_0}-\dfrac{1}{P_1}\right)
+ \big(D_0 - D_1\big)}{g_{g,1}}
\;=\; \nu_{\mathrm{reval}} + \nu_{\mathrm{dam}},
\label{eq:nu}
\end{equation}
with $g_{g,1}=G_g/P_1$, so $\nu$ sums a revaluation term and a
damage dividend. The one-time stock revaluation is
$L = B/P_0 - B/P_1$, a capital levy on (or, if negative, a windfall to)
bondholders.
\end{definition}

The two terms are measured in different currencies, and it is essential not
to read their sum as a fiscal self-financing share; we keep three objects
distinct.

\emph{The resource benefit--cost ratio.} Aggregating the household budget
\eqref{eq:budget} over the invariant distribution, with $\E[y(e;D)]=1-D$
and $\int a\,\dd\Omega=b$, gives $C = r^{ss}b + (1-D) - \tau$, and
substituting the government budget $\tau=r^{ss}b+g_g$ the interest flow
cancels: $C = (1-D) - g_g$. Real interest on the debt is a transfer from
taxpayers to the same households who own the bonds, not a resource cost. So
the change in aggregate consumption from a permanent program is
$\Delta C = (D_0-D_1) - g_{g,1}$, and \emph{aggregate resources rise iff}
$\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}=(D_0-D_1)/g_{g,1}\ge1$. The damage dividend
$\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$ is thus a resource benefit--cost ratio for public
abatement --- comparable to the mitigation/adaptation BCR literature, not to
the fiscal object of \citet{angeletos2024deficits} --- and $\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}\ge1$,
not $\nu\ge1$, is the aggregate-resource threshold.

\emph{The strictly fiscal share.} In the government's books only the
revaluation term is revenue: with lump-sum taxes $\tau=r^{ss}b+g_g$ holds
whatever $D$ is, so the fiscal self-financing share is $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$
alone. It is negative in every lump-sum-financed calibration we report
--- disinflation raises $b=B/P$, raises the real interest bill $r^{ss}b$, and
so raises the required $\tau$ --- and turns positive only once financing
shifts to a proportional levy (Section~\ref{subsec:regimes}). The damage
dividend yields the treasury zero revenue absent a tax base; the production
exercise of Section~\ref{subsec:robustness} supplies exactly that mapping
(an output-tax take $t$ converts an output gain $dY$ into revenue $t\,dY$).

\emph{What $\nu$ is, then.} The composite $\nu=\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}+\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$
is a \emph{net household-burden} offset: by the budget identity
$\tau_1-\tau_0=g_{g,1}-r^{ss}B(1/P_0-1/P_1)$,
$\nu=1-[(\tau_1-\tau_0)-(D_0-D_1)]/g_{g,1}$, so $\nu\ge1$ iff the program does
not raise $\tau+D$. But $\tau+D$ overstates the net \emph{resource} burden by
exactly the transfer $r^{ss}b$ that households receive back as coupon income;
the resource burden is $g_g+D$. We therefore report $\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$
(resources), $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$ (fiscal/distributional), and $L$ (the
transfer) as separate objects, and flag which accounting each headline number
uses rather than leaning on the summed $\nu$ as a financing statement.

\begin{definition}[Demand elasticity]\label{def:elasticity}
Along the demand curve,
\[
\varepsilon_S(P) \;\equiv\;
\frac{d\ln \Sfun\big(1+r^{ss};\ \tau(P),\ D(P)\big)}{d\ln P}.
\]
Since bond supply $B/P$ has price
elasticity exactly $-1$, crossings of \eqref{eq:assetmarket} are
upward (locally unique and stable in the natural tatonnement) where
$\varepsilon_S>-1$ and downward where $\varepsilon_S<-1$.
\end{definition}

A scope note on the clearing object. In the one-asset economy, aggregate
buffer-stock saving and the demand for nominal government debt coincide,
so \eqref{eq:assetmarket} clears total precautionary wealth against
the nominal liability. With richer portfolios --- private capital, equity,
liquidity-differentiated assets --- the DTPL clearing object is the demand
for the nominal government liability \emph{specifically}, and the size of
the revaluation channel then depends on how substitutable that liability
is against its alternatives --- the public-debt-as-private-liquidity margin
of \citet{woodford1990}. The two-asset extension of
Section~\ref{sec:transitions} makes exactly this liquid-bond margin
observable and finds the one-asset qualitative responses intact, but it
closes with a constant liquidity premium; an endogenous convenience yield
on the nominal liability is the open margin, and the one-asset magnitudes
should be read with that caveat.

\subsection{The mechanism, in one picture}\label{subsec:mechanism}

Figure~\ref{fig:mechanism} collects equations \eqref{eq:Kg}--\eqref{eq:assetmarket}
into the causal chain the rest of the paper works through. Green spending
$g_g$ is real resources put in the ground; what makes it a \emph{monetary}
object is that, under a nominal budget, $g_g = G_g/P$ depends on the price
level the program itself helps determine. Green capital lowers climate
damages $D$ through the abatement channel \eqref{eq:abatement}--\eqref{eq:climatefixedpoint};
lower damages raise \emph{effective} household income
\eqref{eq:incidence} (net of the regressive incidence gradient) and lower
\emph{idiosyncratic risk} $\sigma_\epsilon(D)$, and these move
precautionary asset demand $\Sfun(1+r;\tau,D)$ in \emph{opposite}
directions: the income-level effect raises demand (with CRRA
preferences a proportionally richer household holds proportionally more
of the buffer asset --- the scaling of Lemma~\ref{lem:S}), while lower
risk \emph{weakens} the precautionary motive and lowers it. The exact
counterfactual decomposition of Section~\ref{subsec:safeasset} measures
both and finds they partially offset, with the level effect the larger of
the two; the dominant force is the third channel: real taxes
$\tau = r^{ss}b + g_g$ enter the same demand function directly, and
financing the program pushes constraint-adjacent households toward
precautionary saving. Green disinflation is thus tax-and-income driven,
with the insurance content of climate policy the one countervailing
force --- not a standalone story about lower climate risk reducing
precautionary demand.

The asset market then clears
\eqref{eq:assetmarket}, pinning the price level $P^{*}=B/\Sfun(\cdot)$
given the fixed nominal bond stock $B$ --- the DTPL mechanism itself.
The price level does double duty on the way back: it revalues the
outstanding nominal debt stock (the $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$ term of
Definition~\ref{def:nu}, a windfall or a levy on existing bondholders
depending on its sign), and, under a nominal budget, it resets real green
spending for next period, closing the loop between the price level and
the climate block. The avoided damages themselves are the
$\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$ term, and $\nu=\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}+\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$
is the self-financing share of Section~\ref{sec:theory}.

Figure~\ref{fig:mechanism} draws this forward chain only; two channels are
omitted from the picture for legibility. The first is the financing
instrument. Which tax raises $\tau$, and from whom, touches nothing in the
climate block yet has a first-order effect on the sign of
$\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$ (Section~\ref{subsec:financingdesign},
Proposition~\ref{prop:financingdesign}): the same real program can be
revaluation-negative or revaluation-positive depending on how it is paid
for, which is why ``does the green transition cause inflation?'' has no
answer independent of ``who pays for it?'' The second is the feedback from
$P^{*}$ into next period's real spending under a nominal budget --- the loop
the transition of Section~\ref{subsec:tier2} traces out over time rather
than across steady states.

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  font=\footnotesize,
  every node/.style={align=center},
  box/.style={draw, rounded corners, align=center, minimum height=9mm,
              text width=25mm, inner sep=2pt},
  keynode/.style={box, draw=black, thick, fill=black!6},
  arr/.style={-{Latex[length=2mm]}, thick},
  negarr/.style={arr, dashed},
  lbl/.style={font=\tiny, fill=white, inner sep=1pt},
]

 \node[box]    (gg)    at (0,0)    {green spending $g_g$\\ \footnotesize(nominal: $=G_g/P$)};
 \node[box]    (kg)    at (3.6,0)  {abatement capital $K_g$};
 \node[box]    (dmg)   at (7.2,0)  {climate damages $D$};
 \node[box]    (nudam) at (11.0,0) {damage dividend\\ $D_0-D_1=\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$};

 % ---- row 2: climate -> household channels, and real taxes ----
 \node[box] (tau)  at (0,-2.3)   {real taxes\\ $\tau=r^{ss}b+g_g$};
 \node[box] (inc)  at (5.6,-2.3) {effective income\\ $y(e;D)$, regressive};
 \node[box] (risk) at (9.4,-2.3) {idiosyncratic risk\\ $\sigma_\epsilon(D)$};

 % ---- row 3: the market that clears everything above ----
 \node[keynode] (S) at (5.6,-4.6) {precautionary asset demand\\ $\Sfun(1+r;\tau,D)$};

 % ---- row 4: the price level ----
 \node[keynode] (P) at (5.6,-6.9) {price level\\ $P^{*}=B/\Sfun(\cdot)$};

 % ---- row 5: what the price level does ----
 \node[box] (reval) at (3.2,-9.2) {revaluation of\\ nominal debt: $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$};
 \node[box] (nu)    at (8.0,-9.2) {self-financing share\\ $\nu=\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}+\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$};

 % ---- main forward chain (a pure DAG; no feedback drawn -- see text) ----
