 \draw[arr] (dmg) -- (inc);
 \draw[arr] (dmg) -- (risk);
 \draw[arr] (dmg) -- (nudam);
 \draw[arr] (S) -- (P);
 \draw[arr] (reval) -- (nu);
 \draw[arr] (P) -- (reval);
 \draw[arr] (P) -- (nu);
 \draw[arr] (nudam.south) |- (nu.east);

 % ---- signed channels into asset demand (program-induced directions,
 %      matching the exact decomposition of Section 5.8) ----
 \draw[arr]    (tau.south)  |- (S.west)       node[lbl, pos=0.72] {$+$};
 \draw[negarr] (inc.south)  -- (S.north)      node[lbl, midway]   {$+$};
 \draw[negarr] (risk.south) |- (S.east)       node[lbl, pos=0.72] {$-$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\caption{The mechanism. Solid arrows are levels; dashed arrows carry the
\emph{program-induced} direction of each channel into asset demand
(marked $-$/$+$), the signs measured by the exact decomposition of
Section~\ref{subsec:safeasset}. The tax channel and the income-level
channel both push precautionary asset demand up (financing
strengthens the precautionary motive; avoided damages make households
proportionally richer and their buffer stocks scale with income); lower
idiosyncratic risk is the one channel pushing demand down, and it
is too small to overturn them. The price level clears the net shift and
revalues the outstanding nominal debt stock accordingly. Two further
channels, discussed in the text but omitted
here for legibility: the \emph{financing instrument} (which tax raises
$\tau$, and from whom) does not touch the climate block above but flips
the sign of $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$ (Proposition~\ref{prop:financingdesign});
and, under a nominal budget, the price level \emph{feeds back} into next
period's real spending $g_g=G_g/P$, the loop the transition of
Section~\ref{subsec:tier2} traces out over time.}
\label{fig:mechanism}
\end{figure}

% ==========================================================================
\section{Theoretical results}\label{sec:theory}
% ==========================================================================

This section develops the theory in the order the mechanism suggests: when
an equilibrium exists and is unique (determinacy); what the government can
and cannot choose about it (nominal neutrality and demand-determined real
debt); the sufficient-statistic formula through which every remaining
result can be read (the incidence formula,
Section~\ref{subsec:suffstat}); how much of the program comes back, and to
whom (the self-financing decomposition and the sign of the revaluation
channel); when the climate loop itself destabilizes the price level
(sunspots and anchor insulation); and which levers remain once those
constraints bind (accommodation and financing design). Throughout,
Assumption~\ref{ass:feasible} holds.

\begin{assumption}\label{ass:feasible}
$\beta(1+r^{ss})<1$, and the feasibility set
$\{P:\ \tau(P) < \min_e y(e;D(P)) - r^{ss}\bar a\}$ is nonempty (the lower
asset bound is $-\bar a$, from the borrowing limit $a_{t+1}\ge-\bar a$).
\end{assumption}

\paragraph{The analytical status of the results, up front.} The formal
statements below are uniformly labeled but are not of uniform logical
strength, and we flag the status of each so a theory-facing reader can weight
them. Four are \textbf{proved from primitives} and carry the paper's weight:
the nominal neutrality of debt issuance (Lemma~\ref{lem:neutrality}),
steady-state monetary neutrality (Proposition~\ref{prop:muneutral}), the
sufficient-statistic incidence formula (Proposition~\ref{prop:suffstat},
under differentiability at the crossing), and the
exact self-financing decomposition (Proposition~\ref{prop:selffinancing}). The
rest are explicitly qualified. Existence and uniqueness
(Proposition~\ref{prop:determinacy}) hold under the stated crossing and
regularity conditions (R1)--(R2). The multiplicity result
(Proposition~\ref{prop:sunspots}) is \textbf{conditional}: it assumes a
transversal downward crossing, which the calibrated frontier never exhibits.
Anchor insulation (Proposition~\ref{prop:insulation}) rests on a
\textbf{numerically verified} elasticity bound. Optimal accommodation
(Proposition~\ref{prop:optimalmu}) is an analytic \emph{reduction} to a
real-rate problem plus a \textbf{numerically located} interior optimum.
Financing design (Proposition~\ref{prop:financingdesign}) uses an
\textbf{approximate} homogeneity (exact only in the homothetic limit). Each
statement carries a pointer to its proof in Appendix~\ref{app:proofs}, and the
qualifier is repeated at the statement so it is not lost.

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Preliminaries}

\begin{lemma}[Regularity and scaling of asset demand]\label{lem:S}
On the feasibility set, $\Sfun(1+r;\tau,D)$ is well-defined and continuous in
$(\tau,D)$. With $\phi_D=\psi=0$ and a borrowing limit that is either
zero or scales with income ($\bar a = 0$, the benchmark, or
$\bar a \propto 1-D$), CRRA homogeneity gives the exact scaling
\begin{equation}
\Sfun(1+r;\tau,D) = (1-D)\,\Sfun\!\Big(1+r;\ \frac{\tau}{1-D},\ 0\Big):
\label{eq:scaling}
\end{equation}
damages act as a proportional level contraction plus an effective tax
increase. (A fixed positive $\bar a$ breaks the homogeneity, since dividing
the damaged economy by $1-D$ maps the limit $-\bar a$ to $-\bar a/(1-D)$;
the benchmark's $\bar a=0$ is the natural-borrowing-limit case for which the
scaling is exact.) With $\psi>0$ or $\phi_D>0$, $D$ additionally moves the
riskiness of effective income, and $D$ is a genuine second argument.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Climate block]\label{lem:climate}
The fixed point \eqref{eq:climatefixedpoint} exists and is unique for every
$g_g\ge0$, and $D(g_g)$ is continuous and strictly decreasing (strictly, when
$\theta_g\alpha_A>0$). Under the nominal budget regime, $P\mapsto D(G_g/P)$
is therefore strictly increasing; under the real mandate it is constant.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Nominal anchor]\label{lem:anchor}
In any stationary equilibrium with the nominal growth rule
\eqref{eq:nominalrule}, $\pi^{ss}=\mu$ and $1+r^{ss}=(1+i^{ss})/(1+\mu)$:
policy pins the real rate independently of $P$, so all price-level
determination runs through the single equation $\Phi(P)=0$.
(\citealp{hagedorn2026dtpl}.)
\end{lemma}

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Determinacy}

\begin{proposition}[Existence and green determinacy]\label{prop:determinacy}
Suppose $\theta_g=0$, or the green budget is indexed. Under
Assumption~\ref{ass:feasible} and the crossing condition that the
feasibility set extends to price levels low enough for bond supply to exceed
the (bounded) buffer-stock demand, $\sup_{P}\{B/P - \Sfun(1+r^{ss};\tau(P),D)\}>0$:
(i) a stationary green equilibrium exists;
(ii) if $\varepsilon_S(P)>-1$ wherever $\Phi=0$, it is unique;
(iii) the climate block adds only real, recursively determined objects
$(K_g, X, D)$ and does not alter (i)--(ii).
The crossing condition is not implied by Assumption~\ref{ass:feasible} alone:
when the tax burden exhausts feasible consumption before $B/P$ overtakes
demand, no stationary equilibrium exists --- the fiscal-space collapse
documented, under both the nominal budget and the indexed mandate, in
Section~\ref{subsec:sunspots}.
\end{proposition}
\noindent\emph{Status:} \textbf{proved from primitives} under the crossing
condition and the maintained regularity conditions (R1)--(R2) stated in the
proof; proof in Appendix~\ref{app:proofs}.

The economic content of (iii) parallels the capital extension in
\citet{hagedorn2026dtpl}: real state variables added to the economy absorb
real equations; the asset market keeps its one job of pricing nominal debt.
Existence, by contrast, is genuinely conditional --- indexation removes the
multiplicity channel but not the possibility of collapse (Table
\ref{tab:frontier}), so the two failure modes are distinct and only the
first is repaired by denomination.

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{The real fixed point: neutrality and demand-determined debt}
\label{subsec:neutrality}

The determinacy analysis is cleaner --- and one of the paper's messages
sharper --- once we observe that the ``monetary'' equilibrium is a real
fixed point with the price level layered on top. Substituting $b=B/P$ and,
under the nominal budget, $g_g=G_g/P=(G_g/B)\,b$ into the government budget
\eqref{eq:gbc} and the asset-market condition \eqref{eq:assetmarket}
collapses the stationary system to a single equation in \emph{real debt}:
\begin{equation}
b \;=\; \Sfun\!\Big(1+r^{ss};\ b\big(r^{ss}+\tfrac{G_g}{B}\big),\
D\big(\tfrac{G_g}{B}\,b\big)\Big),
\qquad 1+r^{ss}=\tfrac{1+i^{ss}}{1+\mu}.
\label{eq:realfp}
\end{equation}

\begin{lemma}[Nominal neutrality of debt issuance]\label{lem:neutrality}
In \eqref{eq:realfp} the nominal debt stock $B$ enters only through the
ratio $G_g/B$. Hence the stationary real allocation
$(b,g_g,D,K_g,\tau,\Omega)$ depends on $(i^{ss},\mu,G_g/B)$ alone, and the
equilibrium price level $P^{*}=B/b$ is homogeneous of degree one in
$(B,G_g)$: a proportional rescaling of the nominal debt stock and the
nominal green appropriation rescales the price level one-for-one and leaves
every real object unchanged.
\end{lemma}
\noindent\emph{Status:} \textbf{proved from primitives} (homogeneity of the
real fixed point in the nominal anchors); the argument is displayed above.

Two implications reframe the paper's fiscal question. First, in the
stationary equilibrium real debt $b$ is a fixed point of household asset
demand \eqref{eq:realfp} --- the government does not choose it. Its only
fiscal levers are the real rate $r^{ss}$ (through $i^{ss},\mu$), the tax
instrument and its incidence, and the real scale of the program $G_g/B$; it
cannot choose its real debt level. The neutrality is specifically a
\emph{joint} proportional rescaling: multiplying $B$ and $G_g$ by a common
factor (holding $G_g/B$ fixed) raises $P$ in the same proportion and leaves
the entire real allocation unchanged (Lemma~\ref{lem:neutrality}). Raising
$B$ \emph{alone}, at a fixed nominal program $G_g$, is not neutral:
it lowers the real scale $G_g/B$ and therefore does change the real
allocation --- it is a smaller real program, not more real debt.

\emph{There
is therefore no stationary deficit to finance:} taxes adjust to
$\tau=r^{ss}b+g_g$ every period, so a permanent program is
contemporaneously tax-financed, and the financing designs of
Section~\ref{subsec:regimes} differ in \emph{tax incidence}, not in
deficit-versus-tax. A genuine deficit --- a tax path below
$\tau_t=r^{ss}b_t+g_{g,t}$, which issues nominal debt above trend --- can exist
only along a transition, where it is a tax-timing problem in the
sense of \citet{angeletos2024deficits} rather than a change in the terminal
debt level, which Lemma~\ref{lem:neutrality} pins. It is a financing
\emph{choice} (the debt-stabilization lever of the Phillips-curve exercises,
Appendix~\ref{app:nkmodel}); the benchmark nonlinear DTPL transition of
Section~\ref{subsec:tier2} does not take it --- it holds the service rule
$\tau_t=r^{ss}b_t+g_{g,t}$, so its real-debt dynamics are the \emph{revaluation}
of the predetermined nominal stock, not a primary deficit
(Section~\ref{subsec:tier2} makes the accounting explicit).

We recast the
determinacy conditions in $b$ accordingly: $\varepsilon_S(P)<-1$ is
equivalent to $d\ln\Sfun/d\ln b>1$ (asset demand steeper than the
$45^{\circ}$ line in real debt), which is the transparent form of the
multiplicity criterion.

\begin{proposition}[Steady-state monetary neutrality]\label{prop:muneutral}
The nominal-policy pair $(i^{ss},\mu)$ enters \eqref{eq:realfp} only through
$1+r^{ss}=(1+i^{ss})/(1+\mu)$. Hence, holding $r^{ss}$ fixed and moving
$(i^{ss},\mu)$ jointly along the Fisher relation leaves the entire real
allocation and utilitarian welfare $W$ exactly invariant. Steady-state
inflation is neutral at a fixed real rate: $W(\mu)$ computed at fixed
$i^{ss}$ equals $W\big(r^{ss}(\mu)\big)$ relabelled.
\end{proposition}
\noindent\emph{Status:} \textbf{proved from primitives} (substitution into the
real fixed point); the argument is the one displayed above.

Proposition~\ref{prop:muneutral} settles the interpretation of the
optimal-accommodation exercise (Section~\ref{subsec:optmu}) by proof rather
than by assertion: because varying $\mu$ at fixed $i^{ss}$ moves $r^{ss}$
one-for-one, the reported $\mu^{*}=0.045$ is the optimal real rate
$r^{ss*}\approx-0.5\%$, and the welfare gain is the collapse of the real
interest burden --- the \citet{aiyagarimcgrattan1998} optimal-quantity-of-debt
force in a climate economy, not an inflation result. The neutrality is a
property of the deterministic steady state only; it fails along the
transition and under aggregate risk, where the state-contingent real value
$B/P_s$ of the nominal bond makes the nominal level payoff-relevant
(Sections~\ref{subsec:aggrisk},~\ref{subsec:tier2}).

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{The incidence formula}\label{subsec:suffstat}

The results that follow --- the sign of the revaluation channel, its
dependence on the financing instrument, the multiplicity region, and the
insulation value of indexation --- are all local statements about one
equation, the market-clearing condition $\Sfun(P,g)=B/P$. It is worth
extracting the common structure once, as a sufficient-statistic formula, and
then reading each result off it. We state it for a \emph{general} productive
public program, because nothing in the argument is specific to climate: let
$g$ index any permanent public spending that reaches household asset demand
through a vector of channels --- the taxes that finance it, the level of
effective income it changes, the idiosyncratic risk it changes --- and write
equilibrium asset demand as the composite
\begin{equation}
\Sfun(P,g) \;=\;
\Sfun\!\big(1+r^{ss};\ \tau(P,g),\ D(P,g),\ \sigma_\epsilon(P,g)\big),
\label{eq:Scomposite}
\end{equation}
where the $P$-arguments collect every equilibrium feedback already present in
Definition~\ref{def:elasticity} (the interest bill $r^{ss}B/P$ inside
$\tau$; under a nominal budget, the erosion $g_g=G_g/P$ inside $D$) and the
$g$-arguments collect the program's direct effects at a fixed price level.

\begin{proposition}[The price response to public spending: a sufficient
statistic]\label{prop:suffstat}
Let $(P^{*},g)$ satisfy $\Phi(P^{*},g)\equiv\Sfun(P^{*},g)-B/P^{*}=0$, and
suppose $\Sfun$ is differentiable at $(P^{*},g)$ with
$\varepsilon_S(P^{*})\neq-1$, where
$\varepsilon_S\equiv\partial\ln\Sfun/\partial\ln P|_{g}$ is the demand
elasticity of Definition~\ref{def:elasticity}. Then $P^{*}(g)$ is locally the
unique differentiable equilibrium branch, and
\begin{equation}
\frac{d\ln P^{*}}{dg}
\;=\;
-\,\frac{\eta_g}{1+\varepsilon_S},
\qquad
\eta_g \;\equiv\; \frac{\partial\ln \Sfun}{\partial g}\Big|_{P}
\;=\;
\frac{\partial\ln\Sfun}{\partial\tau}\,\frac{\partial\tau}{\partial g}
+\frac{\partial\ln\Sfun}{\partial D}\,\frac{\partial D}{\partial g}
+\frac{\partial\ln\Sfun}{\partial\sigma_\epsilon}\,
 \frac{\partial\sigma_\epsilon}{\partial g},
\label{eq:suffstat}
\end{equation}
all partials at a fixed price level.
In particular: (i) on the locally determinate region $\varepsilon_S>-1$, the
\emph{sign} of the price response is the sign of $-\eta_g$ --- a property of
the program's financing and its income/risk content, not of the asset
market; and (ii) the \emph{magnitude} is amplified by $1/(1+\varepsilon_S)$,
which diverges as $\varepsilon_S\downarrow-1$, the boundary of the
multiplicity region.
\end{proposition}
\noindent\emph{Status:} \textbf{proved from primitives} (implicit function
theorem at the crossing) under differentiability of $\Sfun$ at the
equilibrium, the regularity that (R1)--(R2) supply; proof in
Appendix~\ref{app:proofs}.

Every headline result in this paper is a reading of \eqref{eq:suffstat}.
The financing-design result (Proposition~\ref{prop:financingdesign}) is the
\emph{numerator}: lump-sum finance sets $\partial\tau_{ls}/\partial g=1$ and
activates the tax semi-elasticity $\partial\ln\Sfun/\partial\tau_{ls}\ge0$
(measured at $\epsTau$ in
Section~\ref{subsec:safeasset}), while a proportional levy replaces that
term with $\partial\ln\Sfun/\partial\vartheta<0$ (measured at $\epsLevy$)
--- the same real
program flips the sign of $d\ln P/dg$ because the financing instrument flips
the sign of $\eta_g$, with the denominator untouched. Green disinflation
(Result~\ref{res:disinflation}) is the statement that under lump-sum finance
the tax and income-level terms of $\eta_g$ dominate the risk term. The
sunspot region (Proposition~\ref{prop:sunspots}) is the \emph{denominator}:
$1+\varepsilon_S$ passing through zero is exactly the transversal downward
crossing that generates multiplicity, and anchor insulation
(Proposition~\ref{prop:insulation}) works by deleting the climate feedback
$D(P)$ from $\varepsilon_S$, holding the denominator away from zero for any
program size. The marginal self-financing condition of
Proposition~\ref{prop:selffinancing}(ii) below uses
$\epsilon_P\equiv d\ln P/dg_g$, which is this object evaluated at the
no-program point. The formula also locates the portfolio bound: in a
two-asset economy $\Sfun$ is the demand for the nominal government
liability specifically, so the channel semi-elasticities are computed on
the liquid margin and scale with the liquid share $\omega$ --- which is
where the magnitude discipline of Section~\ref{subsec:safeasset} enters,
an interpretation the two-asset extension makes concrete rather than a
part of the proposition. Finally, the measured elasticity map of
Section~\ref{subsec:safeasset} (Table~\ref{tab:elasticitymap}) is precisely
the empirical content of the formula: it measures
$\partial\ln\Sfun/\partial\tau$ and $\partial\ln\Sfun/\partial\vartheta$
and their dependence on primitives, so the sign claims stand
or fall with measurable objects rather than with the climate block.

Nothing above referenced climate. Any permanent program that changes the
after-tax income process households insure against --- public adaptation
capital, conventional infrastructure, public R\&D --- obeys
\eqref{eq:suffstat}; the climate sector of Section~\ref{subsec:climate} is
one particular map $g\mapsto(\tau,D,\sigma_\epsilon)$, chosen because it
supplies an empirically disciplined income-and-risk channel and a
policy question in which the financing instrument is genuinely contested.
Read this way, the paper's analytical contribution is an incidence theorem
for nominal public liabilities; the climate application supplies its
quantitative discipline.

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Self-financing and the sign of the revaluation channel}

\begin{proposition}[Self-financing decomposition]\label{prop:selffinancing}
(i) $\nu=\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}+\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$ exactly, with
$\nu_{\mathrm{reval}} = r^{ss}B(1/P_0-1/P_1)/g_{g,1}$ and
$\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}=(D_0-D_1)/g_{g,1}$.
(ii) For a marginal program $dG_g>0$ evaluated at the no-program steady
state ($g_g=0$), with
$\kappa \equiv -\partial D/\partial g_g \ge 0$ the marginal damage dividend
and $\epsilon_P \equiv d\ln P/dg_g$ the equilibrium price response, the net
burden changes by
\begin{equation}
dN \;=\; \big(1-\kappa\big)\,dg_g \;-\;
\tau^{*}\,\epsilon_P\, dg_g ,
\label{eq:marginal}
\end{equation}
so the program is marginally self-financing iff
$\kappa + \tau^{*}\,\epsilon_P \ge 1$. (Away from the no-program point the
price-response coefficient is convention-dependent: it is $\tau^{*}-\kappa g_g$
if $dg_g$ denotes the direct change $dG_g/P$ at a fixed price level, and
$\tau^{*}-g_g$ under the equilibrium convention $\epsilon_P\equiv d\ln P/dg_g$
used here. The two conventions differ by $g_g\,d\ln P$, and shifting that
wedge moves the coefficient by $(1-\kappa)g_g$; both collapse to $\tau^{*}$ at
$g_g=0$, which is where the proposition is evaluated. The appendix carries the
full derivation.)
An inflationary
revaluation ($\epsilon_P>0$) relaxes this condition, so a marginal damage
dividend $\kappa\ge1$ then suffices; the benchmark's disinflationary
revaluation ($\epsilon_P<0$) is itself a fiscal cost, so full self-financing
requires $\kappa$ strictly above one --- consistent with the calibrated
threshold, where the damage dividend must exceed unity to offset the
negative revaluation.
(iii) The one-time revaluation $L=B/P_0-B/P_1$ falls on bondholders in
proportion to holdings; because wealth is more concentrated than income in
the invariant distribution, $|L|$ is progressive by wealth rank when
positive and regressive when negative (income/welfare progressivity is the
numerical welfare-group finding of Section~\ref{subsec:welfaregroups}, not a
corollary of the wealth ranking).
\end{proposition}
\noindent\emph{Status:} (i)--(ii) \textbf{proved from primitives} (exact
decomposition and the marginal identity at $g_g=0$); (iii) the wealth-rank
statement is exact, the income/welfare reading numerical. Proof in
Appendix~\ref{app:proofs}.

\begin{result}[Green disinflation]\label{res:disinflation}
When $r^{ss}>0$, $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}<0$ iff $P_1<P_0$ iff
$\Sfun(1+r^{ss};\tau_1,D_1) > \Sfun(1+r^{ss};\tau_0,D_0)$: the revaluation
channel subsidizes bondholders whenever the program raises aggregate
asset demand. (Since $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}\propto r^{ss}(1/P_0-1/P_1)$, the
equivalence between the sign of $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$ and the direction of
the price move requires $r^{ss}>0$; under accommodation with $r^{ss}<0$ the
same disinflation $P_1<P_0$ makes $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}>0$ --- a windfall
becomes a levy. The benchmark has $r^{ss}=2\%>0$.) Sufficient conditions: $\partial\Sfun/\partial\tau\ge0$
(the constraint-tightening precautionary effect of lump-sum taxes, which the
benchmark calibration exhibits; see Section~\ref{sec:quant}) together with a
damage dividend that does not lower demand. Under the opposite sign
(that is, $\partial\Sfun/\partial\tau<0$, as in Hagedorn's Result 1 under his
conditions) a sufficiently tax-heavy program is inflationary and
$\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}>0$, the \citet{angeletos2024deficits} configuration.
The sign of the revaluation channel is thus an empirically diagnosable
object, governed by the slope of asset demand in the tax burden.
\end{result}

The disinflation sign is set by the total change in $\Sfun(\tau,D)$
across the two steady states (Result~\ref{res:disinflation}): the tax slope
$\partial\Sfun/\partial\tau$ is its dominant term, but the damage and risk
channels (decomposed below) also enter, and the result requires them not to
overturn the tax term. That said, the tax slope is the pivotal and least
intuitive ingredient --- it is what makes the net effect disinflationary rather
than inflationary --- so we spell out when a
uniform lump-sum tax raises aggregate demand for the safe asset,
against the representative-agent intuition that a higher permanent tax lowers
saving.

Two forces oppose. For households away from the borrowing limit a
higher $\tau$ is a permanent income loss and lowers desired assets in the
standard way ($\partial\Sfun/\partial\tau<0$ locally, the sign under which
Hagedorn's Result~1 and the \citet{angeletos2024deficits} configuration
operate). For households at or near the constraint the tax cannot be smoothed
against borrowing, so it \emph{tightens} the constraint and \emph{strengthens}
the precautionary motive: they rebuild buffers, raising $\Sfun$. The
aggregate slope is positive when the constrained-household force dominates.
What the sign needs is a nontrivial mass of households at or near the
constraint and a tax burden large enough to bite there --- both properties
the calibration takes from the data rather than chooses to sign the result.
The pivotal primitive is the borrowing limit: the benchmark's $\bar a=0$
places roughly a sixth of the population at the constraint, and the measured
semi-elasticity map of Section~\ref{subsec:safeasset}
(Table~\ref{tab:elasticitymap}) finds the positive sign robust across the
swept primitives \emph{except} at a very loose limit ($\bar a$ of two mean
incomes), where the constrained mass empties out and the slope flips to the
permanent-income sign. We deliberately do not assert magnitude comparative
statics in the other primitives: the measured semi-elasticity \emph{declines}
as risk aversion, income risk, or patience rise across the swept ranges ---
the sign is robust in this region while the magnitude is not monotone --- so
the map, not an intuition, is the record
(Section~\ref{subsec:safeasset} measures the elasticity and decomposes the
demand shift directly). This is
the same constrained-consumer force that makes lump-sum taxes contractionary
for demand in HANK; here it operates on the buffer-stock margin and gives the
green-disinflation sign its foundation.

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Climate sunspots and anchor insulation}

\begin{proposition}[Climate sunspots]\label{prop:sunspots}
Under the nominal budget regime, suppose the damage--incidence feedback is
strong enough that $\Phi$ has a transversal downward crossing --- a
price $P_m$ with $\Phi(P_m)=0$ and $\varepsilon_S(P_m)<-1$ --- with the
boundary conditions of Proposition~\ref{prop:determinacy}(i) holding
($\Phi<0$ at the low edge, $\Phi>0$ at the high edge). Then $\Phi$ has at
least three zeros $P_L^{*}<P_m^{*}<P_H^{*}$, alternating
upward--downward--upward; the two stable equilibria (locally unique
under the natural tatonnement) are the outer roots: a green boom
$P_L^{*}$ (high real abatement, low damages and risk) and a brown
stagnation $P_H^{*}$ (low abatement, high damages and risk), separated by
the unstable middle root. With $\psi>0$ or $\phi_D>0$ the green boom has strictly
lower damages and risk than the brown stagnation, but it also carries the
higher real green spending, real debt, and real taxes a lower price level
implies, so the two need not be Pareto-ranked; whether the utilitarian
ranking $W(P_L^{*})>W(P_H^{*})$ holds is a comparison of the full
equilibrium objects, which we verify numerically where multiplicity arises
rather than assert from lower damages alone. Each equilibrium is
self-fulfilling: expecting the high price
level means expecting low abatement and high damages concentrated on
constrained households, whose precautionary response validates the high price
level, and conversely. The strategic complementarity is
\begin{equation*}
\text{beliefs about } P \ \longrightarrow\ g_g=\tfrac{G_g}{P}
\ \longrightarrow\ D,\ \sigma_\epsilon(D),\ \chi\text{-incidence}
\ \longrightarrow\ \Sfun \ \longrightarrow\ P=\tfrac{B}{\Sfun}.
\end{equation*}
\end{proposition}
\noindent\emph{Status:} \textbf{conditional} on the hypothesized transversal
downward crossing, which the calibrated frontier does not exhibit
(Section~\ref{subsec:sunspots}); proof in Appendix~\ref{app:proofs}.

The incidence gradient $\psi$ is quantitatively central here: it determines
whether the damage channel of $\varepsilon_S$ is strong enough to cross $-1$.
With $\psi=0$ the level and risk effects of $D$ on $\Sfun$ largely offset in
the benchmark (Section~\ref{sec:quant}); with $\psi>0$ damages fall on the
households whose buffers respond most, steepening the feedback.

\begin{proposition}[Anchor insulation]\label{prop:insulation}
Under the real mandate, $D$ is constant in $P$ (Lemma~\ref{lem:climate}), so
the climate channel of $\varepsilon_S$ vanishes and
$\varepsilon_S(P) = \eta_\tau\, d\ln\tau/d\ln P$, where
$\eta_\tau \equiv \partial\ln\Sfun/\partial\ln\tau$ is the tax
\emph{elasticity} of asset demand (the semi-elasticity of
Table~\ref{tab:elasticitymap} times $\tau$) and
$d\ln\tau/d\ln P = -\,r^{ss}b/\tau$. When $r^{ss}\ge0$ and $\tau>0$ this
lies in $(-1,0]$, so $|\varepsilon_S|\le|\eta_\tau|$; when accommodation
drives $r^{ss}<0$ the derivative is positive, with magnitude
$|r^{ss}|b/\tau<1$ precisely when $\tau>|r^{ss}|b$ (i.e.\ $g_g>2|r^{ss}|b$).
On the region where this magnitude condition and $|\eta_\tau|<1$ both hold
--- a magnitude verified numerically across the calibrated
$(\theta_g,\psi,\phi_D)$ grid, not established analytically for all
parameters --- the mandate has $|\varepsilon_S|<1$ everywhere and the
equilibrium, if one exists (Proposition~\ref{prop:determinacy}), is
unique. Indexation thus removes the multiplicity channel conditionally on
these bounds; it does not by itself guarantee existence.
\end{proposition}
\noindent\emph{Status:} \textbf{numerically verified} elasticity bound (holds
across the calibrated grid, not proved for all parameters); proof in
Appendix~\ref{app:proofs}.

\begin{corollary}[Greenness of the nominal anchor]\label{cor:anchor}
Determinacy requires insulating the nominal anchor from the climate loop:
keep debt nominal (Lemma~\ref{lem:anchor} provides the anchor), and
index the green budget (removing the loop).
\end{corollary}

Two remarks follow. Without the climate loop, nominal-growth rules
guarantee uniqueness for any shape of $\Sfun$ \citep{hagedorn2026dtpl}; with
the loop, the guarantee survives only for indexed spending, so the ranking of
nominal versus indexed rules is reversed for the spending line and unchanged
for debt. And full indexation --- real debt and real spending together ---
removes every nominal object from \eqref{eq:assetmarket}, leaving $P$
indeterminate for the classical reason \citep{sargentwallace1975}: insulation
concerns where the anchor sits, not whether there is one.

Three boundaries scope these results. \emph{Existence} in
Proposition~\ref{prop:determinacy} is conditional on Assumption
\ref{ass:feasible}'s feasibility set --- near the low-price edge, bond
supply must exceed the bounded asset demand, which is what the proof's
boundary condition delivers and what can fail when the tax burden exhausts
feasible consumption. \emph{Uniqueness} in
Proposition~\ref{prop:insulation} is conditional on existence: the mandate
guarantees a single crossing wherever a crossing exists, not that one
exists. And quantitatively, the binding failure mode in the calibrated
economy is the first, not the second: the diagnostics of
Section~\ref{subsec:sunspots} find \emph{no-equilibrium} (fiscal-space
collapse) under strong regressive incidence, under the nominal budget
and the mandate alike, while the sunspot region itself is empty at
the benchmark. Multiplicity and its indexation remedy are conditional
design insurance; the measured constraint on green fiscal space is
existence, and the instrument that restores it is accommodation.

The collapse is not an artifact of an unbounded incidence tail. The
regressive gradient $\chi(e)=e^{-\psi}/\E[e^{1-\psi}]$ grows without bound as
$\psi$ rises, so a household's damage share $D\chi(e)$ could in principle
exceed one and drive effective income negative. We impose an \emph{economic}
floor instead: no household loses more than a fixed fraction of its endowment
to climate damages, $y(e;D)\ge \underline{s}\,e$ with a benchmark
$\underline{s}=0.05$, so the fiscal-space object is bounded by construction.
The floor is a parameter, so the collapse frontier can be re-derived under a
tighter bound (the solver exposes $\underline{s}$ for exactly this
robustness check): the mechanism of collapse is the tax burden exhausting
\emph{aggregate} feasible consumption, which the floor does not remove, rather
than a single divergent tail, which it does (Appendix~\ref{app:numerics}).

We close by stating what the nonexistence result does and does not identify.
The collapse is a property of a specific set of baseline instruments and
feasibility assumptions, not a universal fiscal-space limit: it uses
\emph{uniform lump-sum} taxes (so the burden cannot be shifted off the
constrained), a \emph{finite} income-state chain, a \emph{zero} borrowing limit
$\bar a=0$, and a feasibility check tied to the \emph{lowest-endowment}
household. Each of these is load-bearing, and relaxing them relaxes the
collapse: a proportional levy shifts the burden toward households with buffer
stock and enlarges the existence region; monetary \emph{accommodation} (a
higher $\mu$, lower $r^{ss}$) lowers the real interest bill that the taxes must
cover and is the instrument that restores existence where indexation cannot; a
looser borrowing limit or a smoother income tail softens the low-endowment
feasibility edge. The claim is therefore conditional: \emph{given}
lump-sum finance and these primitives, strong regressive incidence can exhaust
fiscal space, and the effective repair is accommodation --- not that green
fiscal space collapses for any instrument. The result is best read as a
\emph{conditional incompatibility} between the program, the incidence gradient,
and a \emph{rigid instrument configuration} (a real rate pinned by
$(i^{ss},\mu)$ and lump-sum finance), not as an inherent limit.

Under a broader
macroeconomic reading the very same excess-precautionary-saving pressure that
breaks existence here would instead show up as a \emph{bound on the sustainable
real rate}, or trigger an \emph{endogenous return adjustment} (a lower market
$r^{ss}$ that clears the extra demand) --- the same force Aiyagari--McGrattan
identify as the optimum-quantity-of-debt margin. Our fixed-instrument
environment converts that adjustment margin into a nonexistence knife-edge; a
model with an endogenous real rate would absorb it as a price. We flag this so
the collapse is not over-read as a structural property of green fiscal space. In the same spirit, the
indexed-mandate uniqueness of Proposition~\ref{prop:insulation} is not an
unconditional theorem: it rests on a \emph{numerically verified} elasticity
bound ($\varepsilon_S>-1$ wherever $\Phi=0$) that holds throughout the reported
frontier but is checked, not proved a priori. We state both results as
conditional on their verified hypotheses rather than as universal claims.

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Optimal accommodation}

The preceding subsection left accommodation --- a higher $\mu$, hence a
lower $r^{ss}$ --- as the instrument that restores existence where
indexation cannot. That raises the natural question of how far to push it:
a lower real rate shrinks the interest bill the taxes must cover, but a
higher price level erodes the real program under a nominal budget. The
tradeoff has an interior optimum.

\begin{proposition}[Optimal nominal growth]\label{prop:optimalmu}
Let $W(\mu)=\int V\,\dd\Omega$ be utilitarian steady-state welfare along the
nominal growth rate, holding $i^{ss}$ fixed. At the fiscal margin, an increase
in $\mu$ (i) lowers $r^{ss}$ and the real interest burden $r^{ss}b$ --- a
transfer from bondholders, whose wealth-weighted marginal utility is low, to
taxpayers, whose population-weighted marginal utility is high; (ii) raises
$P$, eroding $g_g=G_g/P$ and hence raising $D$ and risk; (iii) lowers the
return on the buffer asset. If bond wealth is more concentrated than income,
the first effect is first-order at small $\mu$ while the climate cost is
proportional to the program size; the optimum $\mu^{*}$ is then interior and
strictly positive. Its magnitude is quantitative.
\end{proposition}
\noindent\emph{Status:} analytic \textbf{reduction} to a real-rate problem
(via Proposition~\ref{prop:muneutral}) plus a \textbf{numerically located}
interior optimum; proof in Appendix~\ref{app:proofs}.

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Financing design and the revaluation channel}\label{subsec:financingdesign}

The last result formalizes the channel Figure~\ref{fig:mechanism} flagged:
the financing instrument touches nothing in the climate block, yet it sets
the sign of the revaluation term --- and with it who ultimately pays. In
the language of Proposition~\ref{prop:suffstat}, it is a statement about
the numerator of the incidence formula, holding the denominator fixed.

\begin{proposition}[Financing design controls the revaluation channel]
\label{prop:financingdesign}
Fix the real program $g_g$ and consider financing mixes
$(\tau_{ls}, \vartheta)$ satisfying the aggregate budget identity
$\tau_{ls} + \vartheta\,(1-D) = r^{ss} b + g_g$, where $\vartheta$ is a
proportional levy on effective endowments. Suppose (i)
$\partial \Sfun/\partial \tau_{ls} \ge 0$ (the constraint-floor
precautionary response to lump-sum taxes, the sign measured at the
benchmark), and (ii) $\Sfun$ inherits the approximate degree-one scaling of
Lemma~\ref{lem:S} in the proportional factor $(1-\vartheta)$. Then a
budget-neutral shift of financing from the lump-sum component toward the
levy lowers aggregate asset demand, raises the equilibrium price level, and
raises $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$; augmenting the levy with a lump-sum rebate
(so $\tau_{ls}$ falls below the interest burden) strengthens the shift. In
particular, the sign of the revaluation channel is an instrument of fiscal
design, not a structural constant of the green program. The mechanism holds
under the stated sign conditions; the magnitudes are quantified in
Section~\ref{subsec:regimes}.
\end{proposition}
\noindent\emph{Status:} sign result under the stated conditions, using the
\textbf{approximate} degree-one homogeneity of Lemma~\ref{lem:S} (exact in the
homothetic limit; the residual non-homotheticity is second-order for small
$\vartheta$); proof in Appendix~\ref{app:proofs}.

% ==========================================================================
\FloatBarrier
\section{Quantitative analysis}\label{sec:quant}
% ==========================================================================

This section turns the propositions into numbers, and its subsections are
ordered as an argument rather than a list. A brief worked example
(Section~\ref{subsec:calibration}) makes the moving parts visible; no
headline number rests on it. Every quantitative claim is stated on the
externally disciplined damage columns of Section~\ref{subsec:calibrated}
(debt-to-income and program scale calibrated, damages anchored to DICE /
DJO--BHM / Bilal--K\"anzig), and Section~\ref{subsec:robustness} asks what one
must believe for the answers to change, including the production margin the
endowment economy omits. Having established \emph{how much}, we ask
\emph{why} --- the safe-asset channel isolated node by node
(Section~\ref{subsec:safeasset}) --- and \emph{what it is worth} when the
climate risk is aggregate rather than idiosyncratic
(Section~\ref{subsec:aggrisk}). The final step is \emph{who pays}: welfare
incidence by wealth group (Section~\ref{subsec:welfaregroups}) and the
financing designs that reverse it (Section~\ref{subsec:regimes}).
Section~\ref{sec:transitions} then puts the same economy in motion; the
worked-example detail, the optimal-accommodation exercise, and the bounding
sweeps are collected in Appendix~\ref{app:supp}.

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{A worked example}\label{subsec:calibration}

Before the disciplined calibration, we exhibit the mechanism on a
deliberately magnified example: the household block of the replication
benchmark ($\beta=0.96$, $\sigma=2$, $\bar a=0$), no-abatement damages of
$10\%$ of endowments, and a program several times a realistic scale, chosen
so that every channel is visible (the full parameterization, the benchmark
table, and the $\nu(\theta_g)$ sweep are in
Appendix~\ref{subsec:workedexample}). The example makes three points that
survive at the disciplined scale. The program raises precautionary
asset demand --- avoided damages raise effective incomes, and the taxes that
service the program tighten constraint-adjacent households --- so the price
level falls (Figure~\ref{fig:pfig1}); the revaluation channel is negative and
the entire financing contribution comes from the damage dividend ($\nu=0.60$
with $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}=-0.24$ and $\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}=0.84$). Bondholders
collect a one-time windfall concentrated at the top of the wealth
distribution. And an unproductive nominal program ($\theta_g=0$) is
worse than its sticker price ($\nu=-0.28$), because the
disinflationary asset-demand response operates whatever the abatement
payoff. Nothing in the paper's contribution rests on these magnitudes; the
disciplined columns are in Section~\ref{subsec:calibrated}.

\pfigguard{PFig1_green_steady_state.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.68\textwidth]{PFig1_green_steady_state.pdf}
\caption{Green disinflation as a demand shift. The no-program and program
asset-demand schedules against the bond-supply hyperbola $B/P$: the
program raises precautionary asset demand, so the equilibrium
moves down the supply curve from $P_0^{*}$ to $P_1^{*}$ --- the price
level falls and outstanding nominal debt revalues in bondholders' favor.}
\label{fig:pfig1}
\end{figure}}

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Determinacy diagnostics and the sunspot region}\label{subsec:sunspots}

At the benchmark, the equilibrium is unique for every $\theta_g\le2.5$ under
\emph{both} regimes (Figure~\ref{fig:pfig3}): the measured demand elasticity
never crosses $-1$. The diagnostic explains why, and where to look. The tax
channel contributes $\varepsilon_S \approx -\eta_\tau$ with
$\eta_\tau\approx0.3$ at the benchmark; the damage channel is small because,
with $\psi=0$, the level effect of damages on demand (negative, by the
scaling in Lemma~\ref{lem:S}) and the \emph{risk} effect (positive, via
$\phi_D$) nearly offset --- visible directly in the interpolant nodes, where
$\Sfun$ is almost flat in $D$ at fixed $\tau$. The sunspot region of
Proposition~\ref{prop:sunspots} therefore requires the \emph{incidence}
gradient: with $\psi>0$, damages compress the incomes of
constraint-adjacent households, whose effective floor drops and whose demand
response is largest --- steepening $d\Sfun/dD$ and pushing
$\varepsilon_S$ toward $-1$ for large programs. The extended experiments
sweep $\psi\in\{0,1,2\}$ and a doubled program, at the accommodative stance
$\mu_{\mathrm{ext}}=0.03$ and with the carbon-stock climate sector. Table~\ref{tab:frontier} reports the measured
frontier.

\begin{table}[!htb]
\centering
\caption{The multiplicity frontier (extended run, $n_a=500$,
$\mu_{\mathrm{ext}}=0.03$, carbon-stock sector). ``0*'' = no stationary
equilibrium exists: $\Phi>0$ at every feasible price level (fiscal-space
collapse), verified by same-sign finite boundaries, not a scan artifact.}
\label{tab:frontier}
\begin{tabular}{ccccc}
\toprule
$\psi$ & $G_g$ & roots (nominal budget) & $\min_P \varepsilon_S$ & roots (mandate) \\
\midrule
0 & 0.012 & 1 & $-0.48$ & 1 \\
0 & 0.024 & 1 & $-0.49$ & 1 \\
1 & 0.012 & 1 & $-0.41$ & 1 \\
1 & 0.024 & 1 & $-0.40$ & 1 \\
2 & 0.012 & 1 & $-0.33$ & 1 \\
2 & 0.024 & 0* & $-0.38$ & 0* \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Two conclusions follow. First, \emph{the sunspot region is empty at
the illustrative benchmark}: the measured demand elasticity never falls
below $-0.49$, far from the $-1$ threshold of
Proposition~\ref{prop:sunspots}. Multiplicity therefore remains a
sufficient-condition result in this paper, not a quantitative claim; whether
empirically disciplined incidence and program scales can reach the threshold
is a calibration question we flag for the next pass. Second --- and more
striking --- what strong incidence produces at this calibration is not two
equilibria but \emph{none}: at $\psi=2$ with the doubled program (and,
in the baseline run, at the tight stance $\mu=0.015$), asset demand exceeds
the real value of debt at every price level for which the tax burden is
feasible. Regressive climate damages do not merely select among equilibria;
they can destroy the fiscal space on which the stationary equilibrium
rests --- a collapse that indexation alone does not repair
(the mandate columns fail at the same point) but accommodation does
(all $\psi\le2$ rows with the benchmark program are unique and interior at
$\mu_{\mathrm{ext}}=0.03$).

\pfigguard{PFig3_sunspots_vs_mandate.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{PFig3_sunspots_vs_mandate.pdf}
\caption{Determinacy diagnostics at $\theta_g=2.5$: (a) $\Phi(P)$ under the
nominal budget and the real mandate; (b) measured $\varepsilon_S(P)$ against
the $-1$ line.}
\label{fig:pfig3}
\end{figure}}

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{The extended sector and accommodation neutrality}\label{subsec:extended}

The worked example used the reduced-form climate block; before moving to
the calibrated pass we verify that nothing about the mechanism hinges on
that shortcut. The extended run (carbon-stock climate sector, incidence
$\psi=1$, accommodative stance $\mu_{\mathrm{ext}}=0.03$) delivers a unique interior
green steady state: $P^{*}=0.2826$, $D=0.0709$ (against no-abatement damages
$D(0)=0.0991$), $\tau=0.0768$. Its self-financing decomposition sharpens the
benchmark's message:
\[
\nu = 0.659, \qquad \nu_{\mathrm{reval}} = -0.005, \qquad
\nu_{\mathrm{dam}} = 0.664,
\]
with a one-time bondholder revaluation of only $-0.02$ (against $-0.78$ at
the baseline stance $\mu=0.02$). \emph{Accommodation neutralizes the
revaluation channel}: near the welfare-preferred stance, the no-program and
program price levels nearly coincide ($P_0=0.2842$ vs $P_1=0.2826$), the
program is approximately price-level neutral, and self-financing is almost
entirely the damage dividend. The disinflationary bondholder windfall of the
tight-money benchmark is thus not a fixture of the green program but a property
of the monetary-fiscal stance --- tight stances hand the revaluation to
bondholders; accommodative stances extinguish it. Under the extended sector
the full self-financing threshold also falls: $\nu(\theta_g)$ crosses one
between $\theta_g=1.5$ ($\nu=0.82$) and $\theta_g=2.0$ ($\nu=1.06$), i.e.\
at $\theta_g\approx1.9$ versus $\approx2.4$ in the reduced-form benchmark,
while an unproductive program remains worse than its sticker price
($\nu=-0.11$ at $\theta_g=0$).

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\paragraph{Optimal accommodation is an optimal real rate.} Welfare along the
nominal growth rate is hump-shaped with an interior optimum $\mu^{*}=0.045$
--- but by Proposition~\ref{prop:muneutral} this is not an inflation result:
varying $\mu$ at fixed $i^{ss}$ is varying the real rate, so the
optimum is the optimal real rate $r^{ss*}\approx-0.5\%$, and the welfare gain
is the collapse of the real interest bill --- the
\citet{aiyagarimcgrattan1998} optimal-quantity-of-debt force in a climate
economy, disciplined by the climate loop (real-spending erosion), which is
what makes the optimum interior. The full exercise, its existence boundary,
and its scoping are in Appendix~\ref{subsec:optmu}.

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{The calibrated pass: low, medium, and high damages}
\label{subsec:calibrated}

The results above use the illustrative benchmark, whose fiscal scale is
deliberately large (real debt of roughly five times mean income; a program of
5--6\% of income). The calibrated pass re-targets both margins and
disciplines damages externally: the discount factor is chosen by bisection so
that no-program real debt equals $1.10$ times mean \emph{pre-damage} income
(OECD general-government debt/GDP), giving $\beta^{*}=0.9267$; the program is
scaled to real green spending of $2.0\%$ of mean income at the calibrated
price level (the public share of net-zero investment paths); and the
no-abatement damage level takes three externally disciplined values ---
\textbf{low} $D_0=0.02$ (DICE/\citealp{nordhaus2017}), \textbf{medium}
$D_0=0.06$ (the \citealp{golosov2014}-era empirical range of
Dell--Jones--Olken and Burke--Hsiang--Miguel temperature-damage estimates),
and \textbf{high} $D_0=0.20$ \citep{bilalkanzig2024}. Abatement productivity
$\theta_g$ remains illustrative and is reported as a sweep.
Table~\ref{tab:calibrated} reports the run.

\begin{table}[!htb]
\centering
\caption{Calibrated self-financing by damage column
($\beta^{*}=\betaStar$, debt/income $=1.10$, program $=2\%$ of income,
$\mu=0.02$, $n_a=500$ run, calibration-consistent risk channel). The
$\theta_g$-threshold row is the $\nu=1$ frontier of the robustness sweep
(Section~\ref{subsec:robustness}, same $\beta^{*}$). All numbers are
machine-written from the saved results (\texttt{numbers\_auto.tex}).}
\label{tab:calibrated}
\footnotesize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
 & LOW (DICE) & MEDIUM (DJO--BHM) & HIGH (Bilal--K\"anzig) \\
\midrule
no-abatement damages $D_0$    & 0.02   & 0.06   & 0.20 \\
price level $P_0 \to P_1$     & $\PzeroLow \to \PoneLow$ & $\PzeroMed \to \PoneMed$ & $\PzeroHigh \to \PoneHigh$ \\
damages $D_0 \to D_1$         & $\DzeroLow \to \DoneLow$ & $\DzeroMed \to \DoneMed$ & $\DzeroHigh \to \DoneHigh$ \\
real taxes $\tau_1$           & $\tauOneLow$  & $\tauOneMed$  & $\tauOneHigh$ \\
\midrule
$\nu$                          & $\mathbf{\nuLow}$ & $\mathbf{\nuMed}$ & $\mathbf{\nuHigh}$ \\
\quad revaluation              & $\nuRevalLow$ & $\nuRevalMed$ & $\nuRevalHigh$ \\
\quad damage dividend          & $\nuDamLow$  & $\nuDamMed$  & $\nuDamHigh$ \\
bondholder windfall $|L|$      & $\levyAbsLow$  & $\levyAbsMed$  & $\levyAbsHigh$ \\
full financing at $\theta_g$   & none ($\nu\!=\!\nuDiceAtTwoFive$ at $2.5$) & $\thetaStarMed$ & $\thetaStarHigh$ \\
steady-state welfare $W_0 \to W_1$ & $\WzeroLow \to \WoneLow$ & $\WzeroMed \to \WoneMed$ & $\WzeroHigh \to \mathbf{\WoneHigh}$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Four conclusions. First --- the paper's central conditional statement ---
\emph{whether a green appropriation returns its resource cost is a
climate-damage question}: at a defensible fiscal scale, the self-financing
share runs from
$\nuLowShort$ under conservative DICE damages, through $\nuMedShort$ under
the empirical temperature-damage range, to $\nuHighShort$ --- full
self-financing twice over --- under high-damage estimates. These are
net-burden numbers (Definition~\ref{def:nu}): the strictly fiscal
component is the revaluation term, negative in every column, so
none of the program is fiscally self-financing in the endowment economy
until the production margin of Section~\ref{subsec:robustness} converts
the damage-induced income gain into revenue through a tax base. The model does not claim a green appropriation
returns its cost; it locates the damage regime in which it does. Second,
under DICE damages \emph{no} abatement productivity in the swept range
delivers full financing ($\nu = \nuDiceAtTwoFive$ even at $\theta_g = 2.5$): the
conservative-damage case is a genuine fiscal cost, the sober
counterpart to the high-damage case. Third, \textbf{green disinflation is
robust to the calibration}: the revaluation term is $-0.05$ to $-0.06$ in
every column --- the program lowers the price level by about $5\%$ and hands
bondholders a windfall of $6$--$7\%$ of annual mean income at every damage
level. The sign reversal of the deficit-erosion channel is not an artifact
of the oversized illustrative benchmark. Fourth, in the high-damage column
the program raises steady-state welfare outright ($W$: $-7.55 \to -7.02$)
--- avoided damages and reduced climate risk dominate the added tax burden
--- whereas in the low and medium columns steady-state welfare falls, and
the transition does not rescue it: the transition-inclusive computation of
Section~\ref{subsec:tier2} shows the announced path is more
regressive than the steady-state comparison, so the welfare case at
medium damages rests on the financing design of
Section~\ref{subsec:regimes}, not on transition gains.

\pfigguard{PFig7_calibrated_nu.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth]{PFig7_calibrated_nu.pdf}
\caption{Calibrated self-financing by damage column: stacked revaluation
(negative) and damage-dividend components against the full-financing line.}
\label{fig:pfig7}
\end{figure}}

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Robustness: what one must believe for a green appropriation to
pay for itself}
\label{subsec:robustness}

The climate side of the calibration deserves to be interrogated rather than
defended, and this subsection organizes that interrogation around a simple
principle: parameters that published damage estimates discipline are
\emph{anchored} to those estimates and swept across them; the one parameter
no study pins down is not given a point value at all, but reported as an
axis of the answer. Table~\ref{tab:sources} states where each climate
parameter comes from.

\begin{table}[!htb]
\centering\small
\caption{Climate-block parameters: values, status, and sources. ``Anchored''
parameters take their range from published estimates; ``swept'' parameters
are reported as axes of the results rather than point choices.}
\label{tab:sources}
\footnotesize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}
\begin{tabular}{lll p{0.36\textwidth}}
\toprule
parameter & benchmark & status & source / discipline \\
\midrule
$D_0$ (no-abatement damages) & $0.06$ & anchored, 3 cols &
  $0.02$ DICE \citep{nordhaus2017}; $0.06$ Dell--Jones--Olken /
  Burke--Hsiang--Miguel range \citep{hsiang2017,carleton2022};
  $0.20$ \citet{bilalkanzig2024} \\
$\theta_g$ (abatement effectiveness) & $1.2$ & swept ($0$--$3$) &
  no direct estimate; reported as a frontier (below) \\
$\psi$ (damage incidence) & $0$ & swept ($0$--$2$) &
  regressive incidence: \citet{kanzig2023carbon}, \citet{fried2018} \\
$\phi_D$ (damage--risk link) & $0.5$ & swept ($0$--$1$) &
  reduced form of \citet{acharya2023optimal} risk channel \\
$\delta_g$ (green depreciation) & $0.10$ & swept ($0.05$--$0.10$) &
  public-infrastructure depreciation range \\
program scale & $2\%$ of income & swept ($1$--$4\%$) &
  public green-investment shares in net-zero scenarios \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Two exercises then close the gap between ``illustrative'' and ``robust,''
both on the calibrated economy ($\beta^{*}$ holding debt at $1.1$ times
income; the $2\%$ program). \emph{First, the $(D_0,\theta_g)$ surface.}
The two parameters the headline number is most sensitive to are the
no-abatement damage level and the abatement effectiveness. Rather than
defend point values, we compute the self-financing share on the whole
$(D_0,\theta_g)$ plane and report the $\nu=1$ \emph{frontier} --- for each
damage level, the abatement effectiveness required for full self-financing
(Figure~\ref{fig:pfig20}a). The paper's answer is thus a correspondence,
not a point estimate: a reader who holds DICE-level damage beliefs and a
reader who accepts the \citet{bilalkanzig2024} estimates can each locate
their own $\nu$ on the same map, and the map records at every node whether
the revaluation component stays negative --- so the disinflation result is
checked across the entire plane rather than asserted at the benchmark.
\emph{Second, one-at-a-time sensitivity under a held fiscal target.} Each
household-side variant (risk aversion $\sigma\in\{1,3\}$; the risk channel
$\phi_D\in\{0,1\}$; the incidence gradient $\psi\in\{1,2\}$) re-calibrates
$\beta$ so that debt remains $1.1$ times income before comparing programs
--- differences in $\nu$ along \emph{this} sweep are then pure parameter
effects, not artifacts of a drifting debt ratio --- while variants that
leave the no-program economy untouched (green depreciation $\delta_g$; the
program scale at $1\%$ and $4\%$) hold $\beta^{*}$ fixed
(Figure~\ref{fig:pfig20}b). One caveat applies to the \emph{across-column}
comparison of Table~\ref{tab:calibrated} rather than to this sweep: the
$1.10$ target is set in pre-damage income, so post-damage leverage
$b/[(1-D_0)y]$ drifts up with the damage level ($1.10$, $1.18$, $1.46$
across the low, medium, and high columns), which amplifies the
revaluation and interest-burden terms mechanically; the column-to-column
$\nu$ differences therefore mix the damage effect with a rising leverage
ratio, and only the held-target sweep isolates a pure parameter effect.

Three findings survive the exercise. \emph{First, the required
effectiveness falls sharply in the damage level.} The $\nu=1$ frontier reads
$\theta_g^{*}=\thetaStarMed$ at the medium column, $\thetaStarHigh$ at the
\citet{bilalkanzig2024} high column, and lies above the swept range
($>3$) at the conservative DICE column --- so what one must believe for full
self-financing is monotone and transparent: under high damages even a
modestly effective program pays for itself, while under DICE damages no
effectiveness in the plausible range does, and the answer there is
partial financing ($\nu=0.04$--$0.39$ over $\theta_g\in[0.5,3]$) carried by
the damage dividend net of the revaluation loss. The benchmark $\theta_g=1.2$
sits comfortably below the medium-column frontier, consistent with the
partial self-financing ($\nu=\nuMedShort$) reported above. We read these
thresholds strictly as \emph{internal technology frontiers}, not as
physically-calibrated policy numbers: $\theta_g$ has no clean empirical
counterpart under either the mitigation reading (where $K_g$ acts on the global
carbon stock, so the closed-economy mapping is a coordinated-program stand-in)
or the adaptation reading (where $K_g$ hardens the domestic economy at an
exogenous carbon stock), and the paper straddles the two. So $\theta_g^{*}=
\thetaStarMed$ (medium) and $\thetaStarHigh$ (high) answer ``what abatement or
hardening effectiveness would a $2\%$-of-income program need to break even'',
not ``a $2\%$ program delivers this damage reduction''. Converting the
frontier into physical units --- engineering marginal-abatement-cost curves for
the mitigation reading, or damage-avoided-per-dollar adaptation-cost estimates
for the hardening reading --- is the outstanding external-calibration step; the
statements here are contour results in $(D_0,\theta_g)$ space, and we present
them as such. \emph{Second, the
disinflation is not a calibration artifact:} the revaluation component is
negative at all $\nodesNeg$
nodes of the $(D_0,\theta_g)$ plane, so productive green programs raise
safe-asset demand and lower the price level regardless of the damage or
effectiveness assumption. \emph{Third, the self-financing share is stable to
the household side but genuinely governed by incidence.} Holding the debt
target fixed, $\nu$ moves little with risk aversion ($0.57$ at $\sigma=3$)
or the damage--risk link ($0.56$--$0.61$ as $\phi_D$ runs $0$ to $1$), rises
mechanically when green capital is longer-lived ($\nu=1.09$ at
$\delta_g=0.05$, which doubles the capital stock per unit of spending) or
the program is smaller ($0.62$ at $1\%$ versus $0.50$ at $4\%$, the
concavity of abatement), and --- the one economically important
non-monotonicity --- the revaluation channel \emph{flips sign} under strong
regressive incidence: at $\psi=2$ the program becomes mildly inflationary
($\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}=+0.07$, $\Delta\ln P=+5.9\%$), exactly the
incidence-driven reversal that Propositions~\ref{prop:sunspots} and
\ref{prop:financingdesign} identify. The disinflation result is thus robust
across damage calibrations and effectiveness but is, by design, a knife-edge
in the incidence gradient --- which is the paper's point, not a fragility.
Two variants are informative by their absence: $\sigma=1$ is omitted because
the incomplete-markets economy cannot generate debt of $1.1$ times income at
the calibrated real rate when risk aversion is that low (the precautionary
motive is too weak --- a restriction on the model, not a numerical failure),
which is itself why the benchmark uses $\sigma=2$.

A final exercise probes the endowment structure itself. The natural
objection is that an endowment economy has no output margin: there is no tax
base for the program to expand, so the \citet{angeletos2024deficits} boom
channel is absent by construction. The replication package quantifies one
piece of what this omits with a \emph{hypothetical} production layer
$Y = (1-D(K_g))\,A(K_g)$, $A = 1 + a_Y(1 - e^{-\theta_A K_g})$, run on the
same calibrated program: the productivity margin adds output
$dY_{\mathrm{prod}} = (1-D_0)(A-1)$ whose fiscal take at an average
effective tax rate $t$ is an \emph{additive} revenue term
$\nu_{\mathrm{taxbase}} = t\, dY_{\mathrm{prod}}/g_{g,1}$ (the damage
component of the output gain is already the damage dividend, recomposed not
double-counted). The implied output elasticities $\eta_Y\in[0.026,0.053]$
are reported for scale only; they are not disciplined by the
\citet{bomligthart2014} $0.08$--$0.12$ range, which estimates \emph{core
productive infrastructure} (roads, utilities) that enters output directly.
Abatement capital, in the model and in fact, has no such margin --- it
reduces damages, not marginal cost --- so its true output elasticity is
plausibly near zero, and ``below core public capital'' is not a conservatism
guarantee. The defensible reading is directional: \emph{within this
revenue-only accounting} (labor fixed, no output fed back) the added
tax-base term raises $\nu$, so $\nu^{+}$ rises from $0.58$ toward and past
unity as $\eta_Y$ grows; but this does not sign the full production economy.
Two first-order margins the endowment economy also omits push $\nu$
down --- public capital crowds out private investment (a smaller
private stock, smaller base), and elastic labor supply shrinks the base as
taxes rise --- and the general-equilibrium asset-demand response is unsigned.
The exercise therefore bounds the omitted \emph{revenue} margin in one
direction, not the net general-equilibrium effect. (Under an
adaptation-capital reinterpretation, in which public capital hardens the
economy against damages and plausibly does carry a productive margin ---
the public-adaptation-capital view of \citet{fried2022seawalls} and
\citet{konradtweder2023} --- the
Bom--Ligthart comparison becomes defensible; we return to this in the
conclusion.) Two further pieces of discipline bound the reduced form from
the climate side:
the extended sector of Section~\ref{subsec:extended} replaces the
exponential damage shortcut with the full emissions--carbon-stock
structure and lowers the full-financing threshold (to
$\theta_g \approx 1.9$ from $\approx 2.4$ at the illustrative scale), so
the shortcut is the conservative choice; and the implementation-efficiency
sweep $q_g$ of Section~\ref{subsec:maturity} scales the dividend for
procurement and planning frictions. What no existing study pins down is
$\theta_g$ itself --- a direct calibration to engineering
marginal-abatement-cost curves is the outstanding discipline, which is why
the paper reports frontiers in $\theta_g$ rather than point claims.

% Figure PFig20 (calibration robustness) relocated to
% Appendix~\ref{app:figures} (supplementary figures).

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Why the price level moves: the safe-asset channel isolated}
\label{subsec:safeasset}

The green disinflation result invites an obvious question: \emph{which}
part of the program moves the price level --- the taxes that pay for it,
the damages it avoids, the income risk it removes, or the instrument that
finances it? The steady-state equilibrium condition $P^{*} = B/S(1+r^{ss})$
makes this answerable by exact counterfactuals: we re-solve the economy
switching on one margin at a time and decompose
$\ln P_1 - \ln P_0$ into a tax-burden contribution (program cost, no
climate benefits), a damage level-and-incidence contribution (damages at
the program level, risk held fixed), a risk contribution (income-risk
level at the program value, endowments held fixed --- the $\phi_D$
channel), and an interaction term reported explicitly rather than absorbed.
Each counterfactual is a well-posed economy whose own budget identity
holds, so no node relies on off-equilibrium objects; the financing
contribution is the levy-versus-lump-sum swap at the same program. All
figures in this subsection are steady-state comparisons on the
full-resolution grid ($n_a = 500$) at the calibrated medium-damage scale.

This decomposition splits the total green disinflation of
$\ln P_1 - \ln P_0 = -5.23\%$ (from $P_0 = 0.9051$ to $P_1 = 0.8590$)
into:
\[
\underbrace{-5.77\%}_{\text{tax burden}}
\;\underbrace{-\,1.57\%}_{\text{damage level/incid.}}
\;\underbrace{+\,2.08\%}_{\text{risk}}
\;\underbrace{+\,0.03\%}_{\text{interaction}},
\]
with partial-equilibrium asset demand moving from $S(\tau_0, D_0) = 1.105$
to $S(\tau_1, D_0) = 1.169$ (taxes), $S(\tau_0, D_1^{\mathrm{lev}}) =
1.122$ (damage level), and $S(\tau_0, D_1^{\mathrm{risk}}) = 1.084$
(risk); the near-zero interaction term says the three channels are
additive to two decimal places at this scale. Two lessons, one expected
and one not. The expected one: the tax
burden dominates --- lump-sum taxes push constraint-adjacent households
toward precautionary saving, asset demand rises, and the price level
falls, which is the core of green disinflation. The unexpected one: the
\emph{two climate channels have opposite signs}. Avoided damages are
themselves disinflationary ($-1.57\%$): richer households save more in
levels, so the damage dividend \emph{amplifies} the bondholder windfall
rather than offsetting it. Only the risk channel works toward inflation
($+2.08\%$): with less climate-driven income risk, precautionary demand
falls. This $+2.08\%$ rests on a large semi-elasticity: the program moves
$\sigma_\epsilon$ only $0.66\%$ ($0.2060\to0.2046$, through $\phi_D=0.5$),
so the implied $d\ln\Sfun/d\ln\sigma_\epsilon\approx3$ --- buffer-stock
saving is very convex in risk. Since $\phi_D$ is swept rather than estimated
(Table~\ref{tab:sources}), the risk contribution scales roughly linearly
with it and should be read as an axis, not a point.
Green disinflation is therefore not a tax artifact --- it survives
even at zero program cost through the damage-level node --- and its only
countervailing force is the insurance content of climate policy. The
financing swap confirms the regime section at the level of asset demand:
moving the same program from lump-sum to levy financing moves the price
level by $+8.01$ log points, larger than the entire program effect
itself.

\paragraph{The primitive object, and its empirical discipline.} Every result
in this subsection is a manifestation of one primitive: the semi-elasticity of
safe-asset demand to the lump-sum tax, $\partial\ln\Sfun/\partial\tau$. The
tax node measures it directly --- $\Sfun$ rises from $1.105$ to $1.169$ as the
service tax rises by the program's real cost, a semi-elasticity of
$\epsTau$ per unit of $\tau$ in the dedicated measurement of
Table~\ref{tab:elasticitymap} ($\approx+2.8$ at the decomposition node; mean
income normalized to one) --- and it is this
number, not any nominal-financing mechanism, that signs green disinflation
under lump-sum finance. It is positive for the standard buffer-stock reason
(a lump-sum tax tightens constrained households and raises precautionary
saving), but the sign is a \emph{calibrated} property, and the computed map
across its primitives (Table~\ref{tab:elasticitymap}) is more textured than
the intuition suggests. The elasticity declines modestly --- but stays firmly
positive --- as income risk, risk aversion, or the debt target rises (larger
buffer stocks make log demand less sensitive to a given tax; at $\sigma=1$ the
target debt ratio is infeasible, Section~\ref{subsec:robustness}). In the
\emph{borrowing limit} it is non-monotone and, ultimately, sign-determining:
loosening from $\bar a=0$ first \emph{amplifies} the response (households near
an attainable limit respond strongly), then at a very loose limit the
permanent-income effect takes over and the sign flips. Two further margins
operate by design rather than by calibration: a proportional levy, which falls
on those \emph{with} buffer stock rather than those rebuilding it, reverses the
sign by instrument choice (Section~\ref{subsec:regimes}); and substitution
toward other stores of value --- absent from the one-asset benchmark --- spreads
the response across the portfolio and scales the \emph{magnitude} down toward
the liquid-asset share (next paragraph).
We therefore report the elasticity as a measured object with a stated primitive
map, not as a robust sign. Table~\ref{tab:elasticitymap} reports the map in the
main text --- the semi-elasticity at the endpoints of each primitive sweep ---
and these are exactly the $\partial\ln\Sfun/\partial\tau$ and
$\partial\ln\Sfun/\partial\vartheta$ inputs of the
incidence formula \eqref{eq:suffstat}, so the table is the formula's
empirical content. It sharpens the mechanism in two ways the benchmark
alone cannot. First,
the sign is robust where liquidity constraints \emph{bind} and only there: the
elasticity is positive and stable across the income-risk, risk-aversion, and
debt-target sweeps, and positive and \emph{rising} as the borrowing limit
loosens from $\bar a=0$ to $\bar a=1$ (from $\epsAbarLo$ to $+16.8$, as more
households sit near an attainable limit), but it reverses at a very
loose limit ($\epsAbarHi$ at $\bar a=2$): with enough borrowing capacity the
permanent-income effect dominates, a lump-sum tax lowers asset demand,
and the economy switches to the inflationary
\citet{angeletos2024deficits} configuration. Green disinflation is thus a
property of the empirically relevant tight-constraint regime, not a universal
law --- exactly the boundary the theory draws. Second, the level of the
demand shift sits at the top of the wealth distribution even though the
\emph{motive} originates at the bottom: only $\epsBotShare\%$ of $d\Sfun$ comes
from the bottom two baseline wealth quintiles (whose asset levels are near
zero), while the constrained mass itself falls by $1.3$ percentage points ---
households pushed off the constraint into saving --- and upper-wealth
households scale their buffers up. The incidence results inherit the
\emph{motive}; the aggregate demand shift is carried by the wealthy.

\begin{table}[!htb]
\centering
\caption{The tax semi-elasticity of safe-asset demand, $\partial\ln\Sfun/\partial\tau$,
across the primitives that govern it (endpoints of each sweep; baseline
$\epsTau$). The sign is positive across the risk, risk-aversion, and
debt-target sweeps and reverses only at a very loose borrowing limit; the
proportional levy reverses it by instrument design. Computed by
\texttt{decompose\_tax\_elasticity}.}
\label{tab:elasticitymap}
\begin{tabular}{lcc}
\toprule
primitive swept & first node & last node \\
\midrule
borrowing limit $\bar a$ ($0\to2$; tight\,$\to$\,loose) & $\epsAbarLo$ & $\epsAbarHi$ \\
income-risk scale $\sigma_\epsilon$ ($0.15\to0.25$)     & $\epsRiskLo$ & $\epsRiskHi$ \\
risk aversion $\sigma$ ($1.5\to3$)                      & $\epsSigLo$  & $\epsSigHi$ \\
debt target (via $\beta$, low\,$\to$\,high)             & $\epsBetaLo$ & $\epsBetaHi$ \\
\midrule
instrument: lump-sum $\partial\ln\Sfun/\partial\tau$ & \multicolumn{2}{c}{$\epsTau$} \\
instrument: prop.\ levy $\partial\ln\Sfun/\partial\vartheta$ & \multicolumn{2}{c}{$\epsLevy$} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\paragraph{Observable counterparts.} The primitives that govern the sign are
not free parameters; each has a measured counterpart, and the calibration
sits on the conservative side of the evidence. The sign requires a
nontrivial constrained mass: the model's is $17.7\%$ of households, against
hand-to-mouth shares of roughly a third of U.S. households in
\citet{kaplanviolanteweidner2014} --- on this margin the benchmark
understates, rather than assumes, the force that signs the result. The
buffer-rebuild margin itself is the one that generates the large measured
marginal propensities to consume out of transitory transfers at low liquid
wealth \citep{kaplan2018}; a permanent lump-sum tax working through that
margin is the mirrored experiment. What the empirical record does not pin
down is the semi-elasticity's \emph{magnitude} --- the mapping from the
constrained share and liquid buffers to $\partial\ln\Sfun/\partial\tau$ is
model-mediated --- so we treat the sign as disciplined by the
constrained-share and MPC evidence, and the magnitude as a model output
reported with its full primitive map (Table~\ref{tab:elasticitymap}): a
bounded object, not a trained one.

The \emph{magnitude} of the price-level response carries a second, sharper
caveat, and it is a discipline the one-asset economy cannot supply on its own.
Here the nominal government liability is the household's only store of
value, so the entire buffer stock revalues when $P$ moves. In the data the
nominal safe asset --- currency, reserves, and short government debt --- is a
minority of household financial wealth, so the revaluation base is a fraction
of total precautionary saving and the one-asset magnitudes are \emph{upper
bounds} on the price-level and wealth-revaluation effects (the
public-debt-as-private-liquidity margin of \citealp{woodford1990}). The
two-asset extension of Section~\ref{subsec:twoasset} makes this liquid-bond
share observable and finds the \emph{qualitative} responses intact but the
magnitudes scaled down by roughly the liquid share. The scope of that
extension matters: it is a linearized Phillips-curve exercise
with a constant liquidity premium, not a nonlinear DTPL
price-level solve, so it locates the sign and the rough scaling of the
portfolio margin but \emph{cannot} validate the quantitative $5\%$ repricing
--- that would require solving the two-asset economy as a DTPL price-level
fixed point with an \emph{endogenous} convenience yield. Anchoring the liquid
share to micro portfolio data and endogenizing that yield is therefore the
outstanding discipline that would convert the one-asset sign result into a
robust quantitative magnitude; it remains an open item, and the $5\%$ should
not be read as a portfolio-validated number.

\paragraph{Reading the one-asset magnitudes as bounds.} Concretely, then, the
headline revaluations of this paper --- the $\approx5\%$ steady-state
price-level decline, the year-one capitalization of $77\%$ of the long-run move
(Section~\ref{subsec:tier2}), and the announcement capital levy --- should be
read as \emph{upper bounds}, scaled down by the share $\omega$ of household
precautionary wealth actually held in liquid nominal government liabilities. In
US flow-of-funds terms, currency, reserves, and short Treasuries held by
households and money funds are on the order of $\omega\approx0.15$--$0.25$ of
liquid household financial assets, so a first-order portfolio correction puts
the \emph{empirically relevant} steady-state repricing nearer
$\omega\times5\%\approx1$--$1.3\%$ and the announcement capitalization at a
correspondingly smaller level --- same sign, same front-loading, magnitudes
divided by roughly four to seven. We state this as an explicit
\emph{scaling exercise}, not a computed equilibrium: the exact number requires
the endogenous-convenience-yield DTPL solve above, and $\omega$ itself is
policy- and definition-dependent. The qualitative claims (sign, incidence,
front-loading) do not depend on $\omega$; the quantitative magnitudes do, and
we do not want the one-asset numbers read as point predictions.

\pfigguard{PFig15_safe_asset_decomposition.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{PFig15_safe_asset_decomposition.pdf}

the tax-burden, damage, risk, and interaction margins to
$\ln P_1 - \ln P_0$ (GE counterfactuals); (b) partial-equilibrium asset
