% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\FloatBarrier
\subsection{Aggregate climate risk: the safe asset made literal}
\label{subsec:aggrisk}

Everything to this point has been deterministic, so the ``safe asset''
of the mechanism is safe in real terms too, and the phrase is a
metaphor. This subsection removes the metaphor by adding aggregate climate
risk. Let the aggregate climate state follow a two-state
Markov chain --- \textsc{calm} ($D^0=0.06$) and \textsc{severe}
($D^0=0.20$, the high-damage column of Section~\ref{sec:quant}) --- with
a small annual probability of entering the severe state and a mean severe
spell of a few years ($\Pi^{\mathrm{agg}}=[0.95,0.05;\,0.30,0.70]$).
Households still hold only the one-period nominal bond; markets are
incomplete in the aggregate as well as the idiosyncratic dimension. The
price level is now measurable in the aggregate state, $(P_C,P_S)$, and
the bond's \emph{real} return is state-contingent,
\[
1 + r(s\to s') = (1+r^{ss})\,\frac{P_s}{P_{s'}},
\qquad 1+r^{ss}=\frac{1+i^{ss}}{1+\mu},
\]
where $r^{ss}$ is the deterministic real service rate of
\eqref{eq:fisher}. A caveat on the clean DTPL separation: Lemma
\ref{lem:anchor} --- policy pins the real rate independently of $P$ --- is a
\emph{deterministic}-model property. With a state-contingent price level the
realized real return is $(1+r^{ss})P_s/P_{s'}$, realized inflation is not
$\mu$, and the effective real rate is endogenous to the price vector
$(P_C,P_S)$; the nominal rule fixes only the deterministic service rate
$r^{ss}$, and Lemma~\ref{lem:anchor}'s independence holds state by state only
in ergodic mean. So a nominally safe bond is really risky: it loses real
value in exactly
the state where households are poorest --- the systematic, non-diversifiable
climate risk emphasized by \citet{barnettbrockhansen2020} and
\citet{cailontzek2019}. The DTPL becomes a vector fixed
point --- the bond market clears in \emph{each} aggregate state,
$S_s(P_C,P_S)=B/P_s$ --- solved on the exact ergodic joint distribution
over $(a,e,s)$ (household problem in Appendix~\ref{app:numerics}).

The equilibrium prices this climate risk sharply. At the calibrated
benchmark the price level is $37\%$ higher in the severe state than in the
calm state ($P_S/P_C-1 = 0.37$), so a household that holds the nominal
bond into a climate disaster earns $+2.0\%$ in the calm state but takes a
\textbf{real loss of $26\%$} on the transition $\textsc{calm}\to\textsc{severe}$
--- the nominal safe asset is a poor hedge against systematic climate
risk. Its ergodic-mean real return exceeds the deterministic service rate
$r^{ss}$ by $0.4$ percentage points; this gap is a return statistic under
the ergodic distribution, not a market-cleared risk price (there is one
asset and no security pricing it), and we call it the bond's ergodic-mean
excess return rather than a ``risk premium''.
This is the aggregate counterpart of the paper's central object: the same
incomplete-markets demand for the nominal liability that pins the price
level also determines how badly that liability performs when the climate
turns.

\emph{Green public investment lowers this systematic risk.} A permanent
program of two percent of income builds abatement capital that reduces
damages in both states through the same climate block used everywhere
($D_s = D^0_s e^{-\theta_g K_g}$), and because the severe state starts
from a higher damage level the program compresses the gap between the two:
the state price dispersion falls from $0.37$ to $0.27$ and the disaster
real loss shrinks from $26\%$ to $20\%$ (the bond's ergodic-mean excess
return falls from $0.44$ to $0.25$ percentage points). Green investment is therefore not
only a level intervention on average damages (the damage dividend of
Section~\ref{subsec:calibrated}) but a \emph{reduction in the aggregate
climate risk that the safe nominal asset must bear} --- a channel that
does not exist in the deterministic model and that makes the
safe-asset language of the paper literal (Figure~\ref{fig:pfig19}).%
\footnote{These magnitudes are computed on the same full-resolution asset
grid as the deterministic benchmark ($n_a=500$); both the calm and severe
fixed points converge. Adding the aggregate-risk term to the self-financing
decomposition and to the welfare analysis is a natural extension.}

\pfigguard{PFig19_aggrisk.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{PFig19_aggrisk.pdf}
\caption{Aggregate climate risk ($n_a=500$). Two recurrent
aggregate states (\textsc{calm}/\textsc{severe}); the nominal bond is
nominally safe but really risky ($B/P_s$ state-contingent). (a) the two
state-contingent
price levels, baseline vs.\ a $2\%$-of-income green program; (b) the state
price dispersion $P_S/P_C-1$; (c) the bond's real return on a
$\textsc{calm}\to\textsc{severe}$ transition (the disaster return). Green
investment compresses the dispersion ($0.37\to0.27$) and shrinks the
disaster loss ($-26\%\to-20\%$) --- it reduces the systematic climate risk
the safe asset carries.}
\label{fig:pfig19}
\end{figure}}

\paragraph{Where the risk compression shows up: welfare, not the budget.}
It is tempting to add a fourth term to the self-financing decomposition ---
the fiscal value of the compressed premium. That term is exactly zero, for
a clean reason. The government's ergodic-expected real interest bill is
\begin{equation}
\sum_s \pi_s \sum_{s'} \Pi^{\mathrm{agg}}_{ss'}\,\big(R(s,s')-1\big)\,\frac{B}{P_s}
\;=\; r^{ss}\,B\,\E_\pi\!\left[\tfrac{1}{P_s}\right],
\label{eq:premiuminert}
\end{equation}
which is \emph{independent of the risk premium}: with
$R(s,s')=(1+r^{ss})P_s/P_{s'}$ and ergodic $\pi$, the state-contingent
returns average to $r^{ss}$ times the debt, and the premium --- a
risk-timing wedge that makes the bond expensive to service in the
severe state and cheap in the calm state --- washes out in expectation
(Appendix~\ref{app:proofs}). The aggregate-risk self-financing share is
therefore $\nu=\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}+\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$, the ergodic average
of the deterministic decomposition, with no separate aggregate-risk term;
the driver confirms \eqref{eq:premiuminert} numerically to machine precision
(the direct ergodic bill and the closed form agree to $3\times10^{-18}$). In
the calibrated aggregate-risk economy the self-financing share is
$\nu=0.77$ ($\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}=0.85$, $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}=-0.08$),
above the medium-column deterministic value of $0.56$ --- not because
aggregate risk helps the budget, but because the ergodic-mean damage is
higher when a high-damage severe state is possible, so the expected damage
dividend is larger.

The premium is instead a \emph{risk-sharing} object, priced in welfare rather
than the budget --- the state-contingent-real-value role of nominal debt in the
incomplete-markets optimal-fiscal-policy literature
\citep{begs2017,begs2021}. An equal-mean-reduction counterfactual isolates the
value of preferentially insuring the severe state at $+0.13\%$ of
permanent consumption on average (about a tenth of the program's steady-state
welfare cost), concentrated where the mechanism predicts --- six times larger
for a household \emph{in} the severe state, and, under regressive incidence,
tilted toward the constrained households who hold the nominally-safe,
really-risky bond as their only buffer. The full Stage-B welfare decomposition
is in Appendix~\ref{subsec:aggriskwelfare}.

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\FloatBarrier
\subsection{Welfare incidence by wealth group}
\label{subsec:welfaregroups}

Who pays for the program and who benefits? For each state $(a,e)$ we compute
the permanent consumption transfer $\lambda(a,e)$ that makes a household at
that state indifferent between the no-program and program steady states,
aggregated over baseline-distribution wealth quintiles
(Table~\ref{tab:incidence}; generated by \texttt{main\_project\_calibrated}
section C4).

\begin{table}[!htb]
\centering
\caption{Consumption-equivalent welfare incidence of the program under
lump-sum financing, by baseline wealth quintile (percent;
calibrated pass, $n_a=500$ run). These are \emph{across-steady-state}
comparisons --- the permanent transfer that equates a household at
$(a,e)$ between the no-program and program invariant distributions, an
ex-ante/behind-the-veil object that ignores the transition path. The
accounting is explicit: households are evaluated at the \emph{same real
asset position} $(a,e)$ under the two stationary price levels, so no
revaluation of predetermined nominal claims enters these numbers; the
one-time stock revaluation, and the living-household transition-inclusive
incidence it feeds, are computed in Section~\ref{subsec:tier2}.}
\label{tab:incidence}
\small
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4.5pt}
\begin{tabular}{lccccccc}
\toprule
 & Q1 (poorest) & Q2 & Q3 & Q4 & Q5 & top 10\% & aggregate \\
\midrule
LOW (DICE)          & $-3.57$ & $-2.85$ & $-2.57$ & $-2.28$ & $-1.89$ & $-1.75$ & $-2.64$ \\
MEDIUM (DJO--BHM)   & $-2.60$ & $-1.90$ & $-1.66$ & $-1.42$ & $-1.12$ & $-1.02$ & $-1.75$ \\
HIGH (Bilal--K\"anzig) & $+1.82$ & $+2.33$ & $+2.40$ & $+2.40$ & $+2.29$ & $+2.24$ & $+2.25$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Two findings. First, \textbf{lump-sum-financed green investment is regressive
at the steady-state margin}: in the low and medium columns the poorest
quintile loses the most ($-3.57\%$ versus $-1.89\%$ for the richest in the
low column), because the lump-sum tax that services the program bites
hardest for households at the borrowing constraint, while the price-level
decline raises real debt $b=B/P$ and with it the real interest income that
accrues to bondholders at the top --- the steady-state \emph{flow}
counterpart of the windfall; the one-time \emph{stock} revaluation of
predetermined nominal holdings is a transition object and is priced
separately in Section~\ref{subsec:tier2}. Even in the
high-damage column --- where every quintile gains and the program is a
steady-state Pareto improvement across wealth groups --- the poorest gain
\emph{least} ($+1.82\%$ versus $+2.40\%$ at Q3--Q4), for the same reason. The
distributional politics of green investment under lump-sum financing are
therefore inverted relative to the inflation-tax intuition: it is the
\emph{poor} who carry the program and the bondholders who are compensated.
Second, this regressivity is a property of the \emph{financing}, not of the
program --- which motivates the regime comparison below, where the same real
program is financed progressively and the ranking reverses. These are
steady-state numbers, and they are the \emph{optimistic} reading: the
transition-inclusive incidence (Section~\ref{subsec:tier2}) is more
regressive still, because front-loaded taxes bite the constrained before
damage relief arrives while the announcement windfall accrues at the top.

\pfigguard{PFig8_welfare_incidence.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{PFig8_welfare_incidence.pdf}
\caption{Welfare incidence by baseline wealth quintile across damage
columns (lump-sum financing).}
\label{fig:pfig8}
\end{figure}}

\paragraph{Extended groups.} The package additionally cuts incidence by
income-state quintile, by bondholding quintile (which coincides with wealth in
the one-asset economy), by constrained/unconstrained status, and by a high-MPC
proxy group, at the calibrated medium column (full breakdown in the replication
tables). The pattern is uniform and is the point: under \textsc{r1-lumpsum}
every cut of the population loses monotonically from the bottom up --- the
constrained ($17.7\%$ of the population) lose $-2.64\%$ against $-1.56\%$ for the
unconstrained --- so the program is paid for precisely by the households with
the least self-insurance capacity; under \textsc{r3-prop-levy-rebate} every one
of those orderings reverses, with the bottom half in surplus and an
aggregate gain of $+0.67\%$ against the lump-sum regime's $-1.75\%$. Incidence
is thus regressive under pure lump-sum finance and progressive under the rebate
across every grouping, not just the headline wealth quintiles --- while full
self-financing remains conditional on the high-damage, high-effectiveness
region.

\paragraph{Decile resolution.} Quintile cuts blur exactly the two groups the
mechanism singles out: the bottom quintile pools borrowing-limit households
with buffer-stock savers, and the top quintile pools the merely comfortable
with the bondholders who receive most of the revaluation. The driver
\texttt{welfare\_incidence\_deciles} therefore re-cuts the same
consumption-equivalent object at deciles plus top-5\% and top-1\% tails,
splitting borrowing-limit mass points fractionally so that every decile
holds exactly ten percent of baseline mass. At the medium column under
lump-sum finance the bottom decile's CE change is \WDecBotMed\% against
\WDecTopMed\% for the top decile, \WTopFiveMed\% for the top 5\%, and
\WTopOneMed\% for the top 1\%; under the levy-plus-rebate design the same
three cuts give \RRebDecBot\%, \RRebDecTop\%, and \RRebTopOne\%. Two facts
sharpen at this resolution. Under the pure levy (no rebate) the gradient
flips even though every decile still loses: the bottom decile loses least
(\RLevDecBot\%) and the top decile most (\RLevDecTop\%) --- proportionality
alone shifts the burden onto households with buffer stocks. And under the
levy-plus-rebate the top decile is the \emph{only} decile with a negative
CE change, with the loss deepening into the top 1\% (\RRebTopOne\% against
\RRebDecTop\% for the decile): the rebate design is a transfer from the top
decile, not a broad tax. Two caveats
attach to the tails. The model's top 1\% holds \WShareTopOneMed\% of wealth,
and the one-asset Aiyagari tail is thin relative to the roughly one-third
observed in U.S. data, so the concentration of the bondholder windfall is
more plausibly understated than overstated by these cuts. The package
quantifies this directly rather than leaving it as a caveat: an optional
rare high-income state in the spirit of \citet{castaneda2003} re-targets
the model's top-1\% wealth share to the data (driver
\texttt{wealth\_concentration\_fit}, with the discount factor re-bisected
in the augmented economy to hold the same debt target); under the fitted
concentration --- a top-1\% share of \SupTopOneShare\% --- the
medium-column revaluation term is \SupNuReval{} and the decile gradient
runs from \SupDecBot\% at the bottom to \SupDecTop\% at the top decile
(top 1\%: \SupTopOne\%). And below the
median, resolution is limited by the borrowing-limit mass point --- the
constrained share of the population occupies a single asset state --- so
bottom-decile numbers are exact for the model but should not be read as
fine empirical percentiles.

% Figure PFig16 (extended welfare groups) relocated to
% Appendix~\ref{app:figures} (supplementary figures).

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\FloatBarrier
\subsection{Financing regimes: lump-sum, proportional levy, rebate, mixed}
\label{subsec:regimes}

Green spending is not carbon policy, and financing design is a choice. The
package compares four ways of financing the same \emph{nominal} green
appropriation $G_g$ at the calibrated medium-damage column:
(\textsc{r1-lumpsum}) lump-sum tax
financing (the baseline); (\textsc{r2-prop-levy}) a proportional levy on
effective endowments that funds the program; (\textsc{r3-prop-levy-rebate})
the levy at twice the program with half rebated lump-sum (the progressive
rebate design suggested by the incidence evidence in
\citealp{kanzig2023carbon}); and (\textsc{r4-mixed}) an even
lump-sum/levy mix. A naming caveat, because it bears on the
comparison: these are labels for the \emph{tax instrument}, not for a
deficit-versus-tax choice. In the stationary equilibrium nominal debt
issuance is neutral and real debt is demand-determined
(Lemma~\ref{lem:neutrality}), so \emph{none} of the four is a deficit; each
runs a balanced flow identity $\tau_{ls} + \vartheta\,(1-D) = r^{ss} b + g_g$
at its own price level and holds the nominal appropriation fixed. They differ
only in \emph{incidence} --- proportional versus lump-sum --- transmitted
through precautionary asset demand to the price level, the burden share, and
group welfare. (We retain the mnemonic keys in the replication code, where
the lump-sum regime is the baseline whose \emph{primary-deficit} financing
lever, if switched on, lives along the transition of
Section~\ref{sec:transitions} --- though the benchmark transition itself holds
the balanced service rule, so even there the debt path is revaluation, not a
primary deficit; the steady-state comparison here is neither.)

Holding the \emph{nominal} budget fixed has one consequence that is a
feature of the DTPL mechanism rather than an artifact: since
each regime clears at its own price level, the real program $g_g=G_g/P^{*}$
differs slightly across regimes (Table~\ref{tab:regimes}, $g_g$ row: $0.021$
under lump-sum finance, falling to $0.018$ under the rebate as its higher
price level erodes real spending), and with it the abatement and $D_1$. This
matters because Table~\ref{tab:regimes} holds the \emph{nominal} appropriation
$G_g$ fixed, whereas Proposition~\ref{prop:financingdesign} holds the
\emph{real} program $g_g$ fixed; so in principle the table's cross-regime
differences merge tax incidence with a small shift in the real green scale.
Quantitatively the merge is negligible. The damage-dividend component
$\nu_{\mathrm{dam}} = (D_0-D_1)/g_{g,1}$ moves only $0.636$ to $0.647$ across
all four regimes --- a $0.011$ drift, second-order against the
revaluation swing that is the object of the comparison ($\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$:
$-0.055$ to $+0.111$) --- and the welfare ordering is likewise driven by
incidence, not scale: the fixed-\emph{real} recomputation (letting $G_g$ adjust
per regime so $g_g$ is common) leaves both the self-financing ordering and the
bottom-half-versus-top welfare reversal intact, isolating the incidence channel
Proposition~\ref{prop:financingdesign} states. We nonetheless \emph{report} the
fixed-nominal design in the table because it is the policy-relevant object ---
an appropriation is set in nominal terms --- and flag the fixed-real invariance
here rather than burying it. In the
endowment HA-DTPL block, the levy is a \emph{proportional
financing instrument with carbon-tax-style incidence}; the Pigouvian
carbon-tax margin requires the clean/dirty production extension and is not
claimed here (neither instrument distorts a margin, and abatement is public).
Table~\ref{tab:regimes} reports the run (medium damages, calibrated scale;
no-program baseline $P_0 = \PzeroRegimes$).

\begin{table}[!htb]
\centering
\caption{Financing regimes for the same \emph{nominal} green appropriation
(medium damages, calibrated scale, $n_a=500$ run). $g_g=G_g/P^{*}$ is the
implied real program and $D_1$ the resulting damages --- both vary with the
endogenous price level, which is why $\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$ is not exactly
invariant (text). $\lambda_{b50}$, $\lambda_{t10}$: consumption-equivalent
gains of the bottom 50\% and top 10\% of the baseline wealth distribution.}
\label{tab:regimes}
\footnotesize
\setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt}
\begin{tabular}{lcccccccc}
\toprule
Regime & $P^{*}$ & $g_g$ & $D_1$ & $\nu$ & $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$ & $\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$ &
$\lambda_{b50}$ & $\lambda_{t10}$ \\
\midrule
\textsc{r1-lumpsum}      & $\RDefP$ & $\RDefGg$ & $\RDefDone$ & $\RDefNu$ & $\RDefRev$ & $\RDefDam$ & $\RDefBfifty\%$ & $\RDefTten\%$ \\
\textsc{r2-prop-levy}               & $\RLevP$ & $\RLevGg$ & $\RLevDone$ & $\RLevNu$ & $\RLevRev$ & $\RLevDam$ & $\RLevBfifty\%$ & $\RLevTten\%$ \\
\textsc{r3-prop-levy-rebate}        & $\RRebP$ & $\RRebGg$ & $\RRebDone$ & $\mathbf{\RRebNu}$ & $\mathbf{\RRebRev}$ & $\RRebDam$ & $\mathbf{\RRebBfifty\%}$ & $\RRebTten\%$ \\
\textsc{r4-mixed}      & $\RMixP$ & $\RMixGg$ & $\RMixDone$ & $\RMixNu$ & $\RMixRev$ & $\RMixDam$ & $\RMixBfifty\%$ & $\RMixTten\%$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

To isolate the incidence channel from the (second-order) real-scale drift that
holding the \emph{nominal} appropriation fixed induces, Table~\ref{tab:regimesreal}
recomputes the same four regimes holding the \emph{real} program $g_g$ fixed at
its baseline value (letting $G_g$ adjust per regime) --- the convention of
Proposition~\ref{prop:financingdesign}. By construction the damage dividend is
then identical across regimes, so only the revaluation share and the
welfare incidence can move, and the cross-regime differences are pure tax
incidence plus the price-level response they induce, with no change in the real
green scale. The revaluation swing and the welfare reversal survive the change
of convention; the fixed-nominal and fixed-real revaluation shares differ only
in the third decimal.

\begin{table}[!htb]
\centering
\caption{Financing regimes holding the \emph{real} program fixed (pure
incidence; companion to Table~\ref{tab:regimes}). $g_g$ is common across
regimes, so the damage dividend is identical and only $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$ and
welfare move. Populated by \texttt{regimes\_fixed\_real}; entries show
``\pendingnum'' until that driver has been run.}
\label{tab:regimesreal}
\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
\toprule
regime & $P^{*}$ & $\nu$ & $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$ & bottom-50\%\,/\,top-10\% \\
\midrule
\textsc{r1-lumpsum}         & $\RDefRealP$ & $\RDefRealNu$ & $\RDefRealRev$ & $\RDefRealBfifty\%$\,/\,$\RDefRealTten\%$ \\
\textsc{r2-prop-levy}       & $\RLevRealP$ & $\RLevRealNu$ & $\RLevRealRev$ & $\RLevRealBfifty\%$\,/\,$\RLevRealTten\%$ \\
\textsc{r3-prop-levy-rebate}& $\RRebRealP$ & $\RRebRealNu$ & $\RRebRealRev$ & $\RRebRealBfifty\%$\,/\,$\RRebRealTten\%$ \\
\textsc{r4-mixed}           & $\RMixRealP$ & $\RMixRealNu$ & $\RMixRealRev$ & $\RMixRealBfifty\%$\,/\,$\RMixRealTten\%$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Three results, the first of which we regard as the sharpest in the paper.
First, \textbf{the sign of the revaluation channel is a policy choice}
(Proposition~\ref{prop:financingdesign}): moving from lump-sum to
proportional financing flips green disinflation ($\nu_{\mathrm{reval}} =
-0.055$) into mild green inflation ($+0.030$; $+0.111$ with the rebate).
The mechanism is the asset-demand incidence at the heart of the DTPL:
lump-sum taxes push constraint-adjacent households toward their borrowing
limit and \emph{raise} precautionary demand (the price level falls, the
fisc loses the revaluation); a proportional levy scales endowments and
lowers demand roughly proportionally (the price level rises, real
debt erodes). Second, the progressive design wins on \emph{both} margins
simultaneously: the levy-plus-rebate regime is the most self-financing
($\nu = 0.758$, a fifth more than lump-sum financing) and the most
progressive (the bottom half gains $+1.13\%$ against $-2.16\%$ under
lump-sum financing, while the top decile is nearly unharmed at $-0.12\%$
versus $-1.02\%$) --- a near-Pareto dominance over the conventional lump-sum
regime. Third, the magnitudes quantify how much the DTPL incidence channel
is worth: all four regimes fund the same nominal appropriation (the real
program varies by under a sixth across them, Table~\ref{tab:regimes}), yet
the bottom half's welfare differs by
$3.3$ percentage points of permanent consumption between the worst and best
design. Financing structure is not fiscal bookkeeping in this economy; it
is a price-level and distribution instrument. The rebate design is the
general-equilibrium cousin of carbon fee-and-dividend proposals, whose
progressivity is documented in the incidence evidence we calibrate to
\citep{kanzig2023carbon} and analyzed in the optimal-taxation setting of
\citet{douennehummelpedroni}; what those real-economy analyses cannot
deliver --- and what the DTPL adds --- is that the same rebate also moves
the \emph{price level} and hence the self-financing share, because the
incidence of the financing instrument is what precautionary asset demand
prices.

\pfigguard{PFig9_financing_regimes.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{PFig9_financing_regimes.pdf}
\caption{Financing regimes: (a) self-financing decomposition; (b) welfare
incidence, bottom 50\% versus top 10\%.}
\label{fig:pfig9}
\end{figure}}

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\FloatBarrier
\paragraph{Bounding the revaluation channel.} Because the revaluation channel
is the fiscal side of the mechanism, its magnitude is bounded by the classic
inflating-away limits --- debt maturity, indexation leakage, holder composition
--- and by implementation efficiency. In steady-state comparisons a level jump
in $P$ revalues all nominal promises proportionally regardless of maturity, so
the binding limits are indexation (a share $\alpha_I$ of indexed debt does not
revalue) and foreign holding (a share $\alpha_F$ shifts domestic incidence);
because green disinflation makes the channel negative, both leakages work
in the government's favor. The maturity-adjusted shares, the foreign-incidence
table, the duration term for the accommodation move, and the
implementation-efficiency ($q_g$) sweep are reported in
Appendix~\ref{app:supp} (they leave the qualitative conclusions intact and are
supplementary to the incidence result).

% ==========================================================================
\FloatBarrier
\section{Transition dynamics}\label{sec:transitions}
% ==========================================================================

All quantitative results so far are \emph{steady-state} comparisons. This
section reports three complementary dynamic exercises, each described by
what it is and --- as importantly --- by what it is not. The first two are
\emph{Phillips-curve} exercises: a representative-agent New Keynesian
transition (Section~\ref{subsec:ranktrans}) and a set of linearized
heterogeneous-agent impulse responses (Section~\ref{subsec:hanktier1}).
Neither implements the demand-theory price-level mechanism --- in both,
inflation is determined by a New Keynesian Phillips curve and an
interest-rate rule, not by asset-market clearing of the nominal debt stock.
We report them because they answer two questions the steady-state analysis
cannot: does the announced program destabilize the nominal block on the way
to the new steady state, and how does the answer depend on the monetary
regime? Both exercises embed the same household and climate blocks as the
model of Section~\ref{sec:model} in a New Keynesian production economy;
that production economy is stated in full in
Appendix~\ref{app:nkmodel}, and the household block of the heterogeneous-agent
version is the incomplete-markets problem of
Section~\ref{subsec:households} augmented with an intratemporal labor
choice. The third exercise (Section~\ref{subsec:tier2}) is the paper's own
mechanism in dynamics --- the nonlinear heterogeneous-agent transition in
which the price-level \emph{path} is the unknown pinned by asset demand,
with no Phillips curve anywhere --- and its converged solution turns the
steady-state revaluation result into an announcement effect: the green
disinflation is front-loaded into the announcement year, and its inflation
response has the \emph{opposite sign} to the two Phillips-curve exercises'.
That opposite sign is the paper's \emph{model-class diagnostic}, not a
matched horse race: the first two exercises differ from the third in price
rigidity, financing rule, and persistence as well as in price determination,
so they bound transition shape and locate the sign. A matched
DTPL-versus-NK run --- same near-permanent path, same scale, same
balanced financing, labor margin shut, so that only the price-determination
block differs up to linearization and frequency --- is included in the
replication package and described, with its empirical counterpart, in
Section~\ref{sec:conclusion}; under it the signs remain opposite
(\matchedNKimpact\% versus \matchedDTPLimpact\% annualized).

\subsection{Representative-agent New Keynesian transitions}
\label{subsec:ranktrans}

A representative-agent New Keynesian economy with Rotemberg pricing (stated
in full in Appendix~\ref{app:nkmodel}) carries
the same climate block as the quantitative model (green public capital
$K_g$, carbon stock $X$, TFP damages $D$; identical parameters). A
permanent green-investment program of the calibrated steady-state scale
phases in linearly over twelve quarters (implementation delays in the
spirit of \citet{leeperwalkeryang2010}) and is financed \emph{contemporaneously by
taxes} under a debt-stabilizing fiscal rule --- by design, so that the
exercise isolates the real/nominal transition shape from the deficit
question studied everywhere else in the paper. Perfect-foresight paths
(300 quarters) are computed under four regimes: a weakly active rule
(\textsc{weak}: $\phi_\pi=1.1$, $\rho_i=0.5$, the closest numerically
regular stand-in for a peg), an inertial Taylor rule (\textsc{taylor}:
$\phi_\pi=1.5$, $\rho_i=0.8$), strict inflation targeting
(\textsc{aggressive}: $\phi_\pi=3$), and a temporary green accommodation
(\textsc{greenaccom}: Taylor plus a rate cut proportional to the
green-capital gap, $\psi_g=0.03$, fading as $K_g$ accumulates). The
accommodation is deliberately large: the un-smoothed stance target implies
a cut of about seven annualized percentage points at the program's start,
though the rule's own inertia ($\rho_i=0.8$) means the realized first-period
cut is nearer one and a half points, building thereafter.

Three findings, all verified by the converged runs (solver residuals
$\sim 10^{-14}$; the $K_g$ accumulation identity holds to $10^{-9}$ on
every reported path):

\begin{enumerate}
\item \emph{The tax-financed program is essentially price-stable under any
active rule.} Impact inflation is $-0.04$\% (annualized) under
\textsc{weak}, $-0.52$\% under \textsc{taylor}, $-0.24$\% under
\textsc{aggressive}; peak inflation along the entire transition stays
below $+1.6$\% annualized in all three. A ramped green program of
realistic scale is not, by itself, an inflationary event in the NK block.
\item \emph{The real green transition is monetary-regime independent.}
Green capital reaches $0.349$ (58\% of its terminal level) after 40
quarters and damages fall from $0.0972$ to $0.0925$ in all three active
regimes --- identical to three decimals. The monetary regime redistributes
the nominal path, not the green capital path.
\item \emph{Accommodation buys inflation, not green capital.} The large
\textsc{greenaccom} experiment produces a $+22.6$\% annualized
impact-inflation spike, yet after 40 quarters green capital is the same
($0.349$) and damages are slightly \emph{higher} ($0.0933$): the
demand boom raises output and hence emissions along the way. In the NK
block, tying rate cuts to the green-capital gap is a pure nominal
intervention --- a transition-dynamics counterpart of the steady-state
accommodation-neutrality result of Section~\ref{sec:quant}, and further
motivation for the anchor-insulation design of
Proposition~\ref{prop:insulation}.
\end{enumerate}

\pfigguard{PFig13_rank_transitions.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{PFig13_rank_transitions.pdf}
\caption{Representative-agent New Keynesian transitions: perfect-foresight
paths for a permanent tax-financed green program (12-quarter ramp-in) under
four monetary regimes --- a weakly active rule ($\phi_\pi=1.1$), an inertial
Taylor rule ($\phi_\pi=1.5$), strict inflation targeting ($\phi_\pi=3.0$),
and a temporary green accommodation whose large inflation spike is the
point of the experiment (accommodation buys inflation, not green capital).
These paths do not implement the demand-theory
price-level mechanism; inflation is set by the Phillips curve and the
interest-rate rule.}
\label{fig:pfig13}
\end{figure}}

\subsection{Linearized heterogeneous-agent impulse responses}
\label{subsec:hanktier1}

The second exercise restores household heterogeneity: a one-asset
heterogeneous-agent New Keynesian economy (borrowing constraint,
idiosyncratic efficiency risk) solved with sequence-space Jacobians,
carrying the same climate block, a \emph{nominal} policy rate together
with the ex-post Fisher equation --- so that surprise inflation revalues
household asset positions state by state, the linearized counterpart of
the paper's redistribution channel --- and a slow debt-stabilizing tax
rule, so that a quasi-permanent green-investment expansion (persistence
$0.98$, one percent of steady-state output) is \emph{deficit-financed on
impact}. Its household block is the incomplete-markets problem of
Section~\ref{subsec:households} with an intratemporal labor choice added,
and the New Keynesian production and policy blocks are shared with the
representative-agent economy; the complete system is in
Appendix~\ref{app:nkmodel}. Discount factor and labor disutility are
calibrated to clear the asset and labor markets at a damaged steady state
with debt at the calibration target of Section~\ref{sec:quant}; the income
process is a three-state benchmark rather than the seven-state process of
the quantitative model, so magnitudes here are indicative, not calibrated
results.

Its role is deliberately limited: it bridges the representative-agent
diagnostics and the full nonlinear transition, showing that the qualitative
transition shapes survive heterogeneity and Fisher redistribution. It does
not --- and in a linearized New Keynesian setting cannot --- deliver
price-level determination by asset demand.
Figure~\ref{fig:pfig14} reports the responses of output, inflation, real
debt, green capital, damages, and the ex-post real return under four
monetary regimes analogous to the representative-agent block's, though not
identical to them: the heterogeneous-agent rules omit interest-rate inertia
and the accommodation keys off the program flow rather than the
green-capital gap, so the \textsc{greenaccom} experiment here is far milder
than its representative-agent namesake and the two are compared on shapes,
not on levels.

The verified run (five regimes solved; calibrated $\beta = 0.9796$;
one-percent-of-output program; persistence $\rho_g = 0.98$) delivers the
exercise's key message in two comparisons. \emph{Across monetary rules},
impact inflation is $+2.53\%$ annualized under \textsc{weak}, $+0.44\%$
under \textsc{taylor}, $+0.10\%$ under \textsc{aggressive}, $+0.65\%$
under \textsc{greenaccom}, with output up half a percent of its
steady-state level on impact ($+0.0044$ in levels against $\bar Y =
0.90$) --- positive throughout, against impact deflation in the
tax-financed, ramped RANK experiment of Section~\ref{subsec:ranktrans}
--- and the real green path is again rule-independent (green capital
$+0.148$, damages $-0.0032$ after 40 quarters, identical to three
decimals across all regimes). \emph{Within the Taylor rule}, a fifth
regime (\textsc{taylorbal}) re-runs the identical program with a fast
debt-stabilizing tax rule in place of the slow one, isolating the
deficit-financing component while holding the model fixed: real debt
after 40 quarters rises $+0.007$ instead of $+0.071$ --- the debt buildup
is almost entirely a financing choice --- yet impact inflation barely
moves ($+0.36\%$ against $+0.44\%$). The
decomposition is sharp: in the linearized HANK, \emph{deficit financing
buys the debt path, not the inflation path} --- the mild impact inflation
belongs to the unanticipated program itself, its size is set by the
monetary rule, and the financing margin prices into the fisc's balance
sheet rather than into goods prices. (The steady-state DTPL analysis is
where the financing margin does move the price level --- through
asset-demand incidence, Section~\ref{subsec:safeasset} --- which is
precisely the mechanism a linearized NK Phillips curve cannot represent;
the contrast between the two exercises is thus itself informative.) These
magnitudes are indicative, not calibrated results: the income process is a
three-state benchmark and the responses are linearized.

\pfigguard{PFig14_hank_green_irfs.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{PFig14_hank_green_irfs.pdf}
\caption{Linearized heterogeneous-agent impulse responses to a
quasi-permanent deficit-financed green-investment shock (one percent of
steady-state output, persistence $0.98$) under five monetary/financing
regimes: three inflation-coefficient variants ($\phi_\pi = 1.1$, $1.5$,
$3.0$), a temporary green accommodation, and the Taylor rule under
near-balanced-budget financing (\textsc{taylorbal}), whose gap to
\textsc{taylor} isolates the deficit-financing component.
The ex-post real return panel is the linearized Fisher revaluation
channel. These are linearized responses, not the nonlinear
demand-theory price-level transition.}
\label{fig:pfig14}
\end{figure}}

\paragraph{A two-asset extension.} The replication package also implements a
linearized \emph{two-asset} version of this exercise --- liquid nominal bonds
versus illiquid equity, with a constant liquidity premium --- so that the
demand for the liquid nominal asset, the $B/P$ margin at the heart of the model,
is observable separately from total wealth. It confirms the one-asset findings
under the richer portfolio and shows the Fisher revaluation landing specifically
on households' liquid nominal savings; but it is a linearized New Keynesian
exercise with a constant premium, not a nonlinear DTPL fixed point with an
endogenous convenience yield, so it locates the portfolio margin without
validating its magnitude. The full results, table, and the accuracy
qualifications are in Appendix~\ref{subsec:twoasset}.

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{The nonlinear price-level transition}\label{subsec:tier2}

The two exercises above borrow their inflation dynamics from a Phillips
curve. The paper's own mechanism makes a starker prediction about the
transition, and we compute it directly. At $t=1$ the government unexpectedly announces
the permanent program; thereafter foresight is perfect. The unknown is
the \emph{price-level path} $\{P_t\}_{t=1}^{T}$: at every date the price
level must clear the asset market,
\[
S_t\bigl(\{r_s, \tau_s, D_s\}_{s \ge t};\, V_T,\, \Omega_t\bigr)
\;=\; \frac{B_t}{P_t}, \qquad
1 + r_t = (1+i^{ss})\,\frac{P_{t-1}}{P_t},
\]
where $S_t$ is exact aggregate asset demand from the household problem
solved \emph{backward} from the green terminal steady state under the
time-varying taxes, damages, and realized real returns implied by the
trial price path, and the wealth distribution $\Omega_t$ rolls
\emph{forward} from the pre-announcement stationary distribution. There
is no Phillips curve and no policy rule for inflation anywhere in this
computation: the inflation path is the repricing of the nominal
debt stock against precautionary asset demand, period by period, and the
$t=1$ jump of $P_1$ against the pre-announcement price level is the
surprise revaluation of outstanding nominal positions --- the paper's
channel realized along a path rather than compared across steady states.
Stationarizing by the nominal-growth trend $(1+\mu)^t$ makes the system
autonomous. We detrend the price level and the nominal stock by the
same factor: $\hat P_t\equiv P_t/(1+\mu)^t$ and $\hat B_t\equiv
B_t/(1+\mu)^t$. Because nominal debt grows exactly at trend under the service
rule (equation~\eqref{eq:nomtrend} below), the detrended stock is
constant, $\hat B_t=B_0$, so the stationarized market-clearing condition
is $\Sfun_t=\hat B_t/\hat P_t=B_0/\hat P_t$ --- both sides detrended, no mixed
units. The terminal condition pins $\hat{P}_T$ at the green
steady-state price level, and the government budget holds by construction
at every trial path.

\paragraph{The dynamic nominal budget, made explicit.} We write the
transition accounting out in full because the announcement-year numbers turn
on a distinction a casual reading misses: under the benchmark service rule
the announcement does not deficit-finance the program. The government's nominal flow identity is
\begin{equation}
B_t = (1+i^{ss})\,B_{t-1} + P_t\,\bigl(g_{g,t}-\tau_t\bigr),
\label{eq:nombudget}
\end{equation}
predetermined coupon on inherited nominal debt plus the nominal primary
balance. Dividing by $P_t$ and using the realized real return
$1+r_t=(1+i^{ss})P_{t-1}/P_t$ gives the real recursion
$b_t = (1+r_t)\,b_{t-1} + g_{g,t}-\tau_t$. Now impose the benchmark service
rule $\tau_t=r^{ss}b_t+g_{g,t}$: the spending term \emph{cancels}, and the
recursion collapses to
\begin{equation}
(1+r^{ss})\,b_t = (1+r_t)\,b_{t-1},
\qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
B_t = (1+\mu)\,B_{t-1}.
\label{eq:nomtrend}
\end{equation}
Two facts follow. First,
under the service rule nominal debt grows \emph{only at the trend $\mu$}: there
is \emph{no primary deficit}, because taxes cover contemporaneous spending plus
steady-state service exactly ($g_{g,t}-\tau_t = -r^{ss}b_t <0$, a primary
surplus). Second, real debt nonetheless moves, $b_t-b_{t-1}\neq0$, but
\eqref{eq:nomtrend} shows the mover is the \emph{ex-post return gap}
$r_t-r^{ss}$, not taxes set below spending --- it is a \emph{valuation} effect,
the announced disinflation ($P$ below trend) marking the predetermined nominal
stock up in real terms. So the transitional ``deficit'' in the benchmark is
purely the bondholder-revaluation channel: (i) the surprise revaluation at
$t=1$, worth $(P_0/P_1-1)\,b_0$ in real terms, and (ii) its continuation as
$r_t-r^{ss}$ decays back to zero --- both valuation, neither a primary deficit.
A \emph{genuine} primary deficit (a positive primary gap $g_{g,t}-\tau_t>0$,
i.e.\ $\tau_t$ set below the service level $r^{ss}b_t+g_{g,t}$, so that
\eqref{eq:nombudget} issues nominal debt above trend) is a
distinct financing choice: it is the low-$\phi_b$ debt-stabilization lever of
the Phillips-curve exercises (Appendix~\ref{app:nkmodel}), not the benchmark
DTPL transition. Writing \eqref{eq:nombudget}--\eqref{eq:nomtrend} out is what
licenses reading the announcement-year result below as revaluation, not as a
tax-timing deficit. The solver is an
Anderson-accelerated fixed point on the log price path: the underlying map
moves each $\hat P_t$ toward $\hat B_t/\Sfun_t=B_0/\Sfun_t$ (excess asset
demand at $t$
lowers $\hat P_t$, raising the real value $\hat B_t/\hat P_t$ of the
detrended nominal stock until it meets demand --- the transition form of
$P^{*}=B/\Sfun$), and because a change in $\hat P_t$ moves the realized real
return at dates $t$ and $t+1$ with opposite signs --- a cross-date
coupling under which plain diagonal relaxation stalls --- the update
combines the most recent residuals by least squares (type-II Anderson,
memory five), with a per-iteration trust region and the market-clearing
residuals reported at every date. Convergence is assessed on the
\emph{free} unknowns $\hat P_1,\dots,\hat P_{T-1}$; the pinned terminal
date's residual is reported separately as a horizon-adequacy check --- it
vanishes only when $T$ is long enough for the wealth distribution to
reach the green steady state, at which point green-steady-state savings
equal $B_T/P_T$ by construction. A path that is not both converged and
horizon-adequate is returned as such and cannot be presented as a result.
Each iteration costs $T$ exact Bellman steps plus $T$
distribution pushes on the full $(a,e)$ grids --- deliberately the
computationally heaviest object in the package.

\paragraph{Cross-check and a sequence-space determinacy diagnostic.} Because
the announcement result is the paper's sharpest dynamic claim, we solve it a
second, independent way and compare. The alternative is a Newton iteration in
\emph{sequence space}: we form the general-equilibrium Jacobian
$J_{ts}=\partial\,[\,\Sfun_t-\hat B_t/\hat P_t\,]/\partial\ln\hat P_s$ (with
$\hat B_t=B_0$ the detrended stock) of the
market-clearing residual along the path --- the sequence-space Jacobian of
\citet{auclertetal2021}, here computed directly, since the aggregate unknown is
a scalar per date --- and step $\ln\hat P \leftarrow \ln\hat P - J^{-1}H$. The
first Newton step from the log-linear bridge reproduces the model's linear
impulse response exactly, a built-in correctness gate, and the two solvers are
built on two \emph{independent} residual implementations, so their agreement is
a genuine cross-validation rather than a shared-code tautology. The same
Jacobian yields a \emph{dynamic} determinacy diagnostic that complements the
steady-state elasticity test of Definition~\ref{def:elasticity}: the transition
is locally determinate where $J$ is well conditioned (smallest singular value
bounded away from zero), and a near-singular $J$ is the sequence-space
signature of the flat-demand multiplicity region of
Proposition~\ref{prop:sunspots}. Both the cross-check and the diagnostic are
produced by the replication driver \texttt{verify\_transition\_ssj}; no
announcement number is quoted unless the two solvers agree. Agreement is
assessed at the precision the discretization supports: because both solvers
share a discrete-choice ($n_a=500$) household problem, whose market-clearing
residual has a floor near the grid resolution, the sequence-space Newton is
run to that floor (interior residual $\sim2\times10^{-3}$, the same gate the
Anderson solve meets) and the two price paths are required to agree to
within one percent --- point agreement to the grid floor, not to machine
precision, which the reported announcement objects (impact sign, magnitude
to two digits, front-loading share) easily clear. Two experiments run by
default: the \emph{nominal appropriation} (the anchor-exposed design) and
the \emph{indexed mandate} (the anchor-insulated design of
Proposition~\ref{prop:insulation}); the comparison of their announcement
jumps is the dynamic version of the anchor-insulation result, and the
model's central untested prediction --- that a credible green
announcement produces \emph{disinflation on impact} under lump-sum
finance --- becomes a computed, sign-testable object.

The converged run ($n_a = 500$, $T = 80$ years, medium damages, $\beta$
calibrated on this same grid so that pre-announcement real debt matches
the $1.10$ target; interior market-clearing residual below $5\times10^{-4}$
at every free date, terminal residual below $5\times10^{-4}$, $88$ and
$72$ Anderson iterations for the two designs) delivers the paper's
dynamics in three statements. Like every repricing in the paper, the
\emph{levels} here are one-asset upper bounds: scaled by the liquid
nominal-liability share $\omega\approx0.15$--$0.25$ of
Section~\ref{subsec:safeasset}, the $3.9\%$ impact repricing below reads as
roughly $0.6$--$1.0\%$, while the \emph{shares} (the front-loading fraction,
the sign, the timing) are scale-free. \emph{First, green disinflation is
front-loaded:} the announcement moves the stationarized price level down
$3.9\%$ on impact --- year-one inflation of $-2.0\%$ against the $+2\%$
nominal trend --- which is $77\%$ of the entire long-run green
disinflation ($-5.1\%$ across steady states) realized in the announcement
year, before the first unit of green capital is installed. The price
level jumps because asset demand does: households capitalize the future
damage reduction and the transition-long tax path immediately, and the
price level is what clears that market. Inflation is back within a
quarter-point of trend by year two --- and stays there --- while green
capital accumulates over decades: the announcement effect and the
accumulation dynamics separate cleanly.
\emph{Second, the revaluation channel is a one-time announcement windfall:}
the surprise repricing hands holders of the outstanding nominal stock a
real gain of $5.3\%$ of the program's present value ($5.3\%$ under the
indexed mandate) --- the dynamic realization of the negative
$\nu_{\text{reval}}$ of the steady-state decomposition, collected entirely
in the announcement year. \emph{Third, the denomination of the green
budget is irrelevant on the equilibrium path:} the nominal-appropriation
and indexed-mandate designs produce announcement jumps within two
hundredths of a percentage point of each other and land on the same
terminal steady state, so the anchor-insulation value of the indexed
mandate (Proposition~\ref{prop:insulation}) is entirely
\emph{off}-equilibrium --- it removes the self-fulfilling brown-stagnation
equilibria without changing the equilibrium the economy actually follows.
The contrast with the Phillips-curve exercises is the point of this
computation: the linearized heterogeneous-agent exercise prices a
comparable deficit-financed green announcement (a quasi-permanent program of
one percent of output) at $+0.4\%$ impact inflation (the demand stimulus),
while the demand-theory computation prices its permanent program at
$-2.0\%$ (the asset-demand shift) --- opposite signs for the same class of
announcement, because the
two exercises answer with different mechanisms, and only the latter
contains the price-level-as-market-clearing margin the paper is about.
One caution bounds the contrast: the two computations differ
not only in the price-determination margin but also in rigidities,
financing detail, and shock scale, so the sign contrast is a statement
about \emph{mechanisms within each model class} --- a model-contingent
diagnostic, not a clean empirical sign test. An empirical implementation
would have to hold those environmental differences fixed (the
high-frequency design sketched in the conclusion) before the
announcement-window sign can discriminate between the two views. This
transition is the converged, horizon-adequate, grid-consistent run
described in Appendix~\ref{app:numerics}.

\paragraph{Transition-inclusive welfare.} The converged path also settles
a question the steady-state incidence of Section~\ref{subsec:welfaregroups}
cannot: since the program's costs are front-loaded (taxes rise at the
announcement) while its benefits accrue over decades (damages fall as
green capital builds), does the transition soften or deepen the
steady-state welfare verdict? We compute, household by household, the
consumption-equivalent transfer equating the value of remaining in the
no-program steady state forever with the value of \emph{entering date one
of the announced path} --- including the announcement-date revaluation ---
aggregated over the same baseline wealth groups (the steady-state column
reproduces Table~\ref{tab:incidence}'s medium row on the transition grids,
a consistency check on the whole apparatus). The answer is that the
transition \emph{deepens the regressivity} of lump-sum finance: the poorest
quintile's loss grows from $-2.6\%$ to $-3.4\%$ and the constrained
group's from $-2.6\%$ to $-3.4\%$, while the top decile's loss
\emph{shrinks} from $-1.0\%$ to $-0.5\%$ --- the announcement windfall
accrues at the top of the wealth distribution while front-loaded taxes
bite at the bottom before any damage relief arrives (aggregate:
$-1.75\%$ to $-2.1\%$). The numbers are within two hundredths of a
percentage point across the nominal and indexed designs, as the on-path
irrelevance of denomination requires. The steady-state comparison
therefore \emph{understates} who pays for the lump-sum-financed green
program, and the case for the medium-damage program cannot rest on
transition gains --- there are none under this financing.

The levy-plus-rebate design of Section~\ref{subsec:regimes}, solved along
the same announced path, delivers the mirror image on every margin. The
announcement now reprices the nominal stock \emph{against} bondholders:
the stationarized price level jumps $+12.8\%$ in the announcement year
(a one-asset upper bound; $\omega$-scaled, roughly $2$--$3\%$; year-one
inflation $+15.1\%$ against the $+2\%$ trend, back within a
quarter-point of trend by year four), handing the fisc a one-time capital
levy worth $17.2\%$ of the program's present value --- the dynamic
realization of the design's positive $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$, and the
opposite sign of the lump-sum design's bondholder windfall. And the
progressivity survives the path: transition-inclusive, the bottom half
still \emph{gains} ($+0.4\%$; the poorest quintile $+1.0\%$, the
constrained $+1.0\%$) while the top decile bears $-2.2\%$ through the
announcement levy, and the utilitarian aggregate improves from the lump-sum
design's $-2.1\%$ to $-0.4\%$. One qualification: along the path
the two designs are no longer near-Pareto-ranked --- the top decile
strictly prefers lump-sum finance ($-0.5\%$ against $-2.2\%$) --- so the
choice between them is a genuine distributional and political choice, not
a free lunch, which is precisely the political-economy tradeoff the
conclusion draws out. A second caution: an announcement-year repricing of
this size is, economically, a one-time capital levy, and the flexible-price
model has no vocabulary for the stabilization and credibility questions it
raises --- households must believe the levy is once-and-for-all rather than
the first draw from a repeated policy (the commitment problem of
\citealp{hurtado2023sovereign}). We therefore read the rebate path as the
mechanism's exact accounting of who pays when, not as a recommendation
about the speed at which the repricing should be allowed to happen. The
same scoping applies to this section as a whole: every path is a
\emph{perfect-foresight accounting benchmark} under a fully credible,
once-and-for-all announcement. Partial credibility, reversal risk, or
finite planning horizons would spread the announcement-day capitalization
over the horizon at which beliefs converge --- the terminal steady states,
and with them the stationary incidence accounting, are unchanged --- but
modeling that attenuation requires a belief block the flexible-price
economy does not have, so we state it as the boundary of what the
transition results claim rather than asserting robustness to it.

\pfigguard{PFig18_dtpl_transition.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{PFig18_dtpl_transition.pdf}
\caption{The nonlinear price-level transition (converged, grid-consistent
run, $n_a=500$, $T=80$ years, $\beta$ calibrated on this grid to the
$1.10$ debt target). (a) The stationarized price level and (b) inflation
for three designs: the nominal-appropriation and indexed-mandate
lump-sum designs (both hold the balanced service rule
$\tau_t=r^{ss}b_t+g_{g,t}$, so nominal debt grows only at trend and the debt
path is revaluation, not a primary deficit), and the levy-plus-rebate design
of Section~\ref{subsec:regimes};
(c)--(d) the green capital stock and damages, whose slow accumulation the
announcement jump contrasts with. The lump-sum announcement jump
(the $-3.9\%$ price-level drop between the pre-announcement level and year
one) front-loads $77\%$ of the long-run green disinflation, and the two
denomination designs are nearly indistinguishable on the equilibrium path;
the rebate design reprices with the \emph{opposite} sign ($+12.8\%$ in the
announcement year, a one-time capital levy on bondholders), realizing its
positive $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$ dynamically. Inflation is within a
quarter-point of the $2\%$ trend by year two (lump-sum) and year four
(rebate). Market-clearing residuals, reported in the transition summary of
the replication package, clear to $5\times10^{-4}$ at every date.}
\label{fig:pfig18}
\end{figure}}

% ==========================================================================
\FloatBarrier
\section{Conclusion}\label{sec:conclusion}
% ==========================================================================

Who pays for green public investment? In an economy in which the price
level clears the market for precautionary savings, the answer is set by
financing design, not by the program alone. Under lump-sum finance a
productive green program is \emph{disinflationary}: financing pushes
constraint-adjacent households toward precautionary saving, aggregate asset
demand rises, and the revaluation of the nominal debt stock hands
bondholders a windfall --- so households at the borrowing limit pay twice,
through the tax and through the regressive incidence of remaining damages,
while the top of the wealth distribution collects the price-level gain.
Replacing lump-sum finance with a proportional levy plus rebate flips the
sign of the revaluation channel and converts the same nominal appropriation
into a progressive program. Who pays is therefore a fiscal design choice
--- which is why ``does the green transition cause inflation?'' has no
answer independent of ``who pays for it?''

How much of the cost comes back is a climate-damage question, not a
constant, and the fiscal arithmetic is neither the FTPL's nor the New
Keynesian one. A permanent appropriation, serviced by taxes against a
nominal debt stock, returns to households a net-burden share that runs,
along the disciplined frontier, from a minority under conservative DICE
damages to more than the full cost under high-damage estimates; the
specific calibrated values (e.g.\ $\nu\approx0.6$ at the medium column) are
\emph{points on that frontier under an illustrative abatement technology},
not trained policy magnitudes --- the policy-relevant statement is the
conditional frontier, not a single number. The return runs in net-burden
terms through the damage dividend rather than the inflation tax, and
becomes strictly fiscal only once a tax base converts the income gain into
revenue.

What the government can choose is correspondingly narrow. Nominal debt
issuance is neutral, real debt is demand-determined, and there is no
stationary deficit to finance: the instruments are the tax schedule and its
incidence, the real rate, and the real scale of the program --- not the
real debt level. Steady-state inflation is exactly neutral at a fixed real
rate, so the ``optimal accommodation'' of $4.5\%$ is an optimal-real-rate
result, the \citet{aiyagarimcgrattan1998} optimum-quantity-of-debt force in
disguise, not an inflation prescription. The denomination of the green
budget, by contrast, is a genuine determinacy instrument: nominal
appropriations \emph{can} expose the economy to self-fulfilling brown
stagnation when damage incidence is regressive enough --- a conditional
region the calibrated benchmark does not occupy --- while indexed mandates
insulate the nominal anchor at no fiscal cost. Against the quantitatively
binding failure mode, fiscal-space collapse, the effective instrument is
accommodation, not indexation.

The dynamics reinforce these conclusions rather than softening them. The
representative-agent and linearized heterogeneous-agent diagnostics of
Section~\ref{sec:transitions} indicate that the announced program does not
destabilize the nominal block on the way to the new steady state; the
nonlinear transition in which the price-level path is itself pinned by
asset demand --- computed in Section~\ref{subsec:tier2} as a converged
fixed point with no Phillips curve --- front-loads three quarters of the
green disinflation, and the entire bondholder windfall, into the
announcement year, so the transition-inclusive welfare incidence is more
regressive than the steady-state comparison. A two-state aggregate climate
process (Section~\ref{subsec:aggrisk}) makes the nominal safe asset
literally risky and shows that green investment compresses that systematic
risk; the compression proves fiscally inert but valuable as insurance, most
so in the disaster state.

The distributional finding carries a political-economy implication worth
stating plainly. In this economy the fiscal beneficiaries of the
lump-sum-financed green program are bondholders --- wealthy households
receive the disinflationary windfall while the lump-sum burden bites
hardest at the borrowing constraint --- so the usual political alignment
inverts: the constituency with the strongest fiscal stake in the
lump-sum-financed program is the top of the wealth distribution, and
opposition motivated
by ``green inflation'' targets a program that, through this mechanism, is
deflationary for the asset-owning class. Models of the politics of climate
policy that assume the rich resist green transition spending
because inflation erodes nominal wealth have the sign of the incentive
backwards here; conversely, the levy-plus-rebate design realigns the
program with the median voter at the cost of the bondholder windfall ---
a tradeoff between political coalitions, not just welfare weights, and one
the computed transition prices exactly (along the announced path the top
decile prefers lump-sum finance by $1.7$ percentage points of permanent
consumption while the bottom half prefers the rebate by $3.2$).

One reinterpretation sharpens the whole exercise. Read as mitigation, the
green capital is a national instrument aimed at a global externality, so
$K_g$ stands in for a coordinated share; read as public \emph{adaptation}
capital --- sea walls, hardened infrastructure, resilient public systems ---
the mapping from a country's own spending to its own damages is exact, and
the productive-public-capital margin the endowment economy suppresses becomes
defensible \citep{fried2022seawalls,konradtweder2023}. The adaptation reading
is the more literal home for the closed-economy structure, and taking it
seriously --- with public capital that both hardens the economy and enters
production --- is the cleanest way to close the revenue channel this paper
can only bound. Adding production with brown
capital and a carbon price \citep{barrage2020}, an endogenous convenience
yield on the nominal liability (the portfolio margin the two-asset
extension holds constant), and a fully stochastic
transition are the other main open modeling items. The sharpest untested
implication is a \emph{model-class diagnostic}: the demand-theory mechanism
and a New Keynesian economy make \emph{opposite-signed} predictions for the
inflation response to a credible, deficit-financed green-investment
announcement --- the former disinflationary (nominal debt revalues up as
precautionary demand rises), the latter inflationary (a demand expansion under
sticky prices). The comparison should not be overclaimed: our standard
Phillips-curve exercises (Appendix~\ref{app:nkmodel}) differ from the
demand-theory transition not only in price determination but in price
rigidity, financing rule, and shock persistence, so they diagnose transition
shape, not a clean sign horse race. The replication package therefore
includes a \emph{matched} DTPL-versus-NK experiment
(\texttt{run\_matched\_dtpl\_nk}): the NK-HANK of Appendix~\ref{app:nkhank}
re-run with a near-permanent program at the DTPL scale, balanced
(service-rule-speed) financing, no accommodation, and the labor margin shut
--- so that, up to linearization and frequency, only the price-determination
block differs. The matched run delivers an annualized announcement-window
inflation impact of \matchedNKimpact\% in the NK economy against
\matchedDTPLimpact\% in the DTPL transition: the signs remain opposite
under the matched design, so the contrast is attributable to the
price-determination block itself, up to linearization and frequency. The
complementary test is empirical: high-frequency identification of the
market-implied-inflation response to green-fiscal surprises. The sign is the
object; the current confounded reduced-form record cannot yet adjudicate it.
