\appendix
% ==========================================================================
\FloatBarrier
\section{Proofs}\label{app:proofs}

\subsection{Proof of Lemma \ref{lem:S}}
Feasibility: at $(a,e)=(-\bar a, e_{\min})$ maximal consumption is
$y(e_{\min};D)-\tau-r\bar a>0$ on the stated set, so the constraint set
is nonempty and compact at every state; with $\beta<1$ and $u$ bounded above
on compacts (after the usual truncation of the asset grid at
$\bar A<\infty$, valid since $\beta(1+r)<1$ implies a finite buffer-stock
target), the Bellman operator is a contraction and $V$, the policies, and the
invariant distribution exist; continuity in $(\tau,D)$ follows from the
theorem of the maximum and the continuity of the (finite-state) invariant
distribution in the policy. Scaling: let $\lambda=1-D>0$ and scale
$(a,\tau)\mapsto(\lambda a,\lambda\tilde\tau)$ with
$\tilde\tau=\tau/(1-D)$. The budget set scales linearly \emph{and the
borrowing constraint maps to itself} precisely when $\bar a=0$ (then
$a'\ge0\Leftrightarrow\lambda a'\ge0$) or when $\bar a$ scales with $1-D$;
for a fixed positive $\bar a$ the constraint becomes $-\bar a/(1-D)$ and the
homogeneity is only approximate. Under the stated condition, with CRRA
$u(\lambda c)=\lambda^{1-\sigma}u(c)+\text{const}$, so
$V_\lambda(\lambda a,e)=\lambda^{1-\sigma}V_1(a,e)+\text{const}$ and the
optimal policy satisfies $a'_\lambda(\lambda a,e)=\lambda a'_1(a,e)$. The
invariant distribution over rescaled assets is the pushforward of the
original, hence aggregate assets scale by $\lambda$, which is
\eqref{eq:scaling}. With $\psi>0$ or $\phi_D>0$ the endowment process itself
depends on $D$ and the argument gives only the decomposition into level and
risk components. \qed

\subsection{Proof of Lemma \ref{lem:climate}}
Write the right side of \eqref{eq:climatefixedpoint} as $T(D)$ on
$[0,D_{\max}]$. $T$ is continuous, $T(0)>0$, $T(D_{\max})<D_{\max}$, and
$T'(D) = -D_{\max}e^{-\gamma_x X(D)}\gamma_x \varepsilon_0
(1-\alpha_A A)/\delta_x \cdot(-1) \cdot(-1) <0$... computing directly:
$X(D)=\varepsilon_0(1-\alpha_A A)(1-D)/\delta_x$ is decreasing in $D$, and
$D\mapsto D_{\max}(1-e^{-\gamma_x X})$ is increasing in $X$; hence $T$ is
\emph{decreasing} in $D$. A continuous decreasing self-map of an interval has
exactly one fixed point. Comparative statics: $g_g\uparrow \Rightarrow
K_g\uparrow \Rightarrow A\uparrow \Rightarrow X(\cdot)\downarrow \Rightarrow
T(\cdot)\downarrow$ pointwise, so the fixed point falls; strict when
$\theta_g\alpha_A>0$. Under the nominal regime $g_g=G_g/P$ is strictly
decreasing in $P$, delivering the stated monotonicity. \qed

\subsection{Proof of Lemma \ref{lem:anchor}}
Constant real debt $b=B_t/P_t$ across stationary periods forces
$P_{t+1}/P_t=B_{t+1}/B_t=1+\mu$; the Fisher equation then gives
$1+r^{ss}=(1+i^{ss})/(1+\mu)$. \qed

\subsection{Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:determinacy}}
(i) $\Phi$ is continuous on the feasibility set (Lemma~\ref{lem:S};
$D$ constant here). We use two maintained regularity conditions, both
properties of the incomplete-markets calibration rather than implications of
Assumption~\ref{ass:feasible} alone, and we state them as such: \textbf{(R1)}
\emph{strictly positive limiting asset demand}, $\Sfun(1+r^{ss};\tau,D)>0$ at
the relevant limiting tax --- with a binding precautionary motive and
$\beta(1+r^{ss})<1$ the buffer-stock target is strictly positive; and
\textbf{(R2)} \emph{feasibility of the limiting tax}, i.e.\ it lies strictly
below the poorest household's resources. We give the boundary argument for both
spending regimes.
\emph{Nominal appropriation} ($g_g=G_g/P$, so $\tau(P)=(r^{ss}B+G_g)/P$).
High-$P$ boundary: $\tau(P)\to0$, so by (R1)
$\Sfun\to\Sfun(1+r^{ss};0,D)>0$, $B/P\to0$, and $\Phi>0$. \emph{Indexed real
mandate} ($g_g=\bar g_g$ fixed, so $\tau(P)=r^{ss}B/P+\bar g_g$). High-$P$
boundary: $\tau(P)\to\bar g_g$ (not $0$), so by (R1)
$\Sfun\to\Sfun(1+r^{ss};\bar g_g,D)>0$, while $B/P\to0$, so $\Phi>0$ again ---
requiring (R2) at the constant tax $\bar g_g$, i.e.\ $\bar g_g<\min_e y(e;D)$,
which holds since $\bar g_g$ is a small fraction of income. On the
\emph{negative-real-rate} case ($r^{ss}<0$, relevant to the
optimal-accommodation region of Section~\ref{subsec:optmu}): the
interest-service term $r^{ss}B/P$ is then negative and helps
feasibility as $P$ falls (it lowers the required tax), so (R2) is easier to meet
at finite $P$; the high-$P$ limit is unchanged since $r^{ss}B/P\to0$ regardless
of the sign of $r^{ss}$.
In both regimes the low-$P$ boundary has the same structure:
$\Sfun$ remains bounded (buffer-stock target finite under $\beta(1+r)<1$),
so $\Phi=\Sfun-B/P<0$ \emph{provided} the feasibility set reaches a price
low enough that $B/P$ exceeds that demand bound --- the crossing condition
in the proposition. This is where existence can fail: as $P$ falls,
$\tau(P)$ rises (as $(r^{ss}B+G_g)/P$ under the appropriation, as
$r^{ss}B/P+\bar g_g$ under the mandate), and if it crosses the feasibility edge
$\min_e y(e;D)-r^{ss}\bar a$ before $B/P$ overtakes $\Sfun$, there is no
$P$ at which $\Phi\le0$ and no equilibrium exists (the collapse of
Section~\ref{subsec:sunspots}); the mandate shifts this edge inward by the
additive $\bar g_g$ but leaves the crossing structure intact. Under the crossing
condition, the intermediate value theorem yields a zero between the two
boundaries. (ii) Where $\varepsilon_S>-1$,
$d\Phi/dP=(\Sfun/P)(\varepsilon_S+1)>0$ at any zero: all crossings are upward,
and a continuous function with only upward crossings has exactly one.
(iii) $(K_g,X,D)$ solve \eqref{eq:Kg}--\eqref{eq:climatefixedpoint} given
$g_g$; with $\theta_g=0$ or the mandate, $D$ does not vary with $P$, so the
block is recursive and does not enter $\Phi$'s dependence on $P$. \qed

\subsection{Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:suffstat}}
Write $\Phi(P,g)=\Sfun(P,g)-B/P$ with $\Sfun$ the composite
\eqref{eq:Scomposite}. At the crossing $\Sfun(P^{*},g)=B/P^{*}$, so
\[
\Phi_P
=\Sfun_P+\frac{B}{P^{*2}}
=\frac{\Sfun}{P^{*}}\Big(\frac{P^{*}}{\Sfun}\Sfun_P+1\Big)
=\frac{\Sfun}{P^{*}}\,\big(\varepsilon_S+1\big),
\]
which is nonzero exactly when $\varepsilon_S\neq-1$. The implicit function
theorem then delivers a locally unique differentiable branch $P^{*}(g)$ with
\[
\frac{dP^{*}}{dg}=-\frac{\Phi_g}{\Phi_P}
=-\frac{\Sfun_g}{(\Sfun/P^{*})(1+\varepsilon_S)}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\frac{d\ln P^{*}}{dg}=-\frac{\eta_g}{1+\varepsilon_S},
\qquad \eta_g=\frac{\Sfun_g}{\Sfun}.
\]
The channel decomposition of $\eta_g$ is the chain rule applied to
\eqref{eq:Scomposite} at a fixed price level. Part (i) is immediate since
$1+\varepsilon_S>0$ there; part (ii) reads off the denominator, whose zero is
the tangency at which the upward crossing of
Proposition~\ref{prop:determinacy} degenerates and beyond which the downward
crossing of Proposition~\ref{prop:sunspots} exists. On differentiability: $\Sfun$ aggregates
household policies over the invariant distribution, so borrowing-constraint
kinks at the household level are integrated over a nonatomic distribution of
states; the maintained hypothesis is differentiability of the aggregate at
the crossing --- the same regularity (R1)--(R2) under which
Proposition~\ref{prop:determinacy}(ii) operates --- verified numerically at
every reported equilibrium, where the measured $\varepsilon_S$ is far from
$-1$ (Section~\ref{subsec:sunspots}). \qed

\subsection{Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:selffinancing} and
Result \ref{res:disinflation}}
(i) is Definition~\ref{def:nu} plus the budget identity. (ii) The net burden
is $N=\tau+D$ with $\tau=r^{ss}B/P+g_g$. Under the nominal budget $g_g=G_g/P$,
so $dg_g = dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}} - g_g\,d\ln P$ with
$dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}}=dG_g/P$; and $dD=-\kappa\,dg_g$. Differentiating
$\tau$ gives $d\tau = -(r^{ss}B/P)\,d\ln P + dg_g$, so
\[
dN = d\tau + dD = (1-\kappa)\,dg_g - (r^{ss}B/P)\,d\ln P.
\]
Substituting $dg_g = dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}} - g_g\,d\ln P$ and using
$r^{ss}B/P = \tau - g_g$,
\[
dN = (1-\kappa)\,dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}}
   - \big[(r^{ss}B/P)+(1-\kappa)g_g\big]\,d\ln P
   = (1-\kappa)\,dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}} - (\tau-\kappa g_g)\,d\ln P .
\]
This is the \emph{direct-change} form: the price-response coefficient is
$\tau-\kappa g_g$ when $d\ln P$ is paired with the direct change
$dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}}=dG_g/P$ (first term $(1-\kappa)\,dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}}$).
Under the equilibrium convention of the main text (with $\epsilon_P\equiv d\ln
P/dg_g$ and $dg_g$ the equilibrium change in $g_g=G_g/P$) one instead pairs
$d\ln P$ with $dg_g$: differentiating $N=r^{ss}B/P+g_g+D$ directly in the
equilibrium $g_g$ gives $dN=(1-\kappa)\,dg_g-(\tau-g_g)\,d\ln P$, coefficient
$\tau-g_g$ (first term $(1-\kappa)\,dg_g$). The two forms are the same
differential written in the two $dg_g$ conventions, which differ by
$dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}}-dg_g=g_g\,d\ln P$; moving that wedge from the first term
to the price-response term is exactly what shifts the coefficient by
$(1-\kappa)g_g$, since $(\tau-\kappa g_g)-(\tau-g_g)=(1-\kappa)g_g$. Both
coefficients coincide at $g_g=0$, where they equal $\tau^{*}$ and
$dg_g=dg_g^{\mathrm{direct}}$; evaluating there --- the point at which the
proposition is stated --- gives \eqref{eq:marginal},
$dN=(1-\kappa)\,dg_g-\tau^{*}\epsilon_P\,dg_g$.
(iii) The mechanical part is exact: the revaluation transfer is proportional to
nominal bond holdings $a$, so its \emph{exposure} is concentrated at the top of
the wealth distribution in the one-asset economy (the constrained hold none and
the top holds most), which the wealth-Gini comparison computed at the invariant
distribution captures directly. Concentrated exposure and signed incidence must
not be conflated: when the revaluation is a \emph{levy} on bondholders ($L>0$,
the price level rising), concentration makes the burden progressive by wealth
rank; when it is a \emph{windfall} ($L<0$, the price level falling ---
the lump-sum benchmark case), the same concentration makes the transfer
regressive, delivering the gain to the top. This is the sign convention of
part~(iii) of the proposition ($|L|$ progressive by wealth rank when positive,
regressive when negative).
The scope of the claim is precise: a Gini (Lorenz) statement establishes
progressivity in the \emph{wealth} ranking only; progressivity relative to
\emph{income}, \emph{consumption}, or \emph{welfare} does not follow from it
without an additional monotonicity (a positive wealth--income association) or
Lorenz-dominance condition. We therefore do not claim income/welfare
progressivity analytically; it is the \emph{numerical} finding of the
welfare-by-group computation (Section~\ref{subsec:welfaregroups},
Table~\ref{tab:incidence}), where the consumption-equivalent transfers are
monotone across baseline wealth and income groups at the calibration --- a
computed result, not a corollary of the Gini. Result~\ref{res:disinflation}:
$P=B/\Sfun$ at both
steady states, so $P_1<P_0 \iff \Sfun_1>\Sfun_0$;
$\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}\propto(1/P_0-1/P_1)=(\Sfun_0-\Sfun_1)/B$. The stated
sufficient conditions each raise $\Sfun_1$ relative to $\Sfun_0$. \qed

\subsection{Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:sunspots}}
At a zero of $\Phi$, $d\Phi/dP=(\Sfun/P)(\varepsilon_S+1)$ (the identity is
exact only where $B/P=\Sfun$), so the hypothesized crossing at $P_m$ with
$\varepsilon_S(P_m)<-1$ is downward. The boundary conditions give $\Phi<0$
at the low edge and $\Phi>0$ at the high edge. Immediately left of $P_m$,
$\Phi>0$, but $\Phi<0$ at the low edge forces an upward crossing in between;
immediately right of $P_m$, $\Phi<0$, but $\Phi>0$ at the high edge forces
another upward crossing. Hence at least three zeros in total,
alternating upward--downward--upward, with the outer two stable under the
natural tatonnement and the middle one unstable. (The hypothesis is the
existence of the downward crossing itself: $\varepsilon_S<-1$ merely on an
interval need not place a zero of $\Phi$ there.) Welfare ranking with
$\psi>0$ or $\phi_D>0$: at $P_L^{*}$ the
level and risk components of effective income improve for every household
with $\chi(e)>0$, but the lower price level also raises real green spending
$g_g=G_g/P$, real debt $b=B/P$, and real taxes $\tau=r^{ss}b+g_g$, which cut
the other way for constrained households; the sign of the net utilitarian
comparison $W(P_L^{*})-W(P_H^{*})$ is therefore not implied by lower damages
alone and is a numerical comparison of the full equilibrium objects, which
the package performs \emph{wherever multiplicity arises}. In the calibrated
and extended experiments of Section~\ref{subsec:sunspots} no multiple-root
case occurs (the region is not reached), so the ranking is a conditional
property of the multiplicity region rather than a realized benchmark finding.
Self-fulfillment is Definition~\ref{def:equilibrium}: each root satisfies
every equilibrium condition. \qed

\subsection{Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:insulation} and corollaries}
Under the mandate $dD/dP=0$, so
$\varepsilon_S=\eta_\tau\,d\ln\tau/d\ln P$ with
$d\ln\tau/d\ln P=-(r^{ss}B/P)/\tau$. Two regimes. If $r^{ss}\ge0$ and
$\tau>0$, then $\tau=r^{ss}B/P+g_g\ge r^{ss}B/P$ for $g_g\ge0$, so the
derivative lies in $(-1,0]$ and $|\varepsilon_S|\le|\eta_\tau|$. If
accommodation makes $r^{ss}<0$, the derivative is \emph{positive},
$|d\ln\tau/d\ln P|=|r^{ss}|b/\tau$, which is below one exactly when
$\tau>|r^{ss}|b$ --- equivalently $g_g>2|r^{ss}|b$, since
$\tau=g_g-|r^{ss}|b$; this is a genuine restriction, not automatic, and the
package checks it holds on the calibrated grid. On the region where it
holds together with $|\eta_\tau|<1$ (verified numerically across the
calibrated $\theta_g,\psi,\phi_D$, not proved for all parameters),
$|\varepsilon_S|<1$ and Proposition~\ref{prop:determinacy}(ii) gives
uniqueness conditional on existence. Corollary~\ref{cor:anchor} combines
this with Lemma~\ref{lem:anchor}. The rule-ranking contrast with the
nominal-rule uniqueness of \citet{hagedorn2026dtpl} and the missing-anchor
corner (the classical argument: with no nominal object in the equilibrium
conditions, $P$ and $2P$ support identical real allocations) are the two
remarks following the corollary in the text. \qed

\subsection{Proof sketch of Proposition \ref{prop:financingdesign}}
Consider the budget-neutral perturbation $d\tau_{ls} = -(1-D)\,d\vartheta <
0$ at fixed $g_g$ and $D$. The demand response is
$d\Sfun = \Sfun_{\tau}\, d\tau_{ls} + \Sfun_{\vartheta}\, d\vartheta$. By
condition (i), $\Sfun_{\tau} \ge 0$, so the first term is negative. For the
second, write the levy as the proportional scaling of effective endowments
by $(1-\vartheta)$; by the scaling logic of Lemma~\ref{lem:S}, a pure
proportional contraction scales asset demand approximately one-for-one
(exactly, when the lump-sum component is rescaled alongside; the residual
non-homotheticity is second-order for small $\vartheta$), so
$\Sfun_{\vartheta} \approx -\Sfun/(1-\vartheta) < 0$. Both terms lower
$\Sfun$; hence $P = B/\Sfun$ rises. The step from the price level to the
revaluation share carries a sign restriction: since
$\nu_{\mathrm{reval}} = r^{ss}B\,(1/P_0 - 1/P_1)/g_{g,1}$, a higher $P_1$
raises $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$ only when $r^{ss}>0$ (the Table~\ref{tab:regimes}
stance, $r^{ss}=2\%$); under accommodation with $r^{ss}<0$ the same
price-level ranking reverses the $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$ ranking, exactly as
in the scope note to Result~\ref{res:disinflation}. The rebate
design extends the same perturbation past $\tau_{ls} = r^{ss}b$ into
negative lump-sum territory ($\tau_{ls} < r^{ss}b$), and with
$\Sfun_{\tau} \ge 0$ each further unit of rebate financed by the levy
continues to lower $\Sfun$. The ordering
$P^{*}_{\text{lump-sum}} < P^{*}_{\text{mixed}} < P^{*}_{\text{levy}} <
P^{*}_{\text{rebate}}$ and, on the $r^{ss}>0$ region, the associated $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$ ranking are
verified exactly in Table~\ref{tab:regimes} (all four are balanced stationary
tax-incidence regimes --- the label ``lump-sum'' is the financing instrument,
not a deficit). \qed

\subsection{Proof of Proposition \ref{prop:optimalmu}}
The proposition is a \emph{reduction} plus a numerical characterization; we are
explicit about which part is which. By Proposition~\ref{prop:muneutral},
steady-state welfare depends on $\mu$ (at fixed $i^{ss}$) only through the
real service rate $r^{ss}=(1+i^{ss})/(1+\mu)-1$, which is strictly decreasing in
$\mu$. Hence $W(\mu)=\widetilde W\big(r^{ss}(\mu)\big)$ and the $\mu$-optimum is
the $\mu$ that implements the welfare-maximizing real rate: this is the
\citet{aiyagarimcgrattan1998} optimum-quantity-of-debt problem in the present
climate economy. Crucially, $\widetilde W$ is the \emph{steady-state} welfare
function --- as $r^{ss}$ moves, the value function $V$, the invariant
distribution $\Omega$, real debt $b$, taxes $\tau$, the price level $P$, real
green spending $g_g$ and damages $D$ all re-solve --- so the derivative below is
the total steady-state derivative, not a fixed-distribution envelope and
not an unanticipated revaluation of currently-held debt.

Two steady-state forces sign $d\widetilde W/dr^{ss}$ near the top of the
debt-value region. (i) \emph{The interest-bill channel.} The steady-state real
service $r^{ss}b$ is a transfer from taxpayers to bondholders; a marginal
reduction in $r^{ss}$ shrinks it, moving resources from the
\emph{asset-weighted} marginal utility $\int u'(c)\,a\,\dd\Omega/\int a\,\dd\Omega$
(low, since wealth and consumption are positively associated and wealth is
concentrated) toward the \emph{population-weighted} marginal utility
$\int u'(c)\,\dd\Omega$ (high) --- a first-order welfare gain, and the standard
incomplete-markets force, evaluated here as a comparison of stationary equilibria
rather than a transition windfall. (ii) \emph{The climate channel.} A lower
$r^{ss}$ (higher $\mu$) raises the equilibrium price level, so real green
spending $g_g=G_g/P$ falls and damages rise, a welfare cost proportional to the
program size $g_g$ and hence subdominant at $\mu=0^{+}$ for the calibrated
program. What we do not claim in closed form is the existence and interior
location of the optimum: that $\widetilde W$ rises then falls, with maximum at
$r^{ss*}\approx-0.5\%$ ($\mu^{*}\approx4.5\%$), is \emph{verified numerically}
on the grid (Section~\ref{subsec:optmu}), exactly as the optimum-quantity-of-debt
level is numerical in \citet{aiyagarimcgrattan1998}. The proposition should be
read accordingly: analytic reduction to a real-rate problem, numerical location
of its interior maximum. \qed

\subsection{Fiscal inertness of the climate-risk premium}
The ergodic-expected real interest bill in the aggregate-risk economy is
independent of the risk premium. With realized gross real return
$R(s,s')=(1+r^{ss})P_s/P_{s'}$ and state-contingent real debt $b_s=B/P_s$,
\begin{align*}
\sum_s \pi_s \sum_{s'}\Pi^{\mathrm{agg}}_{ss'}\big(R(s,s')-1\big)b_s
&=\sum_s \pi_s \sum_{s'}\Pi^{\mathrm{agg}}_{ss'}
   \Big[(1+r^{ss})\tfrac{B}{P_{s'}}-\tfrac{B}{P_s}\Big]\\
&=(1+r^{ss})B\sum_{s'}\Big(\textstyle\sum_s \pi_s \Pi^{\mathrm{agg}}_{ss'}\Big)\tfrac{1}{P_{s'}}
  - B\sum_s \pi_s \tfrac{1}{P_s}\\
&=(1+r^{ss})B\,\E_\pi\!\big[\tfrac{1}{P_s}\big]-B\,\E_\pi\!\big[\tfrac{1}{P_s}\big]
  = r^{ss}B\,\E_\pi\!\big[\tfrac{1}{P_s}\big],
\end{align*}
where the third line uses the ergodicity of $\pi$
($\sum_s \pi_s \Pi^{\mathrm{agg}}_{ss'}=\pi_{s'}$). The premium
$\mathbb{E}[R]-(1+r^{ss})>0$ (with $R$ the gross return) therefore adds
nothing to the government's expected
budget: it is a risk-timing wedge that redistributes the debt-service cost
across states but leaves its ergodic mean at $r^{ss}$ times the debt. Its
value is a household risk-sharing object, priced in
Section~\ref{subsec:aggrisk}. \qed

% ==========================================================================
\section{The New Keynesian model used in the transition
diagnostics}\label{app:nkmodel}
% ==========================================================================

The economy of Section~\ref{sec:model} is an \emph{endowment} economy:
households receive the effective endowment $y(e;D)$ of
\eqref{eq:incidence}, and the price level is determined by asset-market
clearing of the nominal debt stock, $P^\ast=B/\Sfun(1+r^{ss};\tau,D)$. The
two Phillips-curve exercises of Sections~\ref{subsec:ranktrans} and
\ref{subsec:hanktier1} embed the same household preferences
\eqref{eq:preferences} and climate sector
\eqref{eq:Kg}--\eqref{eq:climatefixedpoint} in a New Keynesian production
economy: output is produced from labor, monopolistic firms face sticky
prices, and the central bank sets the nominal rate by an interest-rate
rule. The \emph{government} block, by contrast, is deliberately not
the stationary model's nominal growth rule and nominal appropriations
\eqref{eq:nominalrule}--\eqref{eq:gbc}: because inflation here is set by the
policy rule, not by asset-market clearing, the fiscal side is written in real
terms --- a real debt-stabilization rule \eqref{eq:nk-fiscal} and a
quasi-permanent \emph{real} green-investment process (Appendix~\ref{app:nkcommon})
--- so that the diagnostic isolates transition shape under a
conventional fiscal stance rather than re-importing the DTPL's nominal
determination. Inflation is then determined by a New Keynesian Phillips curve and
the policy rule rather than by asset-market clearing --- which is exactly
why these exercises are diagnostics of transition shape and cannot,
by construction, represent the demand-theory price-level mechanism. This
appendix states the environment. Both versions share the production,
pricing, policy, fiscal, and climate blocks (Appendix~\ref{app:nkcommon})
and differ only in the household side: a representative consumer
(Appendix~\ref{app:nkrank}) or the incomplete-markets household of
Section~\ref{subsec:households} augmented with a labor choice
(Appendix~\ref{app:nkhank}). Time is quarterly here; stationarized
real quantities and net inflation $\pi_t$ are dated so that $x_{t-1}$
denotes a predetermined stock.

\subsection{Common blocks}\label{app:nkcommon}

\paragraph{Production and marginal cost.} A unit continuum of
monopolistically competitive firms produces differentiated goods from labor
with economy-wide total factor productivity $Z$ (normalized $Z=1$) net of
climate damages $D$. Aggregate output and real marginal cost are
\begin{equation}
Y_t = (1-D_t)\,Z\,N_t, \qquad
mc_t = \frac{w_t}{(1-D_t)\,Z},
\label{eq:nk-production}
\end{equation}
where $N_t$ is aggregate effective labor and $w_t$ the real wage. Damages
enter as a TFP tax, the production-economy counterpart of the endowment
haircut $y(e;D)$ in Section~\ref{sec:model}.

\paragraph{Sticky prices.} Firms face quadratic price-adjustment costs
(Rotemberg) with steady-state gross markup
$\mu_p=\varepsilon_p/(\varepsilon_p-1)$ --- written $\mu_p$ to keep it
distinct from the nominal-growth rate $\mu$ of Section~\ref{sec:model} ---
and a slope parameter governing price stickiness. This yields the New
Keynesian Phillips curve, written in the exact nonlinear (Rotemberg) form
in the representative-agent version and in the equivalent log form used by
the sequence-space solver in the heterogeneous-agent version (see the two
subsections below).

\paragraph{Monetary policy and the Fisher equation.} The central bank sets
the net nominal rate $i_t$ by an interest-rate rule, and the ex-post real
return on the one-period nominal bond obeys the Fisher relation
\begin{equation}
1 + r_t = \frac{1+i_{t-1}}{1+\pi_t},
\label{eq:nk-fisher}
\end{equation}
the dynamic counterpart of the steady-state Fisher equation
\eqref{eq:fisher}: a surprise increase in $\pi_t$ lowers the realized real
return on predetermined nominal positions, which is the linearized
redistribution channel of the paper.

\paragraph{Fiscal policy.} All objects here are real ($b_t=B_t/P_t$), and the
block is written in \emph{levels} around the \emph{new} (post-program) steady
state, with permanent green spending $\bar g_g>0$ and the associated real debt
target $\bar b$ (so $\bar b$ is not the nominal stock $B$ of the stationary
model). Real government debt evolves as
\begin{equation}
b_t = (1+r_t)\,b_{t-1} + g_{g,t} - \tau_t, \qquad
\tau_t = \underbrace{r^{ss}\bar b + \bar g_g}_{\text{new-steady-state primary tax}}
        + \phi_b\,(b_{t-1}-\bar b).
\label{eq:nk-fiscal}
\end{equation}
The constant is the new-steady-state primary tax $r^{ss}\bar b+\bar g_g$, so the
system is stationary at $(b_t,g_{g,t})=(\bar b,\bar g_g)$: at that point
$\tau_t=r^{ss}\bar b+\bar g_g$ and $b_t=(1+r^{ss})\bar b+\bar g_g-\tau_t=\bar b$
The debt-stabilization speed $\phi_b$ indexes
how fast taxes close the gap between the \emph{pre}-program debt $b_0$ and the
new target $\bar b$: a low $\phi_b$ lets debt drift up to $\bar b$ slowly ---
the program is deficit-financed on impact (the benchmark) --- while a high
$\phi_b$ raises taxes quickly toward $r^{ss}\bar b+\bar g_g$ and approximates
contemporaneous tax finance. Real green investment approaches its permanent
level $\bar g_g$ through a quasi-permanent process,
\begin{equation}
g_{g,t} = \bar g_g + \rho_g\,(g_{g,t-1}-\bar g_g) + \varepsilon_{g,t},
\label{eq:nk-program}
\end{equation}
with persistence $\rho_g$ close to one, so a program announced at $t=1$
(an innovation $\varepsilon_{g,1}$ moving $g_{g,1}$ toward $\bar g_g$) has
\emph{positive} terminal spending $\bar g_g$, not zero --- the nearly-permanent
counterpart of the exactly-permanent program of the DTPL transition
(Section~\ref{subsec:tier2}).

\paragraph{Climate.} The climate block is \emph{identical} to
Section~\ref{subsec:climate}: abatement capital, the carbon stock, and
damages evolve as
\begin{align}
K_{g,t} &= (1-\delta_g)\,K_{g,t-1} + g_{g,t}, \label{eq:nk-kg}\\
X_t &= (1-\delta_x)\,X_{t-1}
      + \varepsilon_0\bigl(1-\alpha_A(1-e^{-\theta_g K_{g,t-1}})\bigr)Y_t,
      \label{eq:nk-carbon}\\
D_t &= D_{\max}\bigl(1-e^{-\gamma_x X_{t-1}}\bigr),
      \label{eq:nk-damages}
\end{align}
with the parameters of Section~\ref{sec:quant}.

\subsection{Representative-agent version}\label{app:nkrank}

A representative household with CRRA utility (risk aversion $\sigma$, the
same coefficient as in Section~\ref{subsec:households}) and Frisch elasticity
$\varphi_F$ chooses consumption $c_t$, labor $n_t$, and one-period nominal bonds.
The private optimum, technology, Rotemberg Phillips curve, resource
constraint, and policy rule are
\begin{align}
c_t^{-\sigma} &= \beta\,\frac{1+i_t}{1+\pi_{t+1}}\,c_{t+1}^{-\sigma},
  &&\text{(Euler)} \label{eq:rank-euler}\\
n_t^{1/\varphi_F} &= w_t\,c_t^{-\sigma}, &&\text{(labor supply)} \label{eq:rank-labor}\\
\pi_t(1+\pi_t) &= \beta\Bigl(\tfrac{c_{t+1}}{c_t}\Bigr)^{-\sigma}
  \pi_{t+1}(1+\pi_{t+1})\,\tfrac{Y_{t+1}}{Y_t}
  + \tfrac{\varepsilon_p}{\kappa_r}\Bigl(mc_t-\tfrac{\varepsilon_p-1}{\varepsilon_p}\Bigr),
  &&\text{(NKPC)} \label{eq:rank-nkpc}\\
Y_t &= c_t + g_{g,t} + \tfrac{\kappa_r}{2}\pi_t^2\,Y_t,
  &&\text{(resource)} \label{eq:rank-resource}\\
i_t &= \rho_i\, i_{t-1} + (1-\rho_i)\Bigl[(\tfrac{1}{\beta}-1)
  + \phi_\pi\,\pi_t - \psi_g\bigl(K_g^{ss}-K_{g,t}\bigr)\Bigr],
  &&\text{(policy)} \label{eq:rank-policy}
\end{align}
together with \eqref{eq:nk-production}, \eqref{eq:nk-fiscal}, the program
\eqref{eq:nk-program}, and the climate block
\eqref{eq:nk-kg}--\eqref{eq:nk-damages}. Here $\kappa_r$ is the Rotemberg
adjustment-cost coefficient and $\varepsilon_p$ the elasticity of
substitution across varieties. The policy rule allows interest-rate inertia
($\rho_i$) and a temporary green accommodation tied to the green-capital gap
($\psi_g$), fading as $K_{g,t}\to K_g^{ss}$; the four monetary regimes of
Section~\ref{subsec:ranktrans} are parameterizations of
\eqref{eq:rank-policy}. Perfect-foresight paths are computed by a
nonlinear boundary-value solver from the pre-program steady state to the
green terminal steady state.

\subsection{Heterogeneous-agent version}\label{app:nkhank}

The one-asset heterogeneous-agent economy of
Section~\ref{subsec:hanktier1} replaces the representative consumer with the
incomplete-markets household of Section~\ref{subsec:households}, adding an
intratemporal labor choice. A household in idiosyncratic state $e$ (the
Markov chain of Section~\ref{subsec:households}) with beginning-of-period
real bond holdings $a_{t-1}$ solves
\begin{align}
c_t(a_{t-1},e)^{-\sigma} &= \beta\,(1+r_{t+1})\,
   \E\!\big[\,c_{t+1}(a_t,e')^{-\sigma}\,\big|\,e\,\big]
   \quad\perp\quad a_t \ge -\bar a, &&\text{(Euler, borrowing constraint)}
   \label{eq:hank-euler}\\
c_t + a_t &= (1+r_t)\,a_{t-1} + w_t\, n_t\, e + (\mathrm{Div}_t-\tau_t)\,e,
   &&\text{(budget)} \label{eq:hank-budget}\\
\vphi\, n_t^{1/\varphi_F} &= w_t\, e\, c_t^{-\sigma},
   &&\text{(labor supply)} \label{eq:hank-labor}
\end{align}
where the conditional expectation $\E[\,\cdot\,|\,e]$ is over next-period
idiosyncratic states $e'$ under the Markov chain of
Section~\ref{subsec:households} --- $a_t$ is chosen before $e'$ is realized, so
the Euler right-hand side is expected next-period marginal utility, not a
deterministic one; the aggregate path $\{r_{t+1}\}$ is perfect-foresight, so
only the idiosyncratic state is integrated out. Here $\sigma$ and $\varphi_F$
are the CRRA coefficient and Frisch elasticity as
above, $\vphi$ the labor-disutility weight, $\mathrm{Div}_t$ real dividends
rebated in proportion to efficiency $e$, and $\perp$ denotes the
complementary-slackness form of the borrowing constraint. The household's asset $a$ is the real value of the nominal
government bond, exactly as in \eqref{eq:budget}: surprise inflation
revalues $a_{t-1}$ through $r_t$ in \eqref{eq:nk-fisher}, state by state.
Writing $\mathrm{SUM}(\cdot)$ for the cross-sectional aggregate over the
joint distribution of $(a,e)$, the aggregate block is
\begin{align}
N_t &= \frac{Y_t}{(1-D_t)Z}, &&\text{(labor demand)} \label{eq:hank-labdemand}\\
\mathrm{Div}_t &= Y_t - w_t N_t
   - \frac{\mu_p}{\mu_p-1}\,\frac{\log(1+\pi_t)^2}{2\kappa_p}\,Y_t,
   &&\text{(dividends)} \label{eq:hank-div}\\
\kappa_p\Bigl(\frac{w_t}{(1-D_t)Z}-\frac{1}{\mu_p}\Bigr)
   &+ \frac{Y_{t+1}}{Y_t}\,\frac{\log(1+\pi_{t+1})}{1+r_{t+1}}
   - \log(1+\pi_t) = 0, &&\text{(NKPC)} \label{eq:hank-nkpc}\\
i_t &= r^{ss} + \phi_\pi\,\pi_t - \psi_g\, g_{g,t}, &&\text{(policy)} \label{eq:hank-policy}\\
\mathrm{SUM}(a_t) &= b_t, \qquad \mathrm{SUM}(n_t\, e) = N_t,
   &&\text{(market clearing)} \label{eq:hank-clearing}
\end{align}
together with the Fisher equation \eqref{eq:nk-fisher}, the fiscal block
\eqref{eq:nk-fiscal}, the program \eqref{eq:nk-program}, and the climate
block \eqref{eq:nk-kg}--\eqref{eq:nk-damages}, where $\kappa_p$ is the
(log-form) Phillips-curve slope. The discount factor $\beta$ and the
labor-disutility weight $\vphi$ are calibrated so that the asset market
\eqref{eq:hank-clearing} and the labor market clear at the damaged steady
state with debt at the target of Section~\ref{sec:quant}. The system is
solved by sequence-space Jacobians: the household block delivers the
Jacobians of aggregate consumption, savings, and labor supply with respect
to the paths of $\{r_t,w_t,\tau_t,\mathrm{Div}_t\}$, which are combined with
the aggregate equations \eqref{eq:hank-labdemand}--\eqref{eq:hank-clearing}
to produce the impulse responses of
Section~\ref{subsec:hanktier1}. Because \eqref{eq:hank-nkpc} pins inflation
off the Phillips curve, this exercise --- like the representative-agent one
--- does not determine the price level by asset demand; that is the
object of the nonlinear transition of Section~\ref{subsec:tier2}, whose
household problem is the same \eqref{eq:hank-euler}--\eqref{eq:hank-budget}
solved globally with the price path, not the Phillips curve, as the unknown.

% ==========================================================================
\section{Numerical method}\label{app:numerics}

The household problem is solved by value function iteration with Howard
acceleration on a 500-node exponentially spaced asset grid (choices on the
grid; no reliance on monotonicity), the invariant distribution by exact power
iteration on the sparse joint transition (no simulation), both inherited from
the underlying replication package and validated against its 10-point check
suite. The package also contains an endogenous-grid solver
\citep{carroll2006egm} with continuous policies and a lottery-method
invariant distribution \citep{young2010}. A cross-validation on the six
calibrated steady states (\texttt{verify\_egm\_vs\_vfi}) finds the two
solvers agree on aggregate asset demand to within $0.4\%$, while the
endogenous-grid method reduces mean off-grid Euler-equation errors from
$10^{-2.1}$ to $10^{-5.4}$ (maxima: $10^{-1.2}$ to $10^{-2.5}$) at roughly
a third of the wall-clock. Because the $0.4\%$ gap exceeds the reporting
precision of the price level, the endogenous-grid solver is the package
default going forward and the steady-state tables regenerate under it at
each replication pass; the grid-choice solver remains available
(\texttt{pg.hh\_solver='vfi'}) and the validation table ships with the
package. A three-grid convergence audit ($n_a=125/250/500$,
\texttt{verify\_grid\_convergence}; all parameters including the calibrated
$\beta$ held fixed) makes the same case independently: between the medium
and research grids the endogenous-grid solution moves by less than
$5\times10^{-4}$ on every audited moment --- aggregate asset demand, the
no-program real-debt fixed point, the wealth Gini, the constrained mass ---
and its tax semi-elasticity is stable to three digits across all three
grids, while the grid-choice solution still moves by roughly $10^{-2}$ on
aggregates at $n_a=500$ and its finite-difference semi-elasticity is
dominated by grid-snap noise. The research-grid tables therefore meet the
stated convergence tolerances under the endogenous-grid solver, which is a
second, independent reason it is the package default. The tier-2 transition keeps its own finite-horizon backward
recursion either way, cross-checked by the sequence-space solve of
Section~\ref{subsec:tier2}; its boundary steady states, terminal value
function, and pre-announcement distribution are pinned to the same
grid-choice method as its interior steps, so the transition is a
self-contained grid-choice object (mixing solvers across the boundary
leaves a spurious terminal wedge and degrades the Newton linearization),
and its boundary price levels may differ from the steady-state tables by
the measured solver gap of up to $0.4\%$. Aggregate asset demand on the $(\tau,D)$ plane is precomputed on a
$7\times4$ node grid at the policy real rate and interpolated bilinearly;
every reported equilibrium is \emph{re-solved exactly} at the root (the
interpolation residual is reported). Roots of $\Phi$ are found by a dense
scan plus bracketed one-dimensional root-finding, returning all roots.
Under the incidence
gradient the high-$(\tau,D)$ corners of the interpolation grid can be
infeasible; the $\tau$-range is clamped at the feasibility bound evaluated at
the median damage node, remaining infeasible corner nodes are filled by
nearest-in-$\tau$ values \emph{for scanning purposes only}, and every reported
equilibrium is re-solved exactly at the root (the interpolation residual is
reported), so filled cells cannot silently produce spurious equilibria. The
extended experiments are evaluated at the accommodative stance
$\mu_{\mathrm{ext}}=0.03$: at $\mu=0.02$ the incidence-amplified precautionary
demand pushes $P^{*}$ so low that the implied tax burden crosses the lump-sum
feasibility bound and no stationary equilibrium exists --- a fiscal-space
collapse reported as a result in its own right. The elasticity
$\varepsilon_S(P)$ is computed by centered log-differences along the demand
curve. The optimal-policy exercise rebuilds the interpolant at each $\mu$
(the real rate changes). Runtime: about one minute per full pass at
$n_a=500$ on commodity hardware (68 s in the authors' benchmark run); a FAST
mode at $n_a=100$ reproduces all qualitative results.

% ==========================================================================
\section{Supplementary results}\label{app:supp}
% ==========================================================================
This appendix collects five supporting analyses whose headline conclusions
are stated --- in one paragraph each --- in the body; here each is developed
in full. Section~\ref{subsec:maturity} disciplines the \emph{size} of the
revaluation channel against the inflating-away literature: maturity,
indexation, holder composition, and implementation efficiency, yielding the
liquid-share bound that the abstract and Section~\ref{subsec:equilibrium}
attach to the one-asset magnitudes. Section~\ref{subsec:twoasset} verifies
the one-asset conclusions in a linearized two-asset economy in which the
liquid-bond margin is observable, the qualification cited in
Sections~\ref{subsec:equilibrium} and~\ref{subsec:tier2}.
Section~\ref{subsec:workedexample} states the worked example of
Section~\ref{subsec:calibration} in full --- parameterization, the benchmark
table, and the $\nu(\theta_g)$ sweep. Section~\ref{subsec:optmu} reports the
optimal-accommodation exercise behind the optimal-real-rate summary of
Section~\ref{subsec:extended}, including its scoping.
Section~\ref{subsec:aggriskwelfare} decomposes the aggregate-risk welfare
gain of Section~\ref{subsec:aggrisk} into its level and insurance
components. Each entry is a robustness check, a bound, or the completed
version of a body summary; none changes a headline result, which is why
they appear here rather than in the text.

\subsection{Bounding the revaluation channel: maturity, indexation, holders}
\label{subsec:maturity}

The inflating-away literature disciplines how much fiscal room nominal-debt
revaluation can really provide: \citet{hilscher2022} show that maturity,
holder composition and the persistence of inflation sharply limit it;
\citet{hallsargent2011} supply the maturity-adjusted accounting;
\citet{doepkeschneider2006} and \citet{auclert2019} the redistribution
mapping. Our framework adds a clarifying twist. In steady-state comparisons
at unchanged $(i,\mu)$, the program moves the price level once --- a
\emph{level jump} --- and a level jump revalues all nominal promises
proportionally \emph{regardless of maturity}: duration neither shields nor
amplifies it. The binding limits are instead (i) \emph{indexation leakage}
--- a share $\alpha_I$ of indexed debt does not revalue, so the fiscal
component scales by $(1-\alpha_I)$; and (ii) \emph{holder composition} ---
a share $\alpha_F$ of foreign-held nominal debt leaves the fiscal gain
unchanged but reallocates the domestic incidence by
$(1-\alpha_I)(1-\alpha_F)$. Because our calibrated revaluation channel is
negative (green disinflation), these leakages work in the
government's favor: indexed debt shrinks the fiscal loss, and foreign
holders absorb part of the bondholder windfall that would otherwise accrue
to wealthy domestic households. Duration proper enters only where the real
rate moves --- the accommodation experiments --- through the repricing of
legacy long bonds (geometric-coupon price $q = 1/(1+r-\delta_m)$). The
replication package reports the maturity-adjusted
share $\nu^{M} = (1-\alpha_I)\,\nu_{\mathrm{reval}} + \nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$
over $\alpha_I \in \{0, 0.10, 0.25\}$, the domestic-incidence table over
$\alpha_F \in \{0, 0.30, 0.50\}$, and the duration term for the
$\mu = 0.02 \to 0.03$ accommodation move (the extended-run stance, real
rate $1.96\% \to 0.97\%$) at coupon-decay rates $\delta_m \in \{0, 0.8,
0.9\}$ --- durations of roughly one, five and eight years; the
implementation-efficiency sweep $q_g \in \{0.6, 0.8, 1.0\}$
(planning delays and procurement frictions in the spirit of
\citealp{leeperwalkeryang2010}, disciplined by \citealp{bomligthart2014}
and the IMF's climate public-investment framework) scales the damage
dividend correspondingly.

The run (calibrated medium column) delivers three quantitative statements.
First, because the revaluation channel is negative, \emph{indexation raises
the self-financing share}: $\nu^{M} = 0.583$, $0.588$ and $0.596$ at indexed
shares $\alpha_I = 0$, $0.10$ and $0.25$ --- indexed debt shields the fisc
from the disinflation loss, the mirror image of the usual
inflating-away logic. Second, foreign holders absorb the windfall: by the
incidence identity $(1-\alpha_I)(1-\alpha_F)\,\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$, the
domestic revaluation incidence shrinks from $-0.048$ to $-0.024$ as the
foreign-held share rises from zero to one half (at $\alpha_I = 0.10$).
Third, duration amplifies who gains from \emph{accommodation}: under the
$\mu = 0.02 \to 0.03$ move the legacy bond repricing hands holders
$+6.2\%$ at one-year duration, $+10.2\%$ at five years and $+14.7\%$ at
eight years --- accommodation redistributes toward long-bond
holders, in line with the \citet{hallsargent2011} accounting.
Implementation frictions scale the damage dividend nearly linearly:
$\nu = 0.583$, $0.466$ and $0.344$ at $q_g = 1.0$, $0.8$ and $0.6$
(the revaluation component barely moves), so a forty
percent efficiency loss forfeits roughly forty percent of self-financing
--- quantifying why climate public-investment management
(Climate-PIMA-style) is a fiscal issue, not only an engineering one.

\subsection{The linearized two-asset extension}\label{subsec:twoasset}

The replication package implements a
two-asset version of the transition exercise --- liquid nominal bonds versus illiquid
equity with convex portfolio-adjustment costs, sticky wages and prices,
Tobin's-$Q$ investment, and endogenous government debt --- so that the
demand for the liquid nominal asset, the $B/P$ margin at the heart of the
model, is observable separately from total wealth.\footnote{Two
qualifications. First, making the liquidity premium an \emph{endogenous
convenience yield} that clears the liquid-bond market is boundary-singular
in a truncated sequence-space system under both natural timings of the
premium; the extension therefore closes with total-wealth clearing and a
constant premium, and the endogenous convenience yield is left to future
work. Second, magnitudes are reported only after an explicit accuracy
protocol clears (an oscillation diagnostic and a finer-grid refinement
re-solve agreeing within its $10\%$ gate, at $6.7\%$); the grids remain
coarse ($n_e=3$, $n_b=15$, $n_a=30$), so magnitudes are indicative rather
than calibrated.}

The results (Table~\ref{tab:twoasset}, Figure~\ref{fig:pfig17}) confirm the
one-asset findings under the richer portfolio and add the two objects the
extension was built to see. Three findings carry over. \emph{The real green
path is again rule-independent}: green capital after forty quarters is
$+0.165$ and avoided damages $-0.0039$ in every regime, identical to
three decimals --- the monetary rule moves nominal quantities, not the
accumulation of abatement capital. \emph{Deficit financing buys the debt
path, not the inflation path}: the balanced-budget comparator
(\textsc{taylorbal}) accumulates real debt of only $+0.008$ after forty
quarters against $+0.074$--$+0.077$ under the slow debt-stabilizing rule ---
a tenfold gap --- while impact inflation is essentially unchanged
($+0.03\%$ against $+0.04\%$ annualized). And \emph{the richer supply side
dampens impact inflation}: under the Taylor rule it is $+0.04\%$ annualized
(against $+0.44\%$ in the one-asset exercise), reinforcing the message that
the inflationary margin is the \emph{financing}, not the program. The two
new objects are the point of the extension. The \emph{liquid-bond return}
$r^b$ falls on impact and mirrors inflation almost exactly (e.g.\ $-0.0017$
against $\pi=+0.0017$ under \textsc{weak}): the Fisher revaluation lands
specifically on households' \emph{liquid} nominal savings --- the $B/P$
margin the one-asset exercise cannot separate --- rather than on total
wealth. And the \emph{equity price} drops $5$--$7\%$ on impact as the
illiquid asset reprices against the program's future debt and tax path,
most (a $7.2\%$ drop) under the balanced-budget rule, where taxes rise
fastest. The extension thus makes the liquid-safe-asset margin at the heart
of the paper directly observable under a substantially
richer household portfolio and production block; its economics are otherwise
covered by the one-asset responses (Fisher redistribution) and by the
nonlinear transition of Section~\ref{subsec:tier2} (the $B/P$ margin proper).

\centering

output) under four monetary/financing regimes. Impact inflation and the
liquid return are per quarter (inflation also annualized); debt and green
capital are at forty quarters; the equity-price response is on impact.
Green capital is rule-independent; the balanced-budget regime
(\textsc{taylorbal}) isolates the deficit-financing component. Both accuracy
gates pass (see text).}
\label{tab:twoasset}

\toprule
regime & $\pi$ impact (ann.) & $Y$ impact & $r^b$ impact & debt (40q) & $K_g$ (40q) & equity (impact) \\
\midrule
\textsc{weak}       & $+0.67\%$ & $+0.0048$ & $-0.0017$ & $+0.074$ & $+0.165$ & $-6.7\%$ \\
\textsc{taylor}     & $+0.04\%$ & $+0.0052$ & $-0.0001$ & $+0.077$ & $+0.165$ & $-5.2\%$ \\
\textsc{greenaccom} & $+0.27\%$ & $+0.0054$ & $-0.0007$ & $+0.076$ & $+0.165$ & $-5.4\%$ \\

\subsection{The worked example in full}\label{subsec:workedexample}

The parameters here are chosen for \emph{transparency}, not discipline: a
large damage scale and a sizable program make every channel move visibly, so
the reader can trace the mechanism before it is confronted with credible
magnitudes. Nothing in the paper's contribution rests on these numbers; the
disciplined calibration is Section~\ref{subsec:calibrated}, and this
subsection magnifies the propositions so each moving part is visible.

The household block inherits the replication benchmark of the underlying DTPL
package: annual frequency, $\beta=0.96$, $\sigma=2$, $\bar a=0$, a 7-state
Rouwenhorst chain with $\rho=0.90$, $\sigma_{\epsilon,0}=0.20$ (unit mean),
$B=1$, $i^{ss}=0.04$, baseline $\mu=0.02$ (so $r^{ss}=0.0196$). The climate
block is \emph{deliberately illustrative and deliberately large}: $D_0=0.10$
(no-abatement damages of 10\% of
endowments, at the upper end of what \citealp{bilalkanzig2024} argue is
conservative), $\theta_g=1.2$, $\delta_g=0.10$, risk channel $\phi_D=0.5$,
nominal green budget $G_g=0.012$ (real spending of roughly 5--6\% of mean
endowment at the equilibrium price level --- several times a realistic
public green-investment share, chosen so the price response is large enough
to read off a table). Version-2 climate parameters:
$D_{\max}=0.25$, $\varepsilon_0=1$, $\delta_x=0.05$, $\gamma_x=0.028$,
$\alpha_A=0.9$; incidence $\psi\in\{0,1,2\}$. Numerical method in
Appendix~\ref{app:numerics}. Because these parameters are an expository
device, no headline result rests on them: the self-financing share is
reported across low-, medium-, and high-damage columns
(Section~\ref{subsec:calibrated}), Section~\ref{subsec:robustness} states
each parameter's source and reports the headline objects over the full
swept ranges, and the qualitative findings --- the disinflationary
revaluation, its sign reversal under financing design, and the compression
of aggregate climate risk --- hold across the range.



Table~\ref{tab:benchmark} reports the worked example (full-resolution,
$n_a=500$); read it as an existence demonstration of the channels, not as a
prediction.\footnote{The level of $P^{*}$ is not comparable across
subsections: it scales with the nominal debt normalization $B$ and the
calibration, so the benchmark ($B=1$), the calibrated pass
(Section~\ref{subsec:calibrated}, $B$ set to the $1.1$ debt-to-income
target), and the aggregate-risk section use different normalizations. Only
\emph{changes} in $P$ --- the disinflation $\ln P_1-\ln P_0$ and the state
dispersion $P_S/P_C$ --- are economically meaningful and comparable, and all
reported results are stated as such.}

\begin{table}[!htb]
\centering
\caption{Benchmark quantitative results ($n_a=500$ run).}
\label{tab:benchmark}
\begin{tabular}{lcc}
\toprule
 & no program & green program \\
\midrule
price level $P^{*}$            & 0.2226 & 0.1897 \\
damages $D$                    & 0.1000 & 0.0468 \\
real green spending $g_g$      & 0      & 0.0633 \\
real taxes $\tau$              & 0.0881 & 0.1666 \\
utilitarian welfare $W$        & $-7.807$ & $-8.954$ \\
\midrule
self-financing $\nu$           & \multicolumn{2}{c}{$0.600$} \\
\quad revaluation $\nu_{\mathrm{reval}}$ & \multicolumn{2}{c}{$-0.241$} \\
\quad damage dividend $\nu_{\mathrm{dam}}$ & \multicolumn{2}{c}{$0.841$} \\
one-time revaluation $L$       & \multicolumn{2}{c}{$-0.778$ (bondholder windfall)} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

Three observations. First, the program is strongly but not fully
self-financing: $\nu=0.60$. Second --- the sign surprise of
Result~\ref{res:disinflation} --- the entire financing contribution comes from
the damage dividend; the revaluation channel is negative because the
program is disinflationary ($\ln P_1/P_0 = -16\%$): avoided damages raise
effective incomes, and the higher taxes that service the program strengthen
precautionary saving (the measured benchmark has
$\partial\Sfun/\partial\tau>0$; see the interpolant nodes in the run log,
where $\Sfun$ rises from $3.49$ to $5.80$ as $\tau$ moves from $-0.05$ to
$0.22$ at $D=0$). Both forces raise $\Sfun$, and $P=B/\Sfun$ falls.
Bondholders receive a one-time windfall $|L|=0.78$ --- 78\% of annual mean
income --- concentrated at the top of the wealth distribution. Green deficits
in this economy transfer resources \emph{to} rentiers, not from them: the
mirror image of the inflation-erosion intuition, with the opposite
distributional politics. Third, the naive welfare comparison
($W_1<W_0$) is not the policy-relevant object --- it compares steady states
with different tax burdens, ignoring transition gains from lower damages
accruing along the path; the optimal-policy exercise of Appendix~\ref{subsec:optmu} chooses among
program designs directly.

Figure~\ref{fig:pfig2}(b) traces $\nu(\theta_g)$: the damage dividend is the
engine, and full self-financing $\nu\ge1$ arrives at
$\theta_g\approx2.4$. At $\theta_g=0$ (pure resource cost, no abatement
payoff) $\nu=-0.275<0$: an unproductive nominal program is worse than
its sticker price, again because of the disinflationary asset-demand
response.

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Optimal accommodation}\label{subsec:optmu}

Figure~\ref{fig:pfig4} traces $W(\mu)$. Welfare is hump-shaped with an
interior optimum $\mu^{*}=0.045$ ($W=-7.681$), against a baseline of
$\mu=0.02$ ($W=-8.981$): the optimal policy accommodates the green program
with roughly $4.5\%$ steady-state inflation. The mechanism matches
Proposition~\ref{prop:optimalmu}: moving from $\mu=0.02$ to $0.045$ cuts the
real rate from $2.0\%$ to $-0.5\%$, the real interest burden collapses
($\tau$ falls from $0.167$ to $0.011$ despite unchanged real green spending
financed within the budget), and the price level roughly triples so real
green spending per unit of nominal budget falls --- at $\mu>0.045$ the
climate erosion and the lower buffer return dominate and welfare declines.
At $\mu=0.015$ no equilibrium exists on the scan range: with $r^{ss}$ that
close to the $1/\beta-1$ asymptote, asset demand exceeds $B/P$ at every
feasible price level --- the existence failure the theory predicts, reported
by the solver rather than smoothed over. Three framing caveats keep
the result strictly scoped. \emph{First and most important}, this is not an
inflation result at all: by Proposition~\ref{prop:muneutral} the steady
state depends on $\mu$ only through $r^{ss}=(1+i^{ss})/(1+\mu)-1$, so varying
$\mu$ at fixed $i^{ss}$ is varying the real rate, and holding
$r^{ss}$ fixed while moving $(i^{ss},\mu)$ leaves $W$ exactly invariant. The
reported $\mu^{*}=0.045$ is the optimal real rate $r^{ss*}\approx-0.5\%$; the
welfare gain --- the collapse of the real interest bill on debt of several
times income, a transfer away from wealth-concentrated bondholders --- is
the \citet{aiyagarimcgrattan1998} optimal-quantity-of-debt force in a climate
economy. A design that separated the two margins cleanly --- vary
$(i^{ss},\mu)$ holding $r^{ss}$ fixed (welfare flat, by
Proposition~\ref{prop:muneutral}) against varying $r^{ss}$ at fixed trend
inflation --- would confirm there is no inflation margin here; we state the
optimum as an optimal-$r^{ss}$ result accordingly. \emph{Second}, it is a
\emph{steady-state financing} statement in the spirit of the risk-sharing
motive for accommodation in \citet{acharya2023optimal} --- not advice about
stabilization policy or the credibility cost of moving an announced target,
which the flexible-price model has no vocabulary for. \emph{Third}, the
interior optimum exists precisely because the climate loop penalizes
inflation (real spending erosion), so economies with indexed green budgets
would rationally choose higher $\mu^{*}$.

\pfigguard{PFig4_optimal_policy.pdf}{%
\begin{figure}[!htb]\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth]{PFig4_optimal_policy.pdf}
\caption{Utilitarian welfare $W(\mu)$; the star marks $\mu^{*}=0.045$.}
\label{fig:pfig4}
\end{figure}}

% --------------------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Aggregate-risk welfare: the insurance decomposition}
\label{subsec:aggriskwelfare}

The compressed climate-risk premium of Section~\ref{subsec:aggrisk} is a
\emph{risk-sharing} object, priced in welfare. We isolate it with an
equal-mean-reduction counterfactual: the actual program lowers damages more in
the severe state (compressing the price dispersion), while the counterfactual
lowers both states by the same amount, so the ergodic-mean damage falls
identically but the dispersion does not compress. The consumption-equivalent gap
between the two is the pure value of preferentially insuring the severe
state --- $+0.13\%$ of permanent consumption on average, which offsets about a
tenth of the program's steady-state welfare cost ($-1.35\%$ at the calibrated
medium scale; the transition-inclusive cost is larger still,
Section~\ref{subsec:tier2}). The insurance value falls exactly where the
mechanism says it should: it is about six times larger for a household
\emph{in} the severe state ($+0.46\%$) than in the calm state ($+0.08\%$) ---
the compression is valued when the disaster bites. Across the wealth
distribution it is essentially flat ($+0.14\%$ for the bottom quintile and
the constrained, $+0.14\%$ for the top), because at the benchmark's
\emph{uniform} damage incidence ($\psi=0$) the compression is a proportional
income gain valued alike up and down the distribution; under the regressive
incidence of Propositions~\ref{prop:sunspots} and
\ref{prop:financingdesign} ($\psi>0$), where damages fall on the poor, the
same exercise tilts the insurance value toward the constrained households who
hold the nominally-safe, really-risky bond as their only buffer.

This appendix collects the supporting figures for four results whose headline
magnitudes are reported in the text: the bounds on the revaluation channel
(Section~\ref{subsec:maturity}), the calibration-robustness frontier
(Section~\ref{subsec:robustness}), the extended welfare incidence
(Section~\ref{subsec:regimes}), the aggregate-risk welfare decomposition
(Section~\ref{subsec:aggrisk}), and the two-asset heterogeneous-agent
impulse responses (Section~\ref{sec:transitions}).

\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{PFig20_robustness.pdf}
\caption{Calibration robustness (steady state, calibrated scale). (a) The
self-financing share $\nu$ over the $(D_0,\theta_g)$ plane; the white line
is the $\nu=1$ frontier (full self-financing), and the marked column
values $D_0\in\{0.02,0.06,0.20\}$ are the sourced damage calibrations of
Table~\ref{tab:sources}. The red markers are the benchmark
$\theta_g=1.2$ at the sourced columns. (b) One-at-a-time sensitivity at the
medium column under the held debt-target protocol; the dashed line is full
financing. ($\sigma=1$ has no bar: the debt target is infeasible at that
risk aversion, as the text explains.)}
\label{fig:pfig20}
\end{figure}}
